text.NRSV: He said to them, “If you are willing that I should bury my dead out of my sight, hear me, and entreat for me Ephron son of Zohar,
text.Rotherham: and spake with them, saying,—If it is with the consent of your minds that I should bury my dead from before me, hear me, and intercede for me, with Ephron son of Zohar:

text.NRSV: so that he may give me the cave of Machpelah, which he owns; it is at the end of his field. For the full price let him give it to me in your presence as a possession for a burying place.”
text.Rotherham: That he would give me, the cave of Machpelah, which pertaineth unto him, which is within the bounds of his field,—For full silver, let him give it me in your midst, For a possession of a buryingplace.

text.NRSV: Now Ephron was sitting among the Hittites; and Ephron the Hittite answered Abraham in the hearing of the Hittites, of all who went in at the gate of his city,
text.Rotherham: Now, Ephron, was sitting in the midst of the sons of Heth,—so Ephron the Hittite responded to Abraham, in the ears of the sons of Heth, even all that were entering the gate of his city, saying:

text.NRSV: “No, my lord, hear me; I give you the field, and I give you the cave that is in it; in the presence of my people I give it to you; bury your dead.”
text.Rotherham: Nay, my lord, hear me, The field, have I given to thee And, the cave that is therein, to thee, have I given it,—In the eyes of the sons of my people, have I given it thee Bury thy dead.

text.NRSV: Then Abraham bowed down before the people of the land.
text.Rotherham: So Abraham boweth himself down, before the people of the land:

text.NRSV: He said to Ephron in the hearing of the people of the land, “If you only will listen to me! I will give the price of the field; accept it from me, so that I may bury my dead there.”
text.Rotherham: and spake unto Ephron in the ears of the people of the land saying, Only if, thou, wouldst, hear me,—I have given the silver of the field Take it of me, That I may bury my dead here.

text.NRSV: Ephron answered Abraham,
text.Rotherham: And Ephron responded to Abraham, saying to him,

text.NRSV: “My lord, listen to me; a piece of land worth four hundred shekels of silver—what is that between you and me? Bury your dead.”
text.Rotherham: My lord, hear me,—Land worth four hundred shekels of silver—betwixt me and thee, what is that? And thy dead., bury thou.

text.NRSV: Abraham agreed with Ephron; and Abraham weighed out for Ephron the silver that he had named in the hearing of the Hittites, four hundred shekels of silver, according to the weights current among the merchants.
text.Rotherham: And Abraham hearkened unto Ephron, and Abraham weighed out to Ephron the silver of which he had spoken in the ears of the sons of Heth,—four hundred shekels of silver, current with the merchant,

text.NRSV: So the field of Ephron in Machpelah, which was to the east of Mamre, the field with the cave that was in it and all the trees that were in the field, throughout its whole area, passed
text.Rotherham: So was confirmed the field of Ephron, which is in Machpelah, which is before Mamre,—the field and the cave which is therein, and all the timber which was in the field, which was in all the boundary thereof round about,

text.NRSV: to Abraham as a possession in the presence of the Hittites, in the presence of all who went in at the gate of his city.
text.Rotherham: to Abraham as a purchase in the eyes of the sons of Heth,—with all who were entering the gate of his city.

text.NRSV: After this, Abraham buried Sarah his wife in the cave of the field of Machpelah facing Mamre (that is, Hebron) in the land of Canaan.
text.Rotherham: And after this, did Abraham bury Sarah his wife, within the cave of the field of Machpelah, over against Mamre, the same, is Hebron,—in the land of Canaan.

text.NRSV: The field and the cave that is in it passed from the Hittites into Abraham’s possession as a burying place.
text.Rotherham: So was confirmed the field with the cave which was therein to Abraham, for a possession of a buryingplace,—from the sons of Heth.

text.NRSV: Now Abraham was old, well advanced in years; and the Lord had blessed Abraham in all things.
text.Rotherham: Now, Abraham, was old, far gone in days,—and, Yahweh, had blessed Abraham, in all things.

text.NRSV: Abraham said to his servant, the oldest of his house, who had charge of all that he had, “Put your hand under my thigh
text.Rotherham: So Abraham said unto his servant, elder of his house, ruler of all that he had,—Place, I pray thee thy hand under my thigh;

text.NRSV: and I will make you swear by the Lord, the God of heaven and earth, that you will not get a wife for my son from the daughters of the Canaanites, among whom I live,
text.Rotherham: that I may put thee on oath, by Yahweh God of the heavens and God of the earth—That thou wilt not take a wife for my son, from among the daughters of the Canaanites in the midst of whom, I, am dwelling:

text.NRSV: but will go to my country and to my kindred and get a wife for my son Isaac.”
text.Rotherham: but unto my own land and unto my own kindred, wilt go,—So shalt thou take a wife for my son—for Isaac.

text.NRSV: The servant said to him, “Perhaps the woman may not be willing to follow me to this land; must I then take your son back to the land from which you came?”
text.Rotherham: And the servant said unto him, Peradventure the woman may not be willing to follow me into this land,—May I take back thy son, into the land from whence thou camest?

text.NRSV: Abraham said to him, “See to it that you do not take my son back there.
text.Rotherham: And Abraham said unto him,—Beware that thou do not take back my son thither!

text.NRSV: The Lord, the God of heaven, who took me from my father’s house and from the land of my birth, and who spoke to me and swore to me, ‘To your offspring I will give this land,’ he will send his angel before you, and you shall take a wife for my son from there.
text.Rotherham: Yahweh, God of the heavens, and God of the earth, who took me out of the house of my father, and out of the land of my kindred, and, who spake to me, and who sware to me, saying, To thy seed, will I give this land, he, will send his messenger before thee, so shalt thou take a wife for my son from thence.

text.NRSV: But if the woman is not willing to follow you, then you will be free from this oath of mine; only you must not take my son back there.”
text.Rotherham: But if unwilling be the woman to follow thee, then shalt thou be clear from this mine oath,—Only my son, shalt thou not take back thither

text.NRSV: So the servant put his hand under the thigh of Abraham his master and swore to him concerning this matter.
text.Rotherham: So the servant placed his hand, under the thigh of Abraham, his lord,—and sware to him, over this thing.

text.NRSV: Then the servant took ten of his master’s camels and departed, taking all kinds of choice gifts from his master; and he set out and went to Aram-naharaim, to the city of Nahor.
text.Rotherham: Then took the servant ten camels, from among the camels of his lord, and went his way,—all the goods of his master being in his hand,—so be mounted, and went his way unto Mesopotamia, unto the city of Nahor.

text.NRSV: He made the camels kneel down outside the city by the well of water; it was toward evening, the time when women go out to draw water.
text.Rotherham: And he made the camels kneel down outside the city, against the well of water,—at the time of evening, at the time of the coming forth of the women that drew water,

text.NRSV: And he said, “O Lord, God of my master Abraham, please grant me success today and show steadfast love to my master Abraham.
text.Rotherham: Then he said, O Yahweh, God of my lord Abraham! I pray thee cause it to fall out before me today, that thou deal in lovingkindness with my lord Abraham.

text.NRSV: I am standing here by the spring of water, and the daughters of the townspeople are coming out to draw water.
text.Rotherham: Here am, I, stationed by the fountain of water,—and, the daughters of the men of the city, are coming forth to draw water:

text.NRSV: Let the girl to whom I shall say, “Please offer your jar that I may drink”, and who shall say, “Drink, and I will water your camels”—let her be the one whom you have appointed for your servant Isaac. By this I shall know that you have shown steadfast love to my master.’
text.Rotherham: so it shall come to pass that the young woman unto whom I shall say. Let down pray. thy pitcher that I may drink, and she shall say, Drink, and to thy camels also, will I give to drink, that, her! hast thou appointed for thy servant for Isaac, And, hereby, shall I get to know, that thou hast dealt in lovingkindness with my lord!

text.NRSV: Before he had finished speaking, there was Rebekah, who was born to Bethuel son of Milcah, the wife of Nahor, Abraham’s brother, coming out with her water-jar on her shoulder.
text.Rotherham: And it came to pass, ere yet, he, had done speaking, that lo! Rebekah, was coming forth who had been born to Bethuel son of Milcah, wife of Nahor, brother of Abraham,—with her pitcher upon her shoulder,

text.NRSV: The girl was very fair to look upon, a virgin whom no man had known. She went down to the spring, filled her jar, and came up.
text.Rotherham: Now the young woman! was of very pleasing appearance, a virgin whom, no man, had known,—and she went down unto the fountain, and filled her pitcher and came up.

text.NRSV: Then the servant ran to meet her and said, ‘Please let me sip a little water from your jar.’
text.Rotherham: And the servant ran to meet her,—and said, I pray thee let me drink a little water out of thy pitcher.

text.NRSV: ‘Drink, my lord,’ she said, and quickly lowered her jar upon her hand and gave him a drink.
text.Rotherham: And she said, Drink my lord,—And she hastened and lowered her pitcher on her hand and let him drink.

text.NRSV: When she had finished giving him a drink, she said, ‘I will draw for your camels also, until they have finished drinking.’
text.Rotherham: And when she had done giving him drink,—she said For thy camels also, will I draw, until they have done drinking.

text.NRSV: So she quickly emptied her jar into the trough and ran again to the well to draw, and she drew for all his camels.
text.Rotherham: So she hastened and emptied her pitcher into the drinking-trough, and ran again unto the well, to draw,—and she drew for all his camels.

text.NRSV: The man gazed at her in silence to learn whether or not the Lord had made his journey successful.
text.Rotherham: Now the man, was eagerly watching her,—keeping silence to see—whether Yahweh had prospered his way., or not.

text.NRSV: When the camels had finished drinking, the man took a gold nose-ring weighing a half-shekel, and two bracelets for her arms weighing ten gold shekels,
text.Rotherham: And it came to pass when the camels had done drinking, that the man took a ring of gold, half a shekel its weight,—and two bracelets for her hands, ten of gold their weight;

text.NRSV: and said, ‘Tell me whose daughter you are. Is there room in your father’s house for us to spend the night?’
text.Rotherham: and said Whose daughter art, thou? I pray thee, tell me. Is there in the house of thy father a place for us to tarry the night?

text.NRSV: She said, ‘I am the daughter of Bethuel son of Milcah, whom she bore to Nahor.’
text.Rotherham: And she said unto him, Daughter of Bethuel, am, I,—[Bethuel] son of Milcah whom she bare to Nahor.

text.NRSV: She added, ‘We have plenty of straw and fodder and a place to spend the night.’
text.Rotherham: And she said unto him, Both straw and fodder in plenty, have we,—also a place to tarry the night.

text.NRSV: The man bowed his head and worshipped the Lord
text.Rotherham: Then the man bowed his head, and bent himself down unto Yahweh;

text.NRSV: and said, ‘Blessed be the Lord, the God of my master Abraham, who has not forsaken his steadfast love and his faithfulness towards my master. As for me, the Lord has led me on the way to the house of my master’s kin.’
text.Rotherham: and said, Blessed be Yahweh. God of my lord Abraham, who hath not withdrawn his lovingkindness and his faithfulness, from my lord,—I—being in the way, Yahweh led me unto the house of the brethren of my lord!

text.NRSV: Then the girl ran and told her mother’s household about these things.
text.Rotherham: And the young woman ran, and told the household of her mother,—according to these words.

text.NRSV: Rebekah had a brother whose name was Laban; and Laban ran out to the man, to the spring.
text.Rotherham: Now, Rebekah, had a brother, whose name, was Laban,—so Laban ran unto the man outside, unto the fountain.

text.NRSV: As soon as he had seen the nose-ring, and the bracelets on her sister’s arms, and when he heard the words of his sister Rebekah, ‘Thus the man spoke to me’, he went to the man; and there he was, standing by the camels at the spring.
text.Rotherham: And it came to pass, when he saw the ring, and the bracelets on the hands of his sister, and when he heard the words of Rebekah his sister saying, Thus, did the man speak unto me, that he came unto the man, and lo! he was standing by the camels near the fountain;

text.NRSV: He said, ‘Come in, O blessed of the Lord. Why do you stand outside when I have prepared the house and a place for the camels?’
text.Rotherham: and he said, Come in. thou blessed of Yahweh,—Wherefore shouldst thou stand outside, when I have made ready the house and a place for the camels?

text.NRSV: So the man came into the house; and Laban unloaded the camels, and gave him straw and fodder for the camels, and water to wash his feet and the feet of the men who were with him.
text.Rotherham: So the man came in towards the house, and ungirded the camels,—and there was given him straw and fodder for the camels, and water for bathing his own feet and the feet of the men who were with him;

text.NRSV: Then food was set before him to eat; but he said, ‘I will not eat until I have told my errand.’ He said, ‘Speak on.’
text.Rotherham: and [food] was set before him to eat, But he said, I cannot eat, until I have spoken my words. And he said—Speak!

text.NRSV: So he said, ‘I am Abraham’s servant.
text.Rotherham: Then said he,—Servant of Abraham, am I.

text.NRSV: The Lord has greatly blessed my master, and he has become wealthy; he has given him flocks and herds, silver and gold, male and female slaves, camels and donkeys.
text.Rotherham: Now, Yahweh, having blessed my lord exceedingly, and made him great,—and given him flocks and herds and silver and gold, and men-servants and maid-servants, and camels, and asses,

text.NRSV: And Sarah my master’s wife bore a son to my master when she was old; and he has given him all that he has.
text.Rotherham: then did Sarah wife of my lord, bear a son to my lord, after she had become old,—so he gave to him all that he had;

text.NRSV: My master made me swear, saying, “You shall not take a wife for my son from the daughters of the Canaanites, in whose land I live;
text.Rotherham: And my lord put me on oath saying,—Thou shalt not take a wife for my son, of the daughters of the Canaanites in whose land, I, am dwelling;

text.NRSV: but you shall go to my father’s house, to my kindred, and get a wife for my son.”
text.Rotherham: but, unto the house of my father, shalt thou go, and unto my family,—and take a wife for my son.

text.NRSV: I said to my master, “Perhaps the woman will not follow me.”
text.Rotherham: So I said unto my lord,—Peradventure the woman will not follow me.

text.NRSV: But he said to me, “The Lord, before whom I walk, will send his angel with you and make your way successful. You shall get a wife for my son from my kindred, from my father’s house.
text.Rotherham: And he said unto me,—Yahweh, before whom I have walked to and fro, will send his messenger with thee, so shall he prosper thy way, and thou shalt take a wife for my son out of my kindred and out of the house of my father.

text.NRSV: Then you will be free from my oath, when you come to my kindred; even if they will not give her to you, you will be free from my oath.”
text.Rotherham: Then, shalt thou clear thyself from mine oath, for thou shalt go in unto my kindred,—and, if they will not grant her unto thee, then shalt thou be clear from mine oath.

text.NRSV: ‘I came today to the spring, and said, “O Lord, the God of my master Abraham, if now you will only make successful the way I am going!
text.Rotherham: So I came in to-day, unto the fountain,—and I said O Yahweh God of my lord Abraham! if, I pray thee thou art prospering my way whereon I am going,

text.NRSV: I am standing here by the spring of water; let the young woman who comes out to draw, to whom I shall say, ‘Please give me a little water from your jar to drink,’
text.Rotherham: here, am I stationed by the fountain of water,—so then it shall come to pass that, the maiden that is coming forth to draw, and I shall say unto her Let me drink. I pray thee a little water out of thy pitcher;

text.NRSV: and who will say to me, ‘Drink, and I will draw for your camels also’—let her be the woman whom the Lord has appointed for my master’s son.”
text.Rotherham: and she shall say unto me Both, thou, drink, and for thy camels also, will I draw, the same, shall be the woman whom Yahweh hath appointed for the son of my lord.

text.NRSV: ‘Before I had finished speaking in my heart, there was Rebekah coming out with her water-jar on her shoulder; and she went down to the spring, and drew. I said to her, “Please let me drink.”
text.Rotherham: Ere yet, I, could make an end of speaking unto mine own heart, lo! then Rebekah, coming forth, with her pitcher on her shoulder, and she went down to the fountain, and drew,—and I said unto her Let me drink I pray thee!

text.NRSV: She quickly let down her jar from her shoulder, and said, “Drink, and I will also water your camels.” So I drank, and she also watered the camels.
text.Rotherham: So she hastened and lowered her pitcher from off her, and said. Drink! and to thy camels also, will I give to drink,—So I drank and the camels also, she let drink.

text.NRSV: Then I asked her, “Whose daughter are you?” She said, “The daughter of Bethuel, Nahor’s son, whom Milcah bore to him.” So I put the ring on her nose, and the bracelets on her arms.
text.Rotherham: Then I asked her, and said, Whose daughter art thou? And she said Daughter of Bethuel son of Nahor, whom, Milcah, bare to him,—Then put I the ring upon her nose, and the bracelets upon her hands;

text.NRSV: Then I bowed my head and worshipped the Lord, and blessed the Lord, the God of my master Abraham, who had led me by the right way to obtain the daughter of my master’s kinsman for his son.
text.Rotherham: and bowed my head and bent myself down unto Yahweh,—and I blessed Yahweh God of my lord Abraham, who had led me in a way of faithfulness, to take the daughter of the brother of my lord for his son.

text.NRSV: Now then, if you will deal loyally and truly with my master, tell me; and if not, tell me, so that I may turn either to the right hand or to the left.’
text.Rotherham: Now, therefore, if ye are dealing in lovingkindness and faithfulness with my lord, tell me,—and, if not, tell me, that I may turn unto the right hand or unto the left.

text.NRSV: Then Laban and Bethuel answered, ‘The thing comes from the Lord; we cannot speak to you anything bad or good.
text.Rotherham: Then answered Laban and Bethuel and said, from Yahweh, hath come forth the word,—we cannot speak unto thee ill or well.

text.NRSV: Look, Rebekah is before you; take her and go, and let her be the wife of your master’s son, as the Lord has spoken.’
text.Rotherham: Lo! Rebekah, is before thee, Take her and go thy way,—And let her become wife unto the son of thy lord, as Yahweh hath spoken.

text.NRSV: When Abraham’s servant heard their words, he bowed himself to the ground before the Lord.
text.Rotherham: And it came to pass when Abraham’s servant heard their words, that he bowed himself down towards the earth unto Yahweh.

text.NRSV: And the servant brought out jewellery of silver and of gold, and garments, and gave them to Rebekah; he also gave to her brother and to her mother costly ornaments.
text.Rotherham: Then the servant brought forth jewels of silver and jewels of gold and raiment, and gave unto Rebekah,—and precious things, gave he to her brother and to her mother.

text.NRSV: Then he and the men who were with him ate and drank, and they spent the night there. When they rose in the morning, he said, ‘Send me back to my master.’
text.Rotherham: And they did eat and drink he, and the men who were with him, and they tarried the night,—and when they arose in the morning, he said Let me go unto my lord!

text.NRSV: Her brother and her mother said, ‘Let the girl remain with us a while, at least ten days; after that she may go.’
text.Rotherham: Then said her brother and her mother, Let the young woman remain with us some days or rather ten,—After that, she shall go.

text.NRSV: But he said to them, ‘Do not delay me, since the Lord has made my journey successful; let me go, that I may go to my master.’
text.Rotherham: And he said unto them, Do not hinder me, when, Yahweh, hath prospered my journey,—Let me go, that I may take my journey unto my lord!

text.NRSV: They said, ‘We will call the girl, and ask her.’
text.Rotherham: Then said they, We must call the young woman and ask at her mouth.

text.NRSV: And they called Rebekah, and said to her, ‘Will you go with this man?’ She said, ‘I will.’
text.Rotherham: So they called Rebekah and said unto her, Wilt thou go with this man? And she said, I will go.

text.NRSV: So they sent away their sister Rebekah and her nurse along with Abraham’s servant and his men.
text.Rotherham: So they let go Rebekah their sister, and her nurse, and Abraham’s servant and his men.

text.NRSV: And they blessed Rebekah and said to her, ‘May you, our sister, become thousands of myriads; may your offspring gain possession of the gates of their foes.’
text.Rotherham: And they blessed Rebekah and said to her, Our sister! become thou, thousands of ten thousands,—And let thy seed take possession of the gate of them that hate them!

text.NRSV: Then Rebekah and her maids rose up, mounted the camels, and followed the man; thus the servant took Rebekah, and went his way.
text.Rotherham: Then mounted Rebekah and her young woman and they rode upon the camels, and followed the man,—so the servant took Rebekah. and went his way.

text.NRSV: Now Isaac had come from Beer-lahai-roi, and was settled in the Negeb.
text.Rotherham: Now, Isaac, had come in at the entrance, of Beer-lahai-roi,—for, he, was dwelling in the land of the South:

text.NRSV: Isaac went out in the evening to walk in the field; and looking up, he saw camels coming.
text.Rotherham: and Isaac came forth to meditate in the field at the approach of evening,—so he lifted up his eyes and looked, and lo! camels, coming in.

text.NRSV: And Rebekah looked up, and when she saw Isaac, she slipped quickly from the camel,
text.Rotherham: And Rebekah lifted up her eyes, and saw Isaac,—so she alighted with haste from off the camel;

text.NRSV: and said to the servant, ‘Who is the man over there, walking in the field to meet us?’ The servant said, ‘It is my master.’ So she took her veil and covered herself.
text.Rotherham: and said unto the servant, Who is this man that is walking in the field to meet us? And the servant said, That, is my lord. So she took the veil and covered herself.

text.NRSV: And the servant told Isaac all the things that he had done.
text.Rotherham: And the servant recounted to Isaac,—all the things which he had done.

text.NRSV: Then Isaac brought her into his mother Sarah’s tent. He took Rebekah, and she became his wife; and he loved her. So Isaac was comforted after his mother’s death.
text.Rotherham: And Isaac brought her into the tent of Sarah his mother; thus he took Rebekah and she became his wife and he loved her,—and Isaac consoled himself, for the loss of his mother.

text.NRSV: Abraham took another wife, whose name was Keturah.
text.Rotherham: And Abraham took another wife and her name, was Keturah;

text.NRSV: She bore him Zimran, Jokshan, Medan, Midian, Ishbak, and Shuah.
text.Rotherham: and she bare to him Zimran, and Yokshan, and Medan, and Midian,—and Ishbak, and Shuah.

text.NRSV: Jokshan was the father of Sheba and Dedan. The sons of Dedan were Asshurim, Letushim, and Leummim.
text.Rotherham: And, Yokshan, begat Sheba and Dedan,—and, the sons of Dedan, were Asshurim and Letushim, and Leummim.

text.NRSV: The sons of Midian were Ephah, Epher, Hanoch, Abida, and Eldaah. All these were the children of Keturah.
text.Rotherham: And the sons of Midian, Ephah and Epher and Hanoch, and Abida. and Eldaah,—all these, were the sons of Keturah.

text.NRSV: Abraham gave all he had to Isaac.
text.Rotherham: And Abraham gave all that he had to Isaac;

text.NRSV: But to the sons of his concubines Abraham gave gifts, while he was still living, and he sent them away from his son Isaac, eastward to the east country.
text.Rotherham: but, to the sons of the concubines whom Abraham had, Abraham gave gifts,—and then sent them away from Isaac his son while he himself yet lived, eastward unto the land of the east.

text.NRSV: This is the length of Abraham’s life, one hundred and seventy-five years.
text.Rotherham: Now, these, are the days of the years of the life of Abraham which he lived—a hundred and seventy-five years.

text.NRSV: Abraham breathed his last and died in a good old age, an old man and full of years, and was gathered to his people.
text.Rotherham: So Abraham breathed his last, and died in a fine old age, old and satisfied,—and was gathered unto his people,

text.NRSV: His sons Isaac and Ishmael buried him in the cave of Machpelah, in the field of Ephron son of Zohar the Hittite, east of Mamre,
text.Rotherham: And Isaac and Ishmael his sons buried him, in the cave of Machpelah,—in the field of Ephron son of Zohar the Hittite, which is over against Mamre;

text.NRSV: the field that Abraham purchased from the Hittites. There Abraham was buried, with his wife Sarah.
text.Rotherham: the field which Abraham purchased of the sons of Heth,—there, was buried Abraham, with Sarah his wife.

text.NRSV: After the death of Abraham God blessed his son Isaac. And Isaac settled at Beer-lahai-roi.
text.Rotherham: And it came to pass after the death of Abraham, that God blessed Isaac his son,—and Isaac dwelt by Beer-lahai-roi.

text.NRSV: These are the descendants of Ishmael, Abraham’s son, whom Hagar the Egyptian, Sarah’s slave-girl, bore to Abraham.
text.Rotherham: Now, these, are the generations of Ishmael son of Abraham,—whom hagar the Egyptian woman the handmaid of Sarah bare to Abraham;

text.NRSV: These are the names of the sons of Ishmael, named in the order of their birth: Nebaioth, the firstborn of Ishmael; and Kedar, Adbeel, Mibsam,
text.Rotherham: and these, are the names of the sons of Ishmael, by their names after their generations,—the firstborn of Ishmael, Nebaioth, and Kedar and Adbeel and Mibsam;

text.NRSV: Mishma, Dumah, Massa,
text.Rotherham: and Mishma and Dumah, and Massa;

text.NRSV: Hadad, Tema, Jetur, Naphish, and Kedemah.
text.Rotherham: Hadad, and Tema, Jetur, Naphish, and Kedemah:

text.NRSV: These are the sons of Ishmael and these are their names, by their villages and by their encampments, twelve princes according to their tribes.
text.Rotherham: these, same, are the sons of Ishmael and these, their names, by their villages and by their encampments,—twelve princes, after their tribes.

text.NRSV: (This is the length of the life of Ishmael, one hundred and thirty-seven years; he breathed his last and died, and was gathered to his people.)
text.Rotherham: And these, are the years of the life of Ishmael, a hundred and thirty-seven years,—and he breathed his last and died, and was gathered unto his people.

text.NRSV: They settled from Havilah to Shur, which is opposite Egypt in the direction of Assyria; he settled down alongside all his people.
text.Rotherham: And they stretched their habitations from Havilah as far as to Shur, which is over against Egypt, as thou goest in towards Assyria,—over against all his brethren, he settled down.

text.NRSV: These are the descendants of Isaac, Abraham’s son: Abraham was the father of Isaac,
text.Rotherham: And, these, are the generations of Isaac, son of Abraham,—Abraham, begat Isaac;

text.NRSV: and Isaac was forty years old when he married Rebekah, daughter of Bethuel the Aramean of Paddan-aram, sister of Laban the Aramean.
text.Rotherham: and it came to pass that, Isaac, was forty years old, when he took Rebekah daughter of Bethuel the Syrian, of the Plain of Syria,—sister of Laban the Syrian. to himself to wife.

text.NRSV: Isaac prayed to the Lord for his wife, because she was barren; and the Lord granted his prayer, and his wife Rebekah conceived.
text.Rotherham: So then Isaac made entreaty unto Yahweh in behalf of his wife, for she was, barren,—and Yahweh suffered himself to be entreated by him, and Rebekah his wife conceived.

text.NRSV: The children struggled together within her; and she said, ‘If it is to be this way, why do I live?’ So she went to inquire of the Lord.
text.Rotherham: And the sons within her struggled together, so she said—If so, wherefore now am, I, [thus] ? And she went to seek Yahweh.

text.NRSV: And the Lord said to her, ‘Two nations are in your womb, and two peoples born of you shall be divided; one shall be stronger than the other, the elder shall serve the younger.’
text.Rotherham: And Yahweh said to her. Two nations, are in thy womb, And two races, from thy body, shall be parted,—And, one race, shall be stronger, than the other race, And the elder, shall serve the younger.

text.NRSV: When her time to give birth was at hand, there were twins in her womb.
text.Rotherham: Then were fulfilled her days to bring forth,—and lo! twins in her womb.

text.NRSV: The first came out red, all his body like a hairy mantle; so they named him Esau.
text.Rotherham: And the first came forth red, all over, as a mantle of hair,—so they called his name, Esau,

text.NRSV: Afterwards his brother came out, with his hand gripping Esau’s heel; so he was named Jacob. Isaac was sixty years old when she bore them.
text.Rotherham: And, after that, came forth his brother with his hand fast hold of the heel of Esau, so they called his name Jacob—Now, Isaac, was sixty years old when she bare them,

text.NRSV: When the boys grew up, Esau was a skilful hunter, a man of the field, while Jacob was a quiet man, living in tents.
text.Rotherham: And when the youths grew up, it came to pass that Esau was a man skilled in game a man of the field,—but, Jacob, was a ready man, dwelling in tents.

text.NRSV: Isaac loved Esau, because he was fond of game; but Rebekah loved Jacob.
text.Rotherham: And, Isaac, loved, Esau, because of the game he put in his mouth,—but, Rebekah, was a lover of Jacob!.

text.NRSV: Once when Jacob was cooking a stew, Esau came in from the field, and he was famished.
text.Rotherham: And Jacob had boiled pottage,—when Esau came in from the field, he, being famished.

text.NRSV: Esau said to Jacob, ‘Let me eat some of that red stuff, for I am famished!’ (Therefore he was called Edom.)
text.Rotherham: So Esau said unto Jacob. Do let me devour some of the red—this red, for, famished, I am. For this cause, was his name called Edom.

text.NRSV: Jacob said, ‘First sell me your birthright.’
text.Rotherham: And Jacob said,—Come sell, just now, thy birthright unto me.

text.NRSV: Esau said, ‘I am about to die; of what use is a birthright to me?’
text.Rotherham: And Esau said, Here am I, on the point of dying,—wherefore, then, should I, have, a birthright?

text.NRSV: Jacob said, ‘Swear to me first.’ So he swore to him, and sold his birthright to Jacob.
text.Rotherham: And Jacob said, Come swear to me just now! And he sware to him,—and sold his birthright to Jacob.

text.NRSV: Then Jacob gave Esau bread and lentil stew, and he ate and drank, and rose and went his way. Thus Esau despised his birthright.
text.Rotherham: And Jacob, having given to Esau bread and a dish of lentils, he did eat and drink and rose up, and went his way,—thus Esau despised his birthright!

text.NRSV: Now there was a famine in the land, besides the former famine that had occurred in the days of Abraham. And Isaac went to Gerar, to King Abimelech of the Philistines.
text.Rotherham: And there came to be a famine in the land, besides the first famine which happened in the days of Abraham,—so Isaac went his way unto Abimelech, king of the Philistines towards Gerar;

text.NRSV: The Lord appeared to Isaac and said, ‘Do not go down to Egypt; settle in the land that I shall show you.
text.Rotherham: and Yahweh appeared unto him, and said, do not go down towards Egypt,—Make thy habitation in the land, which I may name unto thee:

text.NRSV: Reside in this land as an alien, and I will be with you, and will bless you; for to you and to your descendants I will give all these lands, and I will fulfil the oath that I swore to your father Abraham.
text.Rotherham: Sojourn in this land, that I may be with thee and bless thee,—for, to thee, and to thy seed, will I give all these lands, So will I establish the oath which I sware to Abraham thy father;

text.NRSV: I will make your offspring as numerous as the stars of heaven, and will give to your offspring all these lands; and all the nations of the earth shall gain blessing for themselves through your offspring,
text.Rotherham: and will multiply thy seed as the stars of the heavens, and will give to thy seed, all these lands,—And all the nations of the earth shall bless themselves in thy seed:

text.NRSV: because Abraham obeyed my voice and kept my charge, my commandments, my statutes, and my laws.’
text.Rotherham: Because that Abraham hearkened unto my voice,—and so kept my charge, my commandments my statutes and my laws.

text.NRSV: So Isaac settled in Gerar.
text.Rotherham: So Isaac dwelt in Gerar.

text.NRSV: When the men of the place asked him about his wife, he said, ‘She is my sister’; for he was afraid to say, ‘My wife,’ thinking, ‘or else the men of the place might kill me for the sake of Rebekah, because she is attractive in appearance.’
text.Rotherham: Then asked the men of the place as to his wife, and he said, My sister, is she,—For he feared to say My wife, lest [said he] the men of the place should slay me on account of Rebekah, for fair to look on, she is.

text.NRSV: When Isaac had been there a long time, King Abimelech of the Philistines looked out of a window and saw him fondling his wife Rebekah.
text.Rotherham: And it came to pass when the days had lengthened out to him there, that Abimelech king of the Philistines, looked out through the lattice of a window,—and he beheld and lo! Isaac—laughing with Rebekah his wife.

text.NRSV: So Abimelech called for Isaac, and said, ‘So she is your wife! Why then did you say, “She is my sister”?’ Isaac said to him, ‘Because I thought I might die because of her.’
text.Rotherham: So Abimelech called for Isaac, and said, But lo she is, thy wife! How then, saidst thou, She is, my sister? And Isaac said unto him, Because, I said, Lest I die on her account.

text.NRSV: Abimelech said, ‘What is this you have done to us? One of the people might easily have lain with your wife, and you would have brought guilt upon us.’
text.Rotherham: Then, said, Abimelech, What is this thou hast done to us? A little more, and one of the people might have lien with thy wife, so shouldst thou have brought upon us, guilt.

text.NRSV: So Abimelech warned all the people, saying, ‘Whoever touches this man or his wife shall be put to death.’
text.Rotherham: And Abimelech commanded all the people saying,—He that toucheth this man or his wife, shall surely be put to death

text.NRSV: Isaac sowed seed in that land, and in the same year reaped a hundredfold. The Lord blessed him,
text.Rotherham: And Isaac sowed in that land, and found in the same year, a hundredfold,—seeing that Yahweh had blessed him.

text.NRSV: and the man became rich; he prospered more and more until he became very wealthy.
text.Rotherham: So the man waxed great,—and went on and on waxing great until that he had waxed exceeding great.

text.NRSV: He had possessions of flocks and herds, and a great household, so that the Philistines envied him.
text.Rotherham: So he came to have possessions of flocks and possessions of herds, and a large body of servants,—and the Philistines envied him.

text.NRSV: (Now the Philistines had stopped up and filled with earth all the wells that his father’s servants had dug in the days of his father Abraham.)
text.Rotherham: And as for all the wells which the servants of his father digged, in the days of Abraham his father, the Philistines stopped them up, and filled them with dust.

text.NRSV: And Abimelech said to Isaac, ‘Go away from us; you have become too powerful for us.’
text.Rotherham: And Abimelech said unto Isaac,—Get thee from us, for thou hast become far mightier than we.

text.NRSV: So Isaac departed from there and camped in the valley of Gerar and settled there.
text.Rotherham: So Isaac went from thence,—and encamped in the valley of Gerar, and dwelt there.

text.NRSV: Isaac dug again the wells of water that had been dug in the days of his father Abraham; for the Philistines had stopped them up after the death of Abraham; and he gave them the names that his father had given them.
text.Rotherham: And Isaac again digged the wells of water which they had digged in the days of Abraham his father, and which the Philistines stopped up after the death of Abraham,—and he called their names, after the names which his father called them.

text.NRSV: But when Isaac’s servants dug in the valley and found there a well of spring water,
text.Rotherham: And Isaac’s servants digged in the valley,—and found there a well of living water.

text.NRSV: the herders of Gerar quarrelled with Isaac’s herders, saying, ‘The water is ours.’ So he called the well Esek, because they contended with him.
text.Rotherham: And the herdmen of Gerar disputed with the herdmen of Isaac saying, Ours, is the, water! So he called the name of the well Esek, because they had stirred up a quarrel with him.

text.NRSV: Then they dug another well, and they quarrelled over that one also; so he called it Sitnah.
text.Rotherham: And they digged another well, and they disputed over that also,—so he called the name thereof Sitnah.

text.NRSV: He moved from there and dug another well, and they did not quarrel over it; so he called it Rehoboth, saying, ‘Now the Lord has made room for us, and we shall be fruitful in the land.’
text.Rotherham: And he moved on from thence and digged another well, and they disputed not for it,—so he called the name thereof. Rehoboth, and said, For, now, hath Yahweh made room for us, and we shall be fruitful in the land.

text.NRSV: From there he went up to Beer-sheba.
text.Rotherham: And he went up from thence, to Beer-sheba.

text.NRSV: And that very night the Lord appeared to him and said, ‘I am the God of your father Abraham; do not be afraid, for I am with you and will bless you and make your offspring numerous for my servant Abraham’s sake.’
text.Rotherham: And Yahweh appeared unto him the same night, and said, I, am the God of Abraham thy father,—Do not fear for with thee, am, I, And I will bless thee and multiply thy seed, For the sake of Abraham my servant.

text.NRSV: So he built an altar there, called on the name of the Lord, and pitched his tent there. And there Isaac’s servants dug a well.
text.Rotherham: So he builded there all altar and called on the name of Yahweh, and spread out there, his tent,—and the servants of Isaac cut out there a well.

text.NRSV: Then Abimelech went to him from Gerar, with Ahuzzath his adviser and Phicol the commander of his army.
text.Rotherham: And, Abimelech, came unto him from Gerar, with Ahuzzath his friend, and Phicol, commander of his host.

text.NRSV: Isaac said to them, ‘Why have you come to me, seeing that you hate me and have sent me away from you?’
text.Rotherham: And Isaac said unto them, Wherefore have ye come in mite me, seeing that, ye, hated me, and sent me away from you?

text.NRSV: They said, ‘We see plainly that the Lord has been with you; so we say, let there be an oath between you and us, and let us make a covenant with you
text.Rotherham: And they said: We, saw plainly, that Yahweh was with thee, so we said, Let there be we pray thee an oath betwixt us both, betwixt us and thee,—and let us solemnise a covenant with thee:—

text.NRSV: so that you will do us no harm, just as we have not touched you and have done to you nothing but good and have sent you away in peace. You are now the blessed of the Lord.’
text.Rotherham: That, thou wilt not do wrong by us, according as we touched not thee, and according as we did by thee nothing but good, and then sent thee away in peace, thee who, now, art blessed of Yahweh!

text.NRSV: So he made them a feast, and they ate and drank.
text.Rotherham: So he made for them a banquet, and they did eat and drink,—

text.NRSV: In the morning they rose early and exchanged oaths; and Isaac set them on their way, and they departed from him in peace.
text.Rotherham: and rose up early in the morning, and took an oath each man to his brother, so Isaac let them go, and they departed from him in peace.

text.NRSV: That same day Isaac’s servants came and told him about the well that they had dug, and said to him, ‘We have found water!’
text.Rotherham: And it came to pass, on the same day, that the servants of Isaac came in, and told him concerning the well which they had digged, and said to him, We have found water!

text.NRSV: He called it Shibah; therefore the name of the city is Beer-sheba to this day.
text.Rotherham: So he called it, Shibah. For this cause, hath the name of the city, been Beer-sheba, unto this day.

text.NRSV: When Esau was forty years old, he married Judith daughter of Beeri the Hittite, and Basemath daughter of Elon the Hittite;
text.Rotherham: And when Esau was forty years old, he took to wife Judith, daughter of Beeri. the Hittite; and Basemath, daughter of Elon, the Hittite;

text.NRSV: and they made life bitter for Isaac and Rebekah.
text.Rotherham: and they became a bitterness of spirit, to Isaac and to Rebekah.

text.NRSV: When Isaac was old and his eyes were dim so that he could not see, he called his elder son Esau and said to him, ‘My son’; and he answered, ‘Here I am.’
text.Rotherham: And it came to pass that Isaac, was old, and his eyes became too dim to see, so he called Esau his elder son and said unto him My son! And he said unto him, Behold me!

text.NRSV: He said, ‘See, I am old; I do not know the day of my death.
text.Rotherham: And he said, Behold, I pray thee, I am old,—I know not the day of my death.

text.NRSV: Now then, take your weapons, your quiver and your bow, and go out to the field, and hunt game for me.
text.Rotherham: Now, therefore, take I pray thee, thy weapons, thy quiver and thy bow, and go out to the field, and catch for me game;

text.NRSV: Then prepare for me savoury food, such as I like, and bring it to me to eat, so that I may bless you before I die.’
text.Rotherham: and make for me dainty meats, such as I love and bring in to me, that I may eat,—To the end my soul may bless thee, ere yet I die!

text.NRSV: Now Rebekah was listening when Isaac spoke to his son Esau. So when Esau went to the field to hunt for game and bring it,
text.Rotherham: Now, Rebekah, was hearkening, when Isaac spake unto Esau his son, and Esau went his way to the field, to catch game, to bring in.

text.NRSV: Rebekah said to her son Jacob, ‘I heard your father say to your brother Esau,
text.Rotherham: Rebekah, therefore spake unto Jacob her son saying,—Lo! I heard thy father, speaking unto Esau thy brother, saying,

text.NRSV: “Bring me game, and prepare for me savoury food to eat, that I may bless you before the Lord before I die.”
text.Rotherham: Bring in for me game and make for me dainty meats, that I may eat; and let me bless thee in the presence of Yahweh before my death.

text.NRSV: Now therefore, my son, obey my word as I command you.
text.Rotherham: Now, therefore my son, hearken unto my voice,—in that which I am commanding thee:

text.NRSV: Go to the flock, and get me two choice kids, so that I may prepare from them savoury food for your father, such as he likes;
text.Rotherham: Go, I pray thee, unto the flock, and fetch me from thence two kids of the goats, fine ones,—that I may make of them dainty meats for thy father such as he loveth:

text.NRSV: and you shall take it to your father to eat, so that he may bless you before he dies.’
text.Rotherham: Then shalt thou take them in unto thy father and he shall eat,—To the end he may bless thee before his death.

text.NRSV: But Jacob said to his mother Rebekah, ‘Look, my brother Esau is a hairy man, and I am a man of smooth skin.
text.Rotherham: And Jacob said unto Rebekah his mother, Lo! Esau my brother, is a hairy man, whereas, I, am a smooth man:

text.NRSV: Perhaps my father will feel me, and I shall seem to be mocking him, and bring a curse on myself and not a blessing.’
text.Rotherham: peradventure my father might feel me, then should I be in his eyes as one that mocketh,—and should bring upon myself a reproach, and not a blessing!

text.NRSV: His mother said to him, ‘Let your curse be on me, my son; only obey my word, and go, get them for me.’
text.Rotherham: And his mother said to him, Upon me, be thy reproach my son,—only hearken unto my voice and go fetch [them] for me.

text.NRSV: So he went and got them and brought them to his mother; and his mother prepared savoury food, such as his father loved.
text.Rotherham: So he went, and fetched [them], and brought them in to his mother, and his mother made dainty meats, such as his father loved.

text.NRSV: Then Rebekah took the best garments of her elder son Esau, which were with her in the house, and put them on her younger son Jacob;
text.Rotherham: Then took Rebekah the garments of Esau her elder son, the costly ones, which were with her in the house,—and put them on Jacob her younger son:

text.NRSV: and she put the skins of the kids on his hands and on the smooth part of his neck.
text.Rotherham: and the, skins of the kids of the goats, put she upon his hands,—and on the smooth part of his neck;

text.NRSV: Then she handed the savoury food, and the bread that she had prepared, to her son Jacob.
text.Rotherham: then placed she the dainty meats and the bread, which she had made ready, in the hand of Jacob her son.

text.NRSV: So he went in to his father, and said, ‘My father’; and he said, ‘Here I am; who are you, my son?’
text.Rotherham: So he went in unto his father, and said My father! And he said Behold me! who art, thou, my son?

text.NRSV: Jacob said to his father, ‘I am Esau your firstborn. I have done as you told me; now sit up and eat of my game, so that you may bless me.’
text.Rotherham: And Jacob said unto his father I, am Esau thy firstborn, I have made ready, as thou didst bid me. Rise, I pray thee, sit up, and eat thou of my game, To the end thy soul may bless me.

text.NRSV: But Isaac said to his son, ‘How is it that you have found it so quickly, my son?’ He answered, ‘Because the Lord your God granted me success.’
text.Rotherham: Then said Isaac unto his son. How is it thou hast been so quick in finding, my son? And he said, Because Yahweh thy God caused it so to fall out before me.

text.NRSV: Then Isaac said to Jacob, ‘Come near, that I may feel you, my son, to know whether you are really my son Esau or not.’
text.Rotherham: Then said Isaac unto Jacob. Come near, I pray thee, that I may feel thee my son—whether, thou thyself, art my son Esau, or not.

text.NRSV: So Jacob went up to his father Isaac, who felt him and said, ‘The voice is Jacob’s voice, but the hands are the hands of Esau.’
text.Rotherham: So Jacob came near unto Isaac his father, and he felt him; then said he the voice is the voice of Jacob; But, the hands, are the hands of Esau.

text.NRSV: He did not recognize him, because his hands were hairy like his brother Esau’s hands; so he blessed him.
text.Rotherham: So he did not find him out, because, his hands were like the hands of Esau his brother hairy,—so he blessed him.

text.NRSV: He said, ‘Are you really my son Esau?’ He answered, ‘I am.’
text.Rotherham: And he said, Thou thyself, art my son Esau? And he said, I am!

text.NRSV: Then he said, ‘Bring it to me, that I may eat of my son’s game and bless you.’ So he brought it to him, and he ate; and he brought him wine, and he drank.
text.Rotherham: So he said, Bring it near to me., that I may eat of the game of my son, To the end my soul may bless thee. And he brought it near to him, and he did eat, and he brought in to him wine, and he drank.

text.NRSV: Then his father Isaac said to him, ‘Come near and kiss me, my son.’
text.Rotherham: Then Isaac his father said unto him,—Come thou near I pray thee and kiss me my son.

text.NRSV: So he came near and kissed him; and he smelled the smell of his garments, and blessed him, and said, ‘Ah, the smell of my son is like the smell of a field that the Lord has blessed.
text.Rotherham: So he came near, and kissed him, and he smelled the smell of his garments and blessed him,—and said. See! the smell of my son, As the smell of a field, which Yahweh hath blessed;

text.NRSV: May God give you of the dew of heaven, and of the fatness of the earth, and plenty of grain and wine.
text.Rotherham: Then, may God give thee of the dew of the heavens, And of the fatness of the earth,—And abundance of corn and new wine:

text.NRSV: Let peoples serve you, and nations bow down to you. Be lord over your brothers, and may your mother’s sons bow down to you. Cursed be everyone who curses you, and blessed be everyone who blesses you!’
text.Rotherham: Let peoples serve thee And races bow down to thee, Become thou lord to thy brethren, And let the sons of thy mother bow down to thee,—He that curseth thee, be accursed! And he that blesseth thee, be blessed!

text.NRSV: As soon as Isaac had finished blessing Jacob, when Jacob had scarcely gone out from the presence of his father Isaac, his brother Esau came in from his hunting.
text.Rotherham: And it came to pass as soon as Isaac had made an end of blessing Jacob,—yea it came to pass when Jacob, had only just gone forth, from the presence of Isaac his father, that Esau his brother, came in from his hunting.

text.NRSV: He also prepared savoury food, and brought it to his father. And he said to his father, ‘Let my father sit up and eat of his son’s game, so that you may bless me.’
text.Rotherham: Then, he too, made dainty meats, and brought in to his father,—and said to his father, Let my father rise that he may eat of the game of his son, To the end thy soul may bless me!

text.NRSV: His father Isaac said to him, ‘Who are you?’ He answered, ‘I am your firstborn son, Esau.’
text.Rotherham: And Isaac his father said to him Who art, thou? And he said, I, am thy son thy firstborn, Esau.

text.NRSV: Then Isaac trembled violently, and said, ‘Who was it then that hunted game and brought it to me, and I ate it all before you came, and I have blessed him?—yes, and blessed he shall be!’
text.Rotherham: Then did Isaac tremble with an exceeding great trembling, and said Who then was it that caught game and brought in to me and I did eat of all ere yet thou didst come in and I blessed him? Yea blessed, shall he remain!

text.NRSV: When Esau heard his father’s words, he cried out with an exceedingly great and bitter cry, and said to his father, ‘Bless me, me also, father!’
text.Rotherham: When Esau heard the words of his father, then cried he out with an outcry loud and bitter exceedingly,—and said to his father, Bless even me also, O my father!

text.NRSV: But he said, ‘Your brother came deceitfully, and he has taken away your blessing.’
text.Rotherham: Then he said, Thy brother came in with deceit,—and took away thy blessing.

text.NRSV: Esau said, ‘Is he not rightly named Jacob? For he has supplanted me these two times. He took away my birthright; and look, now he has taken away my blessing.’ Then he said, ‘Have you not reserved a blessing for me?’
text.Rotherham: And he said, Is it because his name, is called, Jacob, that he hath tricked me, now twice? My birthright, he took away, And lo! now, he hath taken away my blessing! And he said, Hast thou not reserved for me a blessing?

text.NRSV: Isaac answered Esau, ‘I have already made him your lord, and I have given him all his brothers as servants, and with grain and wine I have sustained him. What then can I do for you, my son?’
text.Rotherham: Then answered Isaac and said to Esau,—Lo a lord, have I appointed him unto thee, And, all his brethren, have I given to him as servants, And with corn and new wine, have I sustained him,—And for thee—now, what can I do, my son?

text.NRSV: Esau said to his father, ‘Have you only one blessing, father? Bless me, me also, father!’ And Esau lifted up his voice and wept.
text.Rotherham: And Esau said unto his father, But one blessing, hast thou, O my father? Bless, me also, O my father! And Esau lifted up his voice and wept.

text.NRSV: Then his father Isaac answered him: ‘See, away from the fatness of the earth shall your home be, and away from the dew of heaven on high.
text.Rotherham: Then answered Isaac his father and said unto him,—Lo! of the fat parts of the earth, shall be thy dwelling, And of the dew of the heavens, above;

text.NRSV: By your sword you shall live, and you shall serve your brother; but when you break loose, you shall break his yoke from your neck.’
text.Rotherham: And on thy sword, shalt thou live, And thy brother, shalt thou serve: But it shall come to pass when thou shalt rove at large, Then shalt thou break his yoke from off thy neck.

text.NRSV: Now Esau hated Jacob because of the blessing with which his father had blessed him, and Esau said to himself, ‘The days of mourning for my father are approaching; then I will kill my brother Jacob.’
text.Rotherham: And Esau lay in wait for Jacob, on account of the blessing wherewith his father had blessed him,—and Esau said in his heart, The days of mourning for my father draw near, when I can slay Jacob my brother.

text.NRSV: But the words of her elder son Esau were told to Rebekah; so she sent and called her younger son Jacob and said to him, ‘Your brother Esau is consoling himself by planning to kill you.
text.Rotherham: Then were told to Rebekah, the words of Esau her elder son, so she sent and called for Jacob, her younger son, and said unto him—Lo! Esau, thy brother, is consoling himself as touching thee, to slay thee.

text.NRSV: Now therefore, my son, obey my voice; flee at once to my brother Laban in Haran,
text.Rotherham: Now, therefore, my son, hearken to my voice,—and rise flee thou to Laban my brother to Haran;

text.NRSV: and stay with him for a while, until your brother’s fury turns away—
text.Rotherham: And thou shalt dwell with him a few days,—until that the wrath of thy brother turn away:

text.NRSV: until your brother’s anger against you turns away, and he forgets what you have done to him; then I will send, and bring you back from there. Why should I lose both of you in one day?’
text.Rotherham: until the turning away of the anger of thy brother from thee, and he forget what thou hast done to him, and I send and fetch thee from thence. Wherefore should I lose, you both, in one day?

text.NRSV: Then Rebekah said to Isaac, ‘I am weary of my life because of the Hittite women. If Jacob marries one of the Hittite women such as these, one of the women of the land, what good will my life be to me?’
text.Rotherham: So then Rebekah said unto Isaac, I am disgusted with my life because of the daughters of Heth,—Should Jacob be taking a wife of the daughters of Heth, like these, of the daughters of the land, wherefore could I wish for life?

text.NRSV: Isaac called Jacob and blessed him, and charged him, ‘You shall not marry one of the Canaanite women.
text.Rotherham: Then Isaac called Jacob, and blessed him,—and commanded him and said to him, Thou shalt not take a wife of the daughters of Canaan.

text.NRSV: Go at once to Paddan-aram to the house of Bethuel, your mother’s father; and take as wife from there one of the daughters of Laban, your mother’s brother.
text.Rotherham: Rise, go thy way to Padan-aram to the house of Bethuel, thy mother’s father,—and take thee from thence a wife, of the daughters of Laban, thy mother’s brother.

text.NRSV: May God Almighty bless you and make you fruitful and numerous, that you may become a company of peoples.
text.Rotherham: And, GOD Almighty, bless thee, and make thee fruitful, and multiply thee, so shalt thou become a multitude of peoples.

text.NRSV: May he give to you the blessing of Abraham, to you and to your offspring with you, so that you may take possession of the land where you now live as an alien—land that God gave to Abraham.’
text.Rotherham: And may he give thee the blessing of Abraham, to thee, and to thy seed with thee,—that thou mayest possess the land of thy sojournings, which God gave to Abraham.

text.NRSV: Thus Isaac sent Jacob away; and he went to Paddan-aram, to Laban son of Bethuel the Aramean, the brother of Rebekah, Jacob’s and Esau’s mother.
text.Rotherham: So Isaac sent away Jacob, and he took his journey to Padan-aram,—unto Laban son of Bethuel, the Syrian, brother of Rebekah, mother of Jacob and Esau.

text.NRSV: Now Esau saw that Isaac had blessed Jacob and sent him away to Paddan-aram to take a wife from there, and that as he blessed him he charged him, ‘You shall not marry one of the Canaanite women’,
text.Rotherham: And Esau beheld that Isaac, when he blessed Jacob, and sent him to Padan-aram, to take to himself from thence a wife, that in blessing him, he laid command upon him, saying, Thou shalt not take a wife of the daughters of Canaan;

text.NRSV: and that Jacob had obeyed his father and his mother and gone to Paddan-aram.
text.Rotherham: And that Jacob hearkened unto his father and unto his mother,—and took his journey to Padan-aram.

text.NRSV: So when Esau saw that the Canaanite women did not please his father Isaac,
text.Rotherham: So then Esau beheld that displeasing, were the daughters of Caanan,—in the eyes of Isaac his father:

text.NRSV: Esau went to Ishmael and took Mahalath daughter of Abraham’s son Ishmael, and sister of Nebaioth, to be his wife in addition to the wives he had.
text.Rotherham: Esau therefore went unto Ishmael,—and took Mahalath daughter of Ishmael son of Abraham sister of Nebaioth, to himself to wife, in addition to the wives that he had.

text.NRSV: Jacob left Beer-sheba and went towards Haran.
text.Rotherham: So Jacob went forth from Beer-sheba, and journeyed towards Haran.

text.NRSV: He came to a certain place and stayed there for the night, because the sun had set. Taking one of the stones of the place, he put it under his head and lay down in that place.
text.Rotherham: And he lighted on a place, and tarried the night there, because the sun had gone in,—and he took of the stones of the place, and put for his pillow, and lay down in that place,

text.NRSV: And he dreamed that there was a ladder set up on the earth, the top of it reaching to heaven; and the angels of God were ascending and descending on it.
text.Rotherham: And he dreamed, and lo! a stairway, planted earthwards, with, its top, reaching towards the heavens,—and lo! messengers of God, ascending and descending thereon;

text.NRSV: And the Lord stood beside him and said, ‘I am the Lord, the God of Abraham your father and the God of Isaac; the land on which you lie I will give to you and to your offspring;
text.Rotherham: and lo! Yahweh, standing by him,—and he said, I, am Yahweh, God of Abraham thy father and God of Isaac,—The land whereon, thou, art lying, to thee, will I give it and to thy seed;

text.NRSV: and your offspring shall be like the dust of the earth, and you shall spread abroad to the west and to the east and to the north and to the south; and all the families of the earth shall be blessed in you and in your offspring.
text.Rotherham: And thy seed shall become as the dust of the earth, and thou shalt break forth westward and eastward, and northward and southward,—And all the families of the ground shall be blessed in thee and in thy seed.

text.NRSV: Know that I am with you and will keep you wherever you go, and will bring you back to this land; for I will not leave you until I have done what I have promised you.’
text.Rotherham: Lo! then, I, am with thee, so will I keep thee in every place whithersoever thou mayest go, and will bring thee back unto this soil,—For I will not forsake thee until I have done that of which I have spoken to thee.

text.NRSV: Then Jacob woke from his sleep and said, ‘Surely the Lord is in this place—and I did not know it!’
text.Rotherham: And Jacob awoke from his sleep, and said. Surely, Yahweh was in this place, And I, knew it not.

text.NRSV: And he was afraid, and said, ‘How awesome is this place! This is none other than the house of God, and this is the gate of heaven.’
text.Rotherham: And he feared and said, How fearful is this place! None other this, than the house of God! Nor this than the gate of the heavens

text.NRSV: So Jacob rose early in the morning, and he took the stone that he had put under his head and set it up for a pillar and poured oil on the top of it.
text.Rotherham: So Jacob rose early in the morning, and took the stone which he had put for his pillow, and put it for a pillar,—and poured out oil upon the top thereof;

text.NRSV: He called that place Bethel; but the name of the city was Luz at the first.
text.Rotherham: and called the name of that place—Beth-el,—nevertheless, Luz, was the name of the city, aforetime.

text.NRSV: Then Jacob made a vow, saying, ‘If God will be with me, and will keep me in this way that I go, and will give me bread to eat and clothing to wear,
text.Rotherham: And Jacob vowed a vow, saying,—If God will be with me, and will keep me in this way whereon, I, am going, and give me bread to eat and raiment to put on;

text.NRSV: so that I come again to my father’s house in peace, then the Lord shall be my God,
text.Rotherham: and I come back in prosperity unto the house of my father, Then will Yahweh prove to be my God,—

text.NRSV: and this stone, which I have set up for a pillar, shall be God’s house; and of all that you give me I will surely give one-tenth to you.’
text.Rotherham: And, this stone which I have put for a pillar, shall be the house of God,—And, of all which thou shalt give me, a tenth, will I tithe unto thee.

text.NRSV: Then Jacob went on his journey, and came to the land of the people of the east.
text.Rotherham: Then Jacob lifted up his feet,—and went his way towards the land of the sons of the East.

text.NRSV: As he looked, he saw a well in the field and three flocks of sheep lying there beside it; for out of that well the flocks were watered. The stone on the well’s mouth was large,
text.Rotherham: And he looked, and lo! a well, in the field and lo! there, three flocks of sheep, lying down near it, for out of that well, do they water the flocks, but, the stone, is great, on the mouth of the well:

text.NRSV: and when all the flocks were gathered there, the shepherds would roll the stone from the mouth of the well, and water the sheep, and put the stone back in its place on the mouth of the well.
text.Rotherham: so they gather together thither all the flocks, and roll away the stone from off the mouth of the well, and water the sheep,—and put back the stone upon the mouth of the well, to its place.

text.NRSV: Jacob said to them, ‘My brothers, where do you come from?’ They said, ‘We are from Haran.’
text.Rotherham: And Jacob said to them, My brethren, whence are ye? And they said: From haran, are we.

text.NRSV: He said to them, ‘Do you know Laban son of Nahor?’ They said, ‘We do.’
text.Rotherham: And he said to them, Know ye Laban, son of Nahor? And they said, We know him.

text.NRSV: He said to them, ‘Is it well with him?’ ‘Yes,’ they replied, ‘and here is his daughter Rachel, coming with the sheep.’
text.Rotherham: And he said to them, Is it well with him? And they said, Well, but lo! Rachel his daughter, coming in with the sheep.

text.NRSV: He said, ‘Look, it is still broad daylight; it is not time for the animals to be gathered together. Water the sheep, and go, pasture them.’
text.Rotherham: And he said Lo! the day is yet high, it is not time for gathering together the cattle,—water the sheep, and go feed.

text.NRSV: But they said, ‘We cannot until all the flocks are gathered together, and the stone is rolled from the mouth of the well; then we water the sheep.’
text.Rotherham: And they said, We cannot, until that all the flocks are gathered together, and they roll away the stone, from off the mouth of the well,—and then can we water the sheep.

text.NRSV: While he was still speaking with them, Rachel came with her father’s sheep; for she kept them.
text.Rotherham: While yet he was speaking with them, Rachel, had come in with the sheep which belonged to her father, for a shepherdess, was she.

text.NRSV: Now when Jacob saw Rachel, the daughter of his mother’s brother Laban, and the sheep of his mother’s brother Laban, Jacob went up and rolled the stone from the well’s mouth, and watered the flock of his mother’s brother Laban.
text.Rotherham: And it came to pass when Jacob saw Rachel daughter of Laban, his mother’s brother, and the sheep of Laban, his mother’s brother, that Jacob went near, and rolled away the stone from off the mouth of the well, and watered the sheep of Laban his mother’s brother,

text.NRSV: Then Jacob kissed Rachel, and wept aloud.
text.Rotherham: And Jacob kissed Rachel,—and lifted up his voice, and wept.

text.NRSV: And Jacob told Rachel that he was her father’s kinsman, and that he was Rebekah’s son; and she ran and told her father.
text.Rotherham: And when Jacob told Rachel that he was her father’s brother, and that he was Rebekah’s son, then ran she, and told her father.

text.NRSV: When Laban heard the news about his sister’s son Jacob, he ran to meet him; he embraced him and kissed him, and brought him to his house. Jacob told Laban all these things,
text.Rotherham: And it came to pass when Laban heard the tidings of Jacob his sister’s son, that he ran to meet him, and embraced him, and fondly kissed him, and brought him into his house,—when he recounted to Laban all these things.

text.NRSV: and Laban said to him, ‘Surely you are my bone and my flesh!’ And he stayed with him for a month.
text.Rotherham: And Laban said to him, Surely, my bone and my flesh, art thou. So he abode with him a month of days.

text.NRSV: Then Laban said to Jacob, ‘Because you are my kinsman, should you therefore serve me for nothing? Tell me, what shall your wages be?’
text.Rotherham: Then said Laban to Jacob, Is it because my brother, thou art, that thou shouldst serve me for naught? Come tell me! What shall be thy wages?

text.NRSV: Now Laban had two daughters; the name of the elder was Leah, and the name of the younger was Rachel.
text.Rotherham: Now, Laban, had two daughters,—the name of the elder, Leah, and the name of the younger, Rachel,

text.NRSV: Leah’s eyes were lovely, and Rachel was graceful and beautiful.
text.Rotherham: But, the eyes of Leah were weak,—whereas, Rachel, was comely in form and comely in countenance.

text.NRSV: Jacob loved Rachel; so he said, ‘I will serve you seven years for your younger daughter Rachel.’
text.Rotherham: So Jacob loved Rachel,—and he said, I will serve thee seven years, for Rachel thy younger daughter.

text.NRSV: Laban said, ‘It is better that I give her to you than that I should give her to any other man; stay with me.’
text.Rotherham: And Laban said, Better that I give her to thee, than that I should give her to another man,—Abide with me!

text.NRSV: So Jacob served seven years for Rachel, and they seemed to him but a few days because of the love he had for her.
text.Rotherham: So Jacob served for Rachel—seven years,—and they became, in his eyes, as single days, for his love to her.

text.NRSV: Then Jacob said to Laban, ‘Give me my wife that I may go in to her, for my time is completed.’
text.Rotherham: Then said Jacob unto Laban: Come give me my wife, for fulfilled are my days,—that I may go in unto her.

text.NRSV: So Laban gathered together all the people of the place, and made a feast.
text.Rotherham: So Laban gathered together all the men of the place and made a banquet,

text.NRSV: But in the evening he took his daughter Leah and brought her to Jacob; and he went in to her.
text.Rotherham: And it came to pass in the evening, that he took Leah his daughter and brought her in unto him,—and he went in unto her.

text.NRSV: (Laban gave his maid Zilpah to his daughter Leah to be her maid.)
text.Rotherham: And Laban gave her Zilpah, his handmaid, unto Leah his daughter. as handmaid.

text.NRSV: When morning came, it was Leah! And Jacob said to Laban, ‘What is this you have done to me? Did I not serve with you for Rachel? Why then have you deceived me?’
text.Rotherham: And it came to pass in the morning, that lo! it was Leah,—and he said unto Laban, What is this thou hast done to me? Was it not, for Rachel, I served with thee? Wherefore then hast thou deceived me?

text.NRSV: Laban said, ‘This is not done in our country—giving the younger before the firstborn.
text.Rotherham: And Laban said, It must not be done so in our place,—to give the later-born before the firstborn.

text.NRSV: Complete the week of this one, and we will give you the other also in return for serving me for another seven years.’
text.Rotherham: Fulfil the week of this one,—then must we give thee, the other one also, for the service wherewith thou shalt serve with me, yet seven years more.

text.NRSV: Jacob did so, and completed her week; then Laban gave him his daughter Rachel as a wife.
text.Rotherham: And Jacob did so, and fulfilled the week of this one,—and he gave him Rachel his daughter—to him to wife.

text.NRSV: (Laban gave his maid Bilhah to his daughter Rachel to be her maid.)
text.Rotherham: And Laban gave to Rachel his daughter Bilhah his handmaid,—to be hers as a handmaid.

text.NRSV: So Jacob went in to Rachel also, and he loved Rachel more than Leah. He served Laban for another seven years.
text.Rotherham: So he went in, unto Rachel also, and loved, Rachel also, more than Leah,—and he served with him, yet seven years more.

text.NRSV: When the Lord saw that Leah was unloved, he opened her womb; but Rachel was barren.
text.Rotherham: And, when Yahweh saw that Leah was hated, he granted her to bear children,—whereas, Rachel, was barren.

text.NRSV: Leah conceived and bore a son, and she named him Reuben; for she said, ‘Because the Lord has looked on my affliction; surely now my husband will love me.’
text.Rotherham: So Leah conceived, and bare a son, and called his name Reuben,—for she said, Because, Yahweh hath looked upon my humiliation, For, now, will my husband love me.

text.NRSV: She conceived again and bore a son, and said, ‘Because the Lord has heard that I am hated, he has given me this son also’; and she named him Simeon.
text.Rotherham: And she conceived again, and bare a son, and said. Because Yahweh heard that, I, was hated, he gave me, this one also. So she called his name Simeon.

text.NRSV: Again she conceived and bore a son, and said, ‘Now this time my husband will be joined to me, because I have borne him three sons’; therefore he was named Levi.
text.Rotherham: And she conceived again and bare a son, and said, Now—this time, will my husband be bound unto me, For I have borne him three sons,—For this cause, called she his name Levi.

text.NRSV: She conceived again and bore a son, and said, ‘This time I will praise the Lord’; therefore she named him Judah; then she ceased bearing.
text.Rotherham: And she conceived again and bare a son, and said This time, will I praise Yahweh, For which cause, she called his name Judah. And she left off bearing.

text.NRSV: When Rachel saw that she bore Jacob no children, she envied her sister; and she said to Jacob, ‘Give me children, or I shall die!’
text.Rotherham: And Rachel saw she had borne no children unto Jacob, so Rachel became envious of her sister,—and said unto Jacob, Come! give me children, or else, I die.

text.NRSV: Jacob became very angry with Rachel and said, ‘Am I in the place of God, who has withheld from you the fruit of the womb?’
text.Rotherham: Then kindled the anger of Jacob with Rachel,—and he said, Am I, in the place of God, who hath withheld from thee the fruit of the womb?

text.NRSV: Then she said, ‘Here is my maid Bilhah; go in to her, that she may bear upon my knees and that I too may have children through her.’
text.Rotherham: And she said, Lo! my handmaid, Bilhah. Go in unto her,—that she may bear upon my knees, so that, I also, may be builded up by her!

text.NRSV: So she gave him her maid Bilhah as a wife; and Jacob went in to her.
text.Rotherham: And she gave him Bilhah her handmaid, to wife,—and Jacob went in unto her;

text.NRSV: And Bilhah conceived and bore Jacob a son.
text.Rotherham: and Bilhah conceived, and bare unto Jacob a son;

text.NRSV: Then Rachel said, ‘God has judged me, and has also heard my voice and given me a son’; therefore she named him Dan.
text.Rotherham: and Rachel said God hath vindicated me. Moreover also he hath hearkened unto my voice, and hath given me a son. For this cause, called she his name Dan.

text.NRSV: Rachel’s maid Bilhah conceived again and bore Jacob a second son.
text.Rotherham: And Bilhah, Rachel’s handmaid conceived again, and bare a second son to Jacob.

text.NRSV: Then Rachel said, ‘With mighty wrestlings I have wrestled with my sister, and have prevailed’; so she named him Naphtali.
text.Rotherham: Then said Rachel With wrestlings of God, have I wrestled with my sister Yea I have prevailed. So she called his name, Naphtali.

text.NRSV: When Leah saw that she had ceased bearing children, she took her maid Zilpah and gave her to Jacob as a wife.
text.Rotherham: Then saw Leah, that she had left off bearing,—so she took Zilpah, her handmaid, and gave her to Jacob to wife.

text.NRSV: Then Leah’s maid Zilpah bore Jacob a son.
text.Rotherham: And Zilpah, Leah’s handmaid, bare to Jacob a son:

text.NRSV: And Leah said, ‘Good fortune!’ so she named him Gad.
text.Rotherham: and Leah said, Victory hath come! So she called his name Gad.

text.NRSV: Leah’s maid Zilpah bore Jacob a second son.
text.Rotherham: And Zilpah, Leah’s handmaid bare a second son to Jacob;

text.NRSV: And Leah said, ‘Happy am I! For the women will call me happy’; so she named him Asher.
text.Rotherham: and Leah said, For my happiness, surely happy have daughters pronounced me. So she called his name, Asher.

text.NRSV: In the days of wheat harvest Reuben went and found mandrakes in the field, and brought them to his mother Leah. Then Rachel said to Leah, ‘Please give me some of your son’s mandrakes.’
text.Rotherham: Then went Reuben, in the days of the wheat harvest, and found mandrakes, in the field, and brought them in unto Leah his mother, and Rachel said unto Leah, Pray give me, some of the mandrakes of thy son

text.NRSV: But she said to her, ‘Is it a small matter that you have taken away my husband? Would you take away my son’s mandrakes also?’ Rachel said, ‘Then he may lie with you tonight for your son’s mandrakes.’
text.Rotherham: And she said to her, Is it, a small thing, that thou hast taken away my husband? And wouldst thou take away, even the mandrakes of my son? Then said Rachel, Therefore, shall he lie with thee to-night, for the mandrakes of thy son.

text.NRSV: When Jacob came from the field in the evening, Leah went out to meet him, and said, ‘You must come in to me; for I have hired you with my son’s mandrakes.’ So he lay with her that night.
text.Rotherham: And Jacob came in from the field, in the evening, so Leah went out to meet him and said: Unto me, shalt thou come in, for I have hired, thee, even with the mandrakes of my son. And he lay with her that night,

text.NRSV: And God heeded Leah, and she conceived and bore Jacob a fifth son.
text.Rotherham: And God hearkened unto Leah,—and she conceived and bare to Jacob a fifth son.

text.NRSV: Leah said, ‘God has given me my hire because I gave my maid to my husband’; so she named him Issachar.
text.Rotherham: Then said Leah. God hath given my hire, in that I gave my handmaid to my husband. So she called his name, Issachar.

text.NRSV: And Leah conceived again, and she bore Jacob a sixth son.
text.Rotherham: And Leah conceived again and bare a sixth son to Jacob.

text.NRSV: Then Leah said, ‘God has endowed me with a good dowry; now my husband will honour me, because I have borne him six sons’; so she named him Zebulun.
text.Rotherham: Then said Leah, God hath dowered me even me with a hand-some dowry, Now! will my husband dwell with me, for I have borne him six sons. So she called his name, Zebulon.

text.NRSV: Afterwards she bore a daughter, and named her Dinah.
text.Rotherham: And afterwards she bare a daughter,—so she called her name, Dinah.

text.NRSV: Then God remembered Rachel, and God heeded her and opened her womb.
text.Rotherham: Then God remembered Rachel,—and God hearkened unto her, and granted her to bear.

text.NRSV: She conceived and bore a son, and said, ‘God has taken away my reproach’;
text.Rotherham: So she conceived and bare a son,—and said, God hath taken away my reproach;

text.NRSV: and she named him Joseph, saying, ‘May the Lord add to me another son!’
text.Rotherham: So she called his name Joseph, saying, Yahweh is adding unto me another son.

text.NRSV: When Rachel had borne Joseph, Jacob said to Laban, ‘Send me away, that I may go to my own home and country.
text.Rotherham: And it came to pass, when Rachel had borne Joseph, that Jacob said unto Laban, Let me go that I may take my journey, unto my place, and to my land.

text.NRSV: Give me my wives and my children for whom I have served you, and let me go; for you know very well the service I have given you.’
text.Rotherham: Come, give me my wives and my children for whom I have served thee and let me take my journey,—for, thou, knowest my service wherewith I have served thee.

text.NRSV: But Laban said to him, ‘If you will allow me to say so, I have learned by divination that the Lord has blessed me because of you;
text.Rotherham: And Laban said unto him, If, I pray thee, I have found favour in thine eyes, …I have divined that Yahweh hath blessed me for thy sake.

text.NRSV: name your wages, and I will give them.’
text.Rotherham: And he said,—Come, fix thy wages for me, that I may give it!

text.NRSV: Jacob said to him, ‘You yourself know how I have served you, and how your cattle have fared with me.
text.Rotherham: And he said unto him, Thou, knowest how I have served thee,—and how thy cattle have fared, with me.

text.NRSV: For you had little before I came, and it has increased abundantly; and the Lord has blessed you wherever I turned. But now when shall I provide for my own household also?’
text.Rotherham: For it was, little, that thou hadst—before I came and then it brake forth into multitude, and Yahweh blessed thee at my every step. Now, therefore, when am, I, also to do something for my own house?
