text.NRSV: He said, ‘What shall I give you?’ Jacob said, ‘You shall not give me anything; if you will do this for me, I will again feed your flock and keep it:
text.Rotherham: And he said, What shall I give thee? And Jacob said: Thou shall give me, nothing at all, If thou wilt do for me this thing, I will return, I will shepherd thy flock I will keep it:

text.NRSV: let me pass through all your flock today, removing from it every speckled and spotted sheep and every black lamb, and the spotted and speckled among the goats; and such shall be my wages.
text.Rotherham: Let me pass through all thy flock to-day, removing from thence every young one that is speckled and spotted and every young one that is dark-coloured among the young sheep, and spotted and speckled among the goats, and they shall be my wages.

text.NRSV: So my honesty will answer for me later, when you come to look into my wages with you. Every one that is not speckled and spotted among the goats and black among the lambs, if found with me, shall be counted stolen.’
text.Rotherham: And my righteousness shall answer for me on a future day, when thou shall come in respecting my hire that is before thee, Every one that is not speckled and spotted among the goats and dark-coloured among the young sheep, stolen, shall it be accounted, if found with me.

text.NRSV: Laban said, ‘Good! Let it be as you have said.’
text.Rotherham: Then said Laban, Lo! would it might be according to thy word!

text.NRSV: But that day Laban removed the male goats that were striped and spotted, and all the female goats that were speckled and spotted, every one that had white on it, and every lamb that was black, and put them in charge of his sons;
text.Rotherham: So he removed, on that day, the he-goats that were striped and spotted and all the she-goats that were speckled and spotted, every one that had white in it, and every dark-coloured one among the young sheep,—and delivered them into the hand of his sons;

text.NRSV: and he set a distance of three days’ journey between himself and Jacob, while Jacob was pasturing the rest of Laban’s flock.
text.Rotherham: then put he a journey of three days between himself and Jacob,—but, Jacob himself, continued tending the flocks of Laban that were left.

text.NRSV: Then Jacob took fresh rods of poplar and almond and plane, and peeled white streaks in them, exposing the white of the rods.
text.Rotherham: So then Jacob took him rods of young storax, and hazel and maple,—and peeled in them white stripes, laying bare the white which was on the rods.

text.NRSV: He set the rods that he had peeled in front of the flocks in the troughs, that is, the watering-places, where the flocks came to drink. And since they bred when they came to drink,
text.Rotherham: And he set the rods which he had peeled, in the channels in the troughs of water,—where the flocks came in to drink straight before the flocks, and the females of the flock used to be in heat when they came in to drink;

text.NRSV: the flocks bred in front of the rods, and so the flocks produced young that were striped, speckled, and spotted.
text.Rotherham: and the males of the flock were in heat before the rods,—so the flocks brought forth ring-straked, speckled, and spotted.

text.NRSV: Jacob separated the lambs, and set the faces of the flocks toward the striped and the completely black animals in the flock of Laban; and he put his own droves apart, and did not put them with Laban’s flock.
text.Rotherham: The rams also, did Jacob separate, and then set the faces of the flocks towards the ring-straked and all the dark-coloured, among the flocks of Laban,—and he put his own droves by themselves, and put them not with the flocks of Laban.

text.NRSV: Whenever the stronger of the flock were breeding, Jacob laid the rods in the troughs before the eyes of the flock, that they might breed among the rods,
text.Rotherham: So it came to pass whensoever the stronger of the flocks were in heat, that Jacob set the rods before the eyes of the flocks in the channels,—that the females might be in heat among the rods;

text.NRSV: but for the feebler of the flock he did not lay them there; so the feebler were Laban’s, and the stronger Jacob’s.
text.Rotherham: but, when the flocks were late in bearing, he did not set them,—and so the late-born were Laban’s, but the strong ones Jacob’s.

text.NRSV: Thus the man grew exceedingly rich, and had large flocks, and male and female slaves, and camels and donkeys.
text.Rotherham: Thus did the man break forth, exceedingly,—thus came he to have flocks in abundance, and maid-servants, and men-servants and camels and asses.

text.NRSV: Now Jacob heard that the sons of Laban were saying, ‘Jacob has taken all that was our father’s; he has gained all this wealth from what belonged to our father.’
text.Rotherham: Then heard he the words of the sons of Laban, saying, Jacob hath taken away all that belonged to our father; Yea out of what belonged to our father, hath he made all this wealth.

text.NRSV: And Jacob saw that Laban did not regard him as favourably as he did before.
text.Rotherham: And Jacob looked upon the face of Laban,—and lo! it was not with him as afore-time.

text.NRSV: Then the Lord said to Jacob, ‘Return to the land of your ancestors and to your kindred, and I will be with you.’
text.Rotherham: Then said Yahweh unto Jacob, Return unto the land of thy fathers and to thy kindred,—that I may be with thee.

text.NRSV: So Jacob sent and called Rachel and Leah into the field where his flock was,
text.Rotherham: So Jacob sent, and called for Rachel and for Leah, to the field unto his flock;

text.NRSV: and said to them, ‘I see that your father does not regard me as favourably as he did before. But the God of my father has been with me.
text.Rotherham: and said to them, Beholding, am I the face of your father, that it is not towards me as aforetime,—nevertheless, the God of my father, hath been with me;

text.NRSV: You know that I have served your father with all my strength;
text.Rotherham: and, ye, know that with all my vigour, have I served your father.

text.NRSV: yet your father has cheated me and changed my wages ten times, but God did not permit him to harm me.
text.Rotherham: But, your father, hath deceived me, and hath changed my wages ten times,—yet God hath not suffered him to deal harmfully with me.

text.NRSV: If he said, “The speckled shall be your wages”, then all the flock bore speckled; and if he said, “The striped shall be your wages”, then all the flock bore striped.
text.Rotherham: If thus he saith, The speckled, shall be thy wages, then do all the flock bring forth speckled,—But if thus he saith, The ring-straked, shall be thy wages, then do all the flock bring forth ring-straked.

text.NRSV: Thus God has taken away the livestock of your father, and given them to me.
text.Rotherham: So hath God stripped away the herds of your father, and given them to me.

text.NRSV: During the mating of the flock I once had a dream in which I looked up and saw that the male goats that leaped upon the flock were striped, speckled, and mottled.
text.Rotherham: And it came to pass in the season when the flock were in heat, then lifted I mine eyes and beheld in a dream,—and lo! the he-goats that were leaping upon the flock, were ring-straked speckled and dappled.

text.NRSV: Then the angel of God said to me in the dream, “Jacob,” and I said, “Here I am!”
text.Rotherham: And the messenger of God said unto me in a dream, Jacob! and I said, Behold me!

text.NRSV: And he said, “Look up and see that all the goats that leap on the flock are striped, speckled, and mottled; for I have seen all that Laban is doing to you.
text.Rotherham: Then he said Lift, I pray thee, thine eyes and behold All, the he-goats that are leaping upon the flock are ring-straked, speckled, and dappled,—for I have seen all that Laban, has been doing to thee.

text.NRSV: I am the God of Bethel, where you anointed a pillar and made a vow to me. Now leave this land at once and return to the land of your birth.”’
text.Rotherham: I, am the GOD of Beth-el, where thou didst anoint a pillar, where thou didst vow to me a vow,—Now, rise, go forth out of this land, and return unto the land of thy kindred.

text.NRSV: Then Rachel and Leah answered him, ‘Is there any portion or inheritance left to us in our father’s house?
text.Rotherham: Then responded Rachel and Leah, and said to him,—Have we any longer a portion or an inheritance in the house of our father?

text.NRSV: Are we not regarded by him as foreigners? For he has sold us, and he has been using up the money given for us.
text.Rotherham: Are we not accounted, aliens, to him seeing that having sold us, he hath then gone on devouring, our silver?

text.NRSV: All the property that God has taken away from our father belongs to us and to our children; now then, do whatever God has said to you.’
text.Rotherham: Surely all the riches which God hath stripped off from our father, unto us, it belongeth, and unto our sons,—Now, therefore, whatsoever God hath said unto thee, do.

text.NRSV: So Jacob arose, and set his children and his wives on camels;
text.Rotherham: So Jacob arose,—and mounted his sons and his wives on the camels;

text.NRSV: and he drove away all his livestock, all the property that he had gained, the livestock in his possession that he had acquired in Paddan-aram, to go to his father Isaac in the land of Canaan.
text.Rotherham: and drave away all his herds and all his goods which he had gathered, the gains he had gained, which he had gathered in Padan-aram; that he might go in unto Isaac his father unto the land of Canaan.

text.NRSV: Now Laban had gone to shear his sheep, and Rachel stole her father’s household gods.
text.Rotherham: Now, Laban, had gone, to shear his sheep,—so Rachel stole the household gods that belonged to her father,

text.NRSV: And Jacob deceived Laban the Aramean, in that he did not tell him that he intended to flee.
text.Rotherham: Thus Jacob stole away unawares to Laban, the Syrian,—in that he had not told him that he was about to flee.

text.NRSV: So he fled with all that he had; starting out he crossed the Euphrates, and set his face towards the hill country of Gilead.
text.Rotherham: So he fled, he—and all that he had, and he arose, and passed over the River,—and set his face towards the mountain of Gilead.

text.NRSV: On the third day Laban was told that Jacob had fled.
text.Rotherham: And it was told Laban, on the third day,—that Jacob had fled.

text.NRSV: So he took his kinsfolk with him and pursued him for seven days until he caught up with him in the hill country of Gilead.
text.Rotherham: So he took his brethren with him, and pursued after him a journey of seven days—and overtook him in the mountain of Gilead.

text.NRSV: But God came to Laban the Aramean in a dream by night, and said to him, ‘Take heed that you say not a word to Jacob, either good or bad.’
text.Rotherham: And God went in unto Laban the Syrian in a dream of the night,—and said to him Take heed to thyself that thou speak not with Jacob either good or bad.

text.NRSV: Laban overtook Jacob. Now Jacob had pitched his tent in the hill country, and Laban with his kinsfolk camped in the hill country of Gilead.
text.Rotherham: So then Laban came up with Jacob,—Jacob, having pitched his tent in the mountain, Laban, also, with his brethren pitched in the mountain of Gilead.

text.NRSV: Laban said to Jacob, ‘What have you done? You have deceived me, and carried away my daughters like captives of the sword.
text.Rotherham: Then said Laban to Jacob, What hadst thou done, that thou shouldst steal away unawares to me,—and shouldst carry off my daughters, as captives of the sword?

text.NRSV: Why did you flee secretly and deceive me and not tell me? I would have sent you away with mirth and songs, with tambourine and lyre.
text.Rotherham: Wherefore didst thou secretly flee, and rob me,—and didst not tell me, that I might send thee away with rejoicing and with songs, with timbrel and with lyre;

text.NRSV: And why did you not permit me to kiss my sons and my daughters farewell? What you have done is foolish.
text.Rotherham: and didst not suffer me to kiss my sons and my daughters? Now, hast thou done, foolishly.

text.NRSV: It is in my power to do you harm; but the God of your father spoke to me last night, saying, “Take heed that you speak to Jacob neither good nor bad.”
text.Rotherham: It is in the power of my hand, to deal with thee for harm,—but the God of your father, last night, spake unto me, saying, Take heed to thyself that thou speak not with Jacob, either good or bad.

text.NRSV: Even though you had to go because you longed greatly for your father’s house, why did you steal my gods?’
text.Rotherham: But, now, though thou, didst even take thy journey, because thou, didst sorely long, for the house of thy father, wherefore didst thou steal away my gods?

text.NRSV: Jacob answered Laban, ‘Because I was afraid, for I thought that you would take your daughters from me by force.
text.Rotherham: And Jacob answered and said unto Laban,—Because I was afraid, for I said, Lest thou take by force thy daughters from me!

text.NRSV: But anyone with whom you find your gods shall not live. In the presence of our kinsfolk, point out what I have that is yours, and take it.’ Now Jacob did not know that Rachel had stolen the gods.
text.Rotherham: He with whom thou find thy gods, shall not live! Before our brethren, note thou for thyself what is with me and take what is thine. Neither did Jacob know that, Rachel, had stolen them.

text.NRSV: So Laban went into Jacob’s tent, and into Leah’s tent, and into the tent of the two maids, but he did not find them. And he went out of Leah’s tent, and entered Rachel’s.
text.Rotherham: Then entered Laban into the tent of Jacob and into the tent of Leah and into the tent of the two handmaids, and found nothing,—then went he forth out of the tent of Leah, and entered into the tent of Rachel.

text.NRSV: Now Rachel had taken the household gods and put them in the camel’s saddle, and sat on them. Laban felt all about in the tent, but did not find them.
text.Rotherham: Now Rachel, had taken the household gods and put them in the basket-saddle of the camel, and taken her seat upon them. And Laban felt about throughout all the tent, and found them not.

text.NRSV: And she said to her father, ‘Let not my lord be angry that I cannot rise before you, for the way of women is upon me.’ So he searched, but did not find the household gods.
text.Rotherham: And she said unto her father, Let it not be vexing in the eyes of my lord that I cannot rise up at thy presence, for, the way of women, is upon me. So he made search, but found not the household gods,

text.NRSV: Then Jacob became angry, and upbraided Laban. Jacob said to Laban, ‘What is my offence? What is my sin, that you have hotly pursued me?
text.Rotherham: And it was vexing to Jacob and he contended with Laban,—and Jacob responded and said to Laban, What was my trespass, what my sin, that thou shouldst have come burning after me?

text.NRSV: Although you have felt about through all my goods, what have you found of all your household goods? Set it here before my kinsfolk and your kinsfolk, so that they may decide between us two.
text.Rotherham: Now that thou hast felt about among all my goods, what hast thou found of the goods of thine own house? Set it out here, right before my brethren and thy brethren,—that they may give sentence betwixt us two.

text.NRSV: These twenty years I have been with you; your ewes and your female goats have not miscarried, and I have not eaten the rams of your flocks.
text.Rotherham: This twenty years’ time, have, I, been with thee—Thy ewes and thy she-goats, have not cast their young,—And the rams of thy flock, have I not eaten,

text.NRSV: That which was torn by wild beasts I did not bring to you; I bore the loss of it myself; of my hand you required it, whether stolen by day or stolen by night.
text.Rotherham: The torn, have I not brought in unto thee—I bare the loss, At my hand, didst thou require it,—Stolen by day, or stolen by night.

text.NRSV: It was like this with me: by day the heat consumed me, and the cold by night, and my sleep fled from my eyes.
text.Rotherham: Thus have I been—By day, drought hath consumed me, And, frost, by, night,—So that my sleep fled away from mine eyes.

text.NRSV: These twenty years I have been in your house; I served you for fourteen years for your two daughters, and six years for your flock, and you have changed my wages ten times.
text.Rotherham: This, hath been my lot twenty years in thy house,—I served thee fourteen years for thy two daughters, And six years for thy sheep—And thou didst change my wages ten times:—

text.NRSV: If the God of my father, the God of Abraham and the Fear of Isaac, had not been on my side, surely now you would have sent me away empty-handed. God saw my affliction and the labour of my hands, and rebuked you last night.’
text.Rotherham: But that the God of my father—The God of Abraham, and the Dread of Isaac—Proved to be mine, Surely even now, empty, hadst thou let me go? My humiliation and the wearying toil of my hands, God had seen, And therefore gave sentence last night!

text.NRSV: Then Laban answered and said to Jacob, ‘The daughters are my daughters, the children are my children, the flocks are my flocks, and all that you see is mine. But what can I do today about these daughters of mine, or about their children whom they have borne?
text.Rotherham: Then responded Laban and said unto Jacob—the daughters, are my daughters, And the sons, are my sons, And the sheep are my sheep, And, all that, thou, beholdest, To me doth it belong! But to my daughters, what can I do to these this day, Or to their sons whom they have borne?

text.NRSV: Come now, let us make a covenant, you and I; and let it be a witness between you and me.’
text.Rotherham: Now, therefore, come on! Let us solemnise a covenant, I and thou,—So shall it be a witness betwixt me and thee.

text.NRSV: So Jacob took a stone, and set it up as a pillar.
text.Rotherham: So Jacob took a stone,—and set it up as a pillar.

text.NRSV: And Jacob said to his kinsfolk, ‘Gather stones,’ and they took stones, and made a heap; and they ate there by the heap.
text.Rotherham: Then said Jacob to his brethren—Gather stones. And they took stones and made a heap,—and they did eat there, by the heap.

text.NRSV: Laban called it Jegar-sahadutha: but Jacob called it Galeed.
text.Rotherham: And Laban called it, Jegar-sahadutha,—but, Jacob, called it, Galeed.

text.NRSV: Laban said, ‘This heap is a witness between you and me today.’ Therefore he called it Galeed,
text.Rotherham: Then said Laban: This heap, be witness betwixt me, and thee, to-day. For this cause, is the name thereof called Galeed;

text.NRSV: and the pillar Mizpah, for he said, ‘The Lord watch between you and me, when we are absent one from the other.
text.Rotherham: also, The Watch Tower, as to which it is said, Yahweh watch betwixt me and thee, when we are parted one from another.

text.NRSV: If you ill-treat my daughters, or if you take wives in addition to my daughters, though no one else is with us, remember that God is witness between you and me.’
text.Rotherham: If thou oppress my daughters, or if thou take wives besides my daughters,—there may be no man with us—see! God, be witness betwixt me, and thee!

text.NRSV: Then Laban said to Jacob, ‘See this heap and see the pillar, which I have set between you and me.
text.Rotherham: And Laban said to Jacob—Behold this heap, and behold this pillar, which I have set betwixt me, and thee:

text.NRSV: This heap is a witness, and the pillar is a witness, that I will not pass beyond this heap to you, and you will not pass beyond this heap and this pillar to me, for harm.
text.Rotherham: A witness, be this heap, and, a witness, the pillar,—That, I, am not to pass, unto thee, over this heap, And, thou, art not to pass, unto me, over this heap and this pillar for harm.

text.NRSV: May the God of Abraham and the God of Nahor’—the God of their father—‘judge between us.’ So Jacob swore by the Fear of his father Isaac,
text.Rotherham: The Gods of Abraham and the Gods of Nahor judge betwixt us—the Gods of their fathers. And Jacob sware by the Dread of his father Isaac:

text.NRSV: and Jacob offered a sacrifice on the height and called his kinsfolk to eat bread; and they ate bread and tarried all night in the hill country.
text.Rotherham: and Jacob offered sacrifice in the mountain, and called on his brethren to eat bread. So they did eat bread, and tarried the night in the mountain.

text.NRSV: Early in the morning Laban rose up, and kissed his grandchildren and his daughters and blessed them; then he departed and returned home.
text.Rotherham: And Laban rose up early in the morning, and kissed his sons and his daughters, and blessed them: and Laban went his way and returned to his place.

text.NRSV: Jacob went on his way and the angels of God met him;
text.Rotherham: When, Jacob, had gone on his way, there met him, messengers of God.

text.NRSV: and when Jacob saw them he said, ‘This is God’s camp!’ So he called that place Mahanaim.
text.Rotherham: And Jacob said, when he saw them, the camp of God, is this! So he called the name of that place, Mahanaim.

text.NRSV: Jacob sent messengers before him to his brother Esau in the land of Seir, the country of Edom,
text.Rotherham: Then did Jacob send messengers before him unto Esau his brother, to the land of Seir, the field of Edom.

text.NRSV: instructing them, ‘Thus you shall say to my lord Esau: Thus says your servant Jacob, “I have lived with Laban as an alien, and stayed until now;
text.Rotherham: And he commanded them, saying, Thus, shall ye say to my lord to Esau, Thus, saith thy servant Jacob, With Laban, have I sojourned, and tarried until now:

text.NRSV: and I have oxen, donkeys, flocks, male and female slaves; and I have sent to tell my lord, in order that I may find favour in your sight.”’
text.Rotherham: And I have oxen and asses, flocks and men-servants, and maid-servants, So I must needs send to tell my lord, that I might find favour in thine eyes.

text.NRSV: The messengers returned to Jacob, saying, ‘We came to your brother Esau, and he is coming to meet you, and four hundred men are with him.’
text.Rotherham: And the messengers returned unto Jacob, saying,—We came in unto thy brother unto Esau, moreover also he is on his way to meet thee, and four hundred men with him.

text.NRSV: Then Jacob was greatly afraid and distressed; and he divided the people that were with him, and the flocks and herds and camels, into two companies,
text.Rotherham: Then was Jacob greatly afraid, and in distress. So he divided the people that were with him and the flocks and the herds and the camels into two camps,

text.NRSV: thinking, ‘If Esau comes to one company and destroys it, then the company that is left will escape.’
text.Rotherham: And he said. Should Esau come upon the one camp, and smite it, yet shall the camp that is left escape.

text.NRSV: And Jacob said, ‘O God of my father Abraham and God of my father Isaac, O Lord who said to me, “Return to your country and to your kindred, and I will do you good”,
text.Rotherham: And Jacob said, God of my father Abraham, and God of my father Isaac,—Yahweh, who wast saying unto me,—Return to thy land and to thy kindred that I may deal well with thee:

text.NRSV: I am not worthy of the least of all the steadfast love and all the faithfulness that you have shown to your servant, for with only my staff I crossed this Jordan; and now I have become two companies.
text.Rotherham: I am too small for all the lovingkindnesses and for all the faithfulness, which thou hast done unto thy servant,—For with my staff, passed I over this Jordan, But, now, have I become two camps.

text.NRSV: Deliver me, please, from the hand of my brother, from the hand of Esau, for I am afraid of him; he may come and kill us all, the mothers with the children.
text.Rotherham: Deliver me I pray thee out of the hand of my brother out of the hand of Esau, for I, am afraid of him, lest he come in and smite mother as well as sons

text.NRSV: Yet you have said, “I will surely do you good, and make your offspring as the sand of the sea, which cannot be counted because of their number.”’
text.Rotherham: But, thou thyself, saidst,—I will deal well with thee. So will I make thy seed as the sand of the sea, which is not to be counted, for multitude.

text.NRSV: So he spent that night there, and from what he had with him he took a present for his brother Esau,
text.Rotherham: And he tarried there that night, and took of that which came to his hand a present for Esau his brother:

text.NRSV: two hundred female goats and twenty male goats, two hundred ewes and twenty rams,
text.Rotherham: She-goats, two hundred; And he-goats, twenty; Ewes, two hundred; And rams, twenty;

text.NRSV: thirty milch camels and their colts, forty cows and ten bulls, twenty female donkeys and ten male donkeys.
text.Rotherham: Milch camels, with their colts, thirty; Cows, forty; And bulls, ten; She-asses, twenty; And young he asses ten.

text.NRSV: These he delivered into the hand of his servants, each drove by itself, and said to his servants, ‘Pass on ahead of me, and put a space between drove and drove.’
text.Rotherham: And he gave them into the hand of his Servants, each drove by itself, and said unto his servants—Pass over before me, and, a breathing-space, shall ye put betwixt drove and drove.

text.NRSV: He instructed the foremost, ‘When Esau my brother meets you, and asks you, “To whom do you belong? Where are you going? And whose are these ahead of you?”
text.Rotherham: And he commanded the first one saying: When Esau my brother shall fall in with thee, and shall ask thee, saying Whose art thou? and whither wouldst thou go? and to whom belong these before thee?

text.NRSV: then you shall say, “They belong to your servant Jacob; they are a present sent to my lord Esau; and moreover he is behind us.”’
text.Rotherham: then shalt thou say, To thy servant Jacob: It is, a present, sent to my lord, to Esau; And behold he himself also is behind us,

text.NRSV: He likewise instructed the second and the third and all who followed the droves, ‘You shall say the same thing to Esau when you meet him,
text.Rotherham: And he commanded the second also, and the third yea all that were going on behind the droves, saying,—According to this word, shall ye speak unto Esau, when ye find him.

text.NRSV: and you shall say, “Moreover your servant Jacob is behind us.”’ For he thought, ‘I may appease him with the present that goes ahead of me, and afterwards I shall see his face; perhaps he will accept me.’
text.Rotherham: Then shall ye say, Also, lo! thy servant Jacob is behind us. For he said: I must pacify him with the present that goeth on before me. And, after that, will I see his face: Peradventure he will accept me.

text.NRSV: So the present passed on ahead of him; and he himself spent that night in the camp.
text.Rotherham: So the present passed over before him,—whereas he himself tarried that night in the camp.

text.NRSV: The same night he got up and took his two wives, his two maids, and his eleven children, and crossed the ford of the Jabbok.
text.Rotherham: And he arose in that night and took his two wives, and his two handmaids, and his eleven sons,—and passed over the ford of Jabbok.

text.NRSV: He took them and sent them across the stream, and likewise everything that he had.
text.Rotherham: So he took them, and sent them over the brook,—and sent over that which he had.

text.NRSV: Jacob was left alone; and a man wrestled with him until daybreak.
text.Rotherham: And Jacob was left alone,—and there wrestled a man with him, until the uprisings of the dawn.

text.NRSV: When the man saw that he did not prevail against Jacob, he struck him on the hip socket; and Jacob’s hip was put out of joint as he wrestled with him.
text.Rotherham: And when he saw, that he prevailed not against him, he touched the hollow of his thigh,—and the hollow of Jacob’s thigh was put out of joint, as he wrestled with him.

text.NRSV: Then he said, ‘Let me go, for the day is breaking.’ But Jacob said, ‘I will not let you go, unless you bless me.’
text.Rotherham: Then said he—Let me go, for uprisen hath the dawn. And he said: I will not let thee go, except thou have blessed me

text.NRSV: So he said to him, ‘What is your name?’ And he said, ‘Jacob.’
text.Rotherham: Then he said unto him—What is thy name? And he said—Jacob.

text.NRSV: Then the man said, ‘You shall no longer be called Jacob, but Israel, for you have striven with God and with humans, and have prevailed.’
text.Rotherham: Then he said—Not Jacob, shall thy name be called any more, but Israel,—For thou hast contended with God and with men and hast prevailed.

text.NRSV: Then Jacob asked him, ‘Please tell me your name.’ But he said, ‘Why is it that you ask my name?’ And there he blessed him.
text.Rotherham: Then asked Jacob and said, Do, I pray thee, tell me thy name! And he said—Wherefore now, shouldest thou ask for my name? And he blessed him there,

text.NRSV: So Jacob called the place Peniel, saying, ‘For I have seen God face to face, and yet my life is preserved.’
text.Rotherham: So Jacob called the name of the place Peniel; For I saw God face to face; and my soul was delivered,

text.NRSV: The sun rose upon him as he passed Penuel, limping because of his hip.
text.Rotherham: And the sun rose on him as soon as he had passed over Penuel,—he, moreover was halting upon his thigh,

text.NRSV: Therefore to this day the Israelites do not eat the thigh muscle that is on the hip socket, because he struck Jacob on the hip socket at the thigh muscle.
text.Rotherham: For this cause, the sons of Israel eat not of the nerve of the large hip-sinew, which is by the hollow of the thigh, until this day,—because he touched the hollow of the thigh of Jacob, in the nerve of the hip-sinew.

text.NRSV: Now Jacob looked up and saw Esau coming, and four hundred men with him. So he divided the children among Leah and Rachel and the two maids.
text.Rotherham: Then Jacob lifted up his eyes and looked, and lo! Esau, coming in,—and with him, four hundred men. So he divided the children—unto Leah and unto Rachel, and unto the two handmaids;

text.NRSV: He put the maids with their children in front, then Leah with her children, and Rachel and Joseph last of all.
text.Rotherham: and put the handmaids and their children first,—and Leah and her children next, and Rachel and Joseph next;

text.NRSV: He himself went on ahead of them, bowing himself to the ground seven times, until he came near his brother.
text.Rotherham: but he himself, passed over before them, and bowed himself to the earth seven times, until he had come near unto his brother.

text.NRSV: But Esau ran to meet him, and embraced him, and fell on his neck and kissed him, and they wept.
text.Rotherham: Then ran Esau to meet him, and embraced him, and fell on his neck,—and they wept.

text.NRSV: When Esau looked up and saw the women and children, he said, ‘Who are these with you?’ Jacob said, ‘The children whom God has graciously given your servant.’
text.Rotherham: Then lifted he up his eyes, and beheld the women and the children, and said What are these to thee? And he said—The children wherewith God hath favoured thy servant,

text.NRSV: Then the maids drew near, they and their children, and bowed down;
text.Rotherham: Then came near the handmaids, they—and their children,—and bowed themselves.

text.NRSV: Leah likewise and her children drew near and bowed down; and finally Joseph and Rachel drew near, and they bowed down.
text.Rotherham: Then came near Leah also with her children,—and they bowed themselves. And afterwards, came near Joseph with Rachel, and they bowed themselves,

text.NRSV: Esau said, ‘What do you mean by all this company that I met?’ Jacob answered, ‘To find favour with my lord.’
text.Rotherham: Then said he, What to thee is all this camp, which I have fallen in with? And he said,—To find favour in the eyes of my lord.

text.NRSV: But Esau said, ‘I have enough, my brother; keep what you have for yourself.’
text.Rotherham: Then said Esau, I have an abundance,—my brother, be thine what thou hast!

text.NRSV: Jacob said, ‘No, please; if I find favour with you, then accept my present from my hand; for truly to see your face is like seeing the face of God—since you have received me with such favour.
text.Rotherham: Then said Jacob, Nay, I pray thee, if, I pray thee, I have found favour in thine eyes, then thou wilt take my present at my hand,—For on this account, hath my seeing thy face been like seeing the face of God in that thou wast well-pleased with me.

text.NRSV: Please accept my gift that is brought to you, because God has dealt graciously with me, and because I have everything I want.’ So he urged him, and he took it.
text.Rotherham: Take, I pray thee my blessing which hath been brought in to thee, because God hath shewed me favour and because I have everything. So he was urgent with him and he took it.

text.NRSV: Then Esau said, ‘Let us journey on our way, and I will go alongside you.’
text.Rotherham: Then he said, Let us break up and go, and let me go on before thee.

text.NRSV: But Jacob said to him, ‘My lord knows that the children are frail and that the flocks and herds, which are nursing, are a care to me; and if they are overdriven for one day, all the flocks will die.
text.Rotherham: And he said unto him, My lord, is taking note, that, the children are tender,—and the flocks and the herds are giving suck with me,—and, should I overdrive them a single day, then would all the flocks die.

text.NRSV: Let my lord pass on ahead of his servant, and I will lead on slowly, according to the pace of the cattle that are before me and according to the pace of the children, until I come to my lord in Seir.’
text.Rotherham: Let my lord, I pray thee pass over before his servant, and let, me, lead on in my own easy way, at the pace of the cattle that are before me, and at the pace of the children, until that I come in unto my lord, towards Seir.

text.NRSV: So Esau said, ‘Let me leave with you some of the people who are with me.’ But he said, ‘Why should my lord be so kind to me?’
text.Rotherham: Then said Esau: Let me leave, I pray thee, along with thee, some of the people who are with me! And he said—Why so? let me find favour in the eyes of my lord!

text.NRSV: So Esau returned that day on his way to Seir.
text.Rotherham: So Esau returned, that day, to his journey towards Mount Seir.

text.NRSV: But Jacob journeyed to Succoth, and built himself a house, and made booths for his cattle; therefore the place is called Succoth.
text.Rotherham: But, Jacob, brake up towards Succoth, and built for himself a house, for his cattle, made he places of shelter, for which cause, the name of the place was called, Succoth.

text.NRSV: Jacob came safely to the city of Shechem, which is in the land of Canaan, on his way from Paddan-aram; and he camped before the city.
text.Rotherham: And Jacob came in peace to the city of Shechem, which is in the land of Canaan,—when he came in from Padan-aram,—and he encamped before the city.

text.NRSV: And from the sons of Hamor, Shechem’s father, he bought for one hundred pieces of money the plot of land on which he had pitched his tent.
text.Rotherham: And he bought the portion of the field where he had spread out his tent, at the hand of the sons of Hamor, father of Shechem,—for a hundred kesitahs.

text.NRSV: There he erected an altar and called it El-Elohe-Israel.
text.Rotherham: And he set up there an altar,—and called it, El-elohe-Israel.

text.NRSV: Now Dinah the daughter of Leah, whom she had borne to Jacob, went out to visit the women of the region.
text.Rotherham: Then went forth Dinah, the daughter of Leah, whom she had borne to Jacob,—to see the daughters of the land.

text.NRSV: When Shechem son of Hamor the Hivite, prince of the region, saw her, he seized her and lay with her by force.
text.Rotherham: And Shechem, son of Hamor the Hivite, prince of the land, saw her,—so he took her, and lay with her, and humbled her.

text.NRSV: And his soul was drawn to Dinah daughter of Jacob; he loved the girl, and spoke tenderly to her.
text.Rotherham: And his soul clave unto Dinah, daughter of Jacob,—and he loved the young woman, and spake to the heart of the young woman.

text.NRSV: So Shechem spoke to his father Hamor, saying, ‘Get me this girl to be my wife.’
text.Rotherham: Then spake Shechem unto Hamor his father, saying,—Take me this girl to wife!

text.NRSV: Now Jacob heard that Shechem had defiled his daughter Dinah; but his sons were with his cattle in the field, so Jacob held his peace until they came.
text.Rotherham: Now, Jacob, had heard that he had defiled Dinah his daughter, but, his sons, happened to be with his cattle in the field,—so Jacob kept quiet until they came in.

text.NRSV: And Hamor the father of Shechem went out to Jacob to speak with him,
text.Rotherham: Then came forth Hamor, father of Shechem, unto Jacob,—to speak with him.

text.NRSV: just as the sons of Jacob came in from the field. When they heard of it, the men were indignant and very angry, because he had committed an outrage in Israel by lying with Jacob’s daughter, for such a thing ought not to be done.
text.Rotherham: Now the sons of Jacob came in from the field as soon as they heard, and the men were grieved, and it was vexing to them exceedingly,—for, a disgraceful deed, had he done with Israel in lying with Jacob’s daughter, seeing that so, it should not be done.

text.NRSV: But Hamor spoke with them, saying, ‘The heart of my son Shechem longs for your daughter; please give her to him in marriage.
text.Rotherham: So then Hamor spake with them saying—As for Shechem my son, his soul hath become attached to your daughter,—I pray you give her to him, to wife.

text.NRSV: Make marriages with us; give your daughters to us, and take our daughters for yourselves.
text.Rotherham: And exchange ye daughters with us in marriage,—your daughters, shall ye give to us, and our daughters, shall ye take to you.

text.NRSV: You shall live with us; and the land shall be open to you; live and trade in it, and get property in it.’
text.Rotherham: And with us, shall ye dwell,—and, the land, shall be before you, tarry, and go through it, and get possessions therein.

text.NRSV: Shechem also said to her father and to her brothers, ‘Let me find favour with you, and whatever you say to me I will give.
text.Rotherham: Then said Shechem unto her father and unto her brethren, Let me find favour in your eyes,—and, whatsoever ye may say unto me, will I give:

text.NRSV: Put the marriage present and gift as high as you like, and I will give whatever you ask me; only give me the girl to be my wife.’
text.Rotherham: Heap on me, exceedingly, dowry and gift, and I will give, just as ye may say unto me,—but give me the young woman, to wife.

text.NRSV: The sons of Jacob answered Shechem and his father Hamor deceitfully, because he had defiled their sister Dinah.
text.Rotherham: And the sons of Jacob answered Shechem and Hamor his father deceitfully, and spake (because he had defiled Dinah their sister)—

text.NRSV: They said to them, ‘We cannot do this thing, to give our sister to one who is uncircumcised, for that would be a disgrace to us.
text.Rotherham: and said unto them—We cannot do this thing, to give our sister to a man that is uncircumcised,—for a reproach, it would be to us.

text.NRSV: Only on this condition will we consent to you: that you will become as we are and every male among you be circumcised.
text.Rotherham: Only herein, can we consent to you,—If ye will become like us, by circumcising to you every male,

text.NRSV: Then we will give our daughters to you, and we will take your daughters for ourselves, and we will live among you and become one people.
text.Rotherham: then will we give our daughters to you, and your daughters, will we take to us,—and we will dwell with you, and become one people.

text.NRSV: But if you will not listen to us and be circumcised, then we will take our daughter and be gone.’
text.Rotherham: But if ye will not hearken unto us, to be circumcised, then will we take our daughter and be gone.

text.NRSV: Their words pleased Hamor and Hamor’s son Shechem.
text.Rotherham: And their words looked fair in the eyes of Hamor,—and in the eyes of Shechem, Hamor’s son;

text.NRSV: And the young man did not delay to do the thing, because he was delighted with Jacob’s daughter. Now he was the most honoured of all his family.
text.Rotherham: and the young man deferred not to do the thing, because he had delight in Jacob’s daughter,—he, moreover being more honourable than all the house of his father.

text.NRSV: So Hamor and his son Shechem came to the gate of their city and spoke to the men of their city, saying,
text.Rotherham: So Hamor entered with Shechem his son into the gate of their city,—and they spake unto the men of their city, saying:

text.NRSV: ‘These people are friendly with us; let them live in the land and trade in it, for the land is large enough for them; let us take their daughters in marriage, and let us give them our daughters.
text.Rotherham: As for these men, in peace, they are with us—let them therefore dwell in the land and go through it, for, the land, lo! it is roomy on both hands before them,—their daughters, let us take to us for wives, and our daughters, let us give to them.

text.NRSV: Only on this condition will they agree to live among us, to become one people: that every male among us be circumcised as they are circumcised.
text.Rotherham: Only herein, will the men consent to us, to dwell with us, to become one people,—If we circumcise to us every male, even as they are circumcised.

text.NRSV: Will not their livestock, their property, and all their animals be ours? Only let us agree with them, and they will live among us.’
text.Rotherham: Their herds and their substance, and all their beasts, shall they, not be, ours? Only, we must consent to them, that they may dwell with us.

text.NRSV: And all who went out of the city gate heeded Hamor and his son Shechem; and every male was circumcised, all who went out of the gate of his city.
text.Rotherham: And they hearkened unto Hamor, and unto Shechem his son, even all that were going forth out of the gate of his city,—and every male was circumcised, all that were going forth out of the gate of his city.

text.NRSV: On the third day, when they were still in pain, two of the sons of Jacob, Simeon and Levi, Dinah’s brothers, took their swords and came against the city unawares, and killed all the males.
text.Rotherham: And it came to pass on the third day, when they were in pain, that two of the sons of Jacob Simeon and Levi, Dinah’s brothers, took each man his sword, and came in upon the city, boldly,—and slew every, male;

text.NRSV: They killed Hamor and his son Shechem with the sword, and took Dinah out of Shechem’s house, and went away.
text.Rotherham: Hamor also, and Shechem his son, slew they with the edge of the sword,—and took Dinah out of the house of Shechem, and came forth,

text.NRSV: And the other sons of Jacob came upon the slain, and plundered the city, because their sister had been defiled.
text.Rotherham: The sons of Jacob, came in upon the slain and plundered the city,—because they had defiled their sister:

text.NRSV: They took their flocks and their herds, their donkeys, and whatever was in the city and in the field.
text.Rotherham: their flocks, and their herds and their asses,—and that which was in the city, and that which was in the field, they took;

text.NRSV: All their wealth, all their little ones and their wives, all that was in the houses, they captured and made their prey.
text.Rotherham: and all their wealth and all their little ones, and their women, took they captive, and seized as plunder,—even all that was in their houses.

text.NRSV: Then Jacob said to Simeon and Levi, ‘You have brought trouble on me by making me odious to the inhabitants of the land, the Canaanites and the Perizzites; my numbers are few, and if they gather themselves against me and attack me, I shall be destroyed, both I and my household.’
text.Rotherham: Then said Jacob unto Simeon and unto Levi—Ye have troubled me by making me odious among them that dwell in the land, among the Canaanites, and among the Perizzites,—I, having only men that may be counted, they will gather themselves, together against me and smite me,—and I shall be destroyed both I and my house.

text.NRSV: But they said, ‘Should our sister be treated like a whore?’
text.Rotherham: And they said,—As with a harlot, was he to deal with our sister.

text.NRSV: God said to Jacob, ‘Arise, go up to Bethel, and settle there. Make an altar there to the God who appeared to you when you fled from your brother Esau.’
text.Rotherham: Then said God unto Jacob, Arise, go up to Bethel, and dwell there,—and make there an altar, to the GOD who appeared unto thee, when thou fleddest from the face of Esau thy brother.

text.NRSV: So Jacob said to his household and to all who were with him, ‘Put away the foreign gods that are among you, and purify yourselves, and change your clothes;
text.Rotherham: And Jacob said unto his house, and unto all who were with him,—Put away the gods of the alien which are in your midst, and purify yourselves, and change your garments;

text.NRSV: then come, let us go up to Bethel, so that I may make an altar there to the God who answered me in the day of my distress and has been with me wherever I have gone.”
text.Rotherham: And let us arise, and go up to Beth-el,—that I may make there an altar to the GOD who answered me in the day of my distress, and was with me in the way that I went.

text.NRSV: So they gave to Jacob all the foreign gods they had, and the rings that were in their ears; and Jacob hid them under the sacred tree near Shechem.
text.Rotherham: So they gave unto Jacob all the gods of the alien which were in their hand, and the earrings which were in their ears,—and Jacob hid them under the oak, which was by Shechem.

text.NRSV: And they journeyed; and the terror of God fell upon the cities that were around them, so that they did not pursue the sons of Jacob.
text.Rotherham: And they brake up,—and it came to pass that the terror of God, was on the cities which were round about them, so that they pursued not after the sons of Jacob.

text.NRSV: Jacob came to Luz (that is, Bethel), which is in the land of Canaan, he and all the people who were with him,
text.Rotherham: And Jacob came in towards Luz, which was in the land of Canaan, the same, is Beth-el,—he and all the people who were with him.

text.NRSV: and there he built an altar and called the place El-bethel, because it was there that God had revealed himself to him when he fled from his brother.
text.Rotherham: And he built there an altar, and called the place El-Beth-el,—because, there, God revealed himself unto him, when he fled from the face of his brother,

text.NRSV: And Deborah, Rebekah’s nurse, died, and she was buried under an oak below Bethel. So it was called Allon-bacuth.
text.Rotherham: And Deborah, Rebekah’s nurse died, and was buried below Beth-el, under the oak,—so the name thereof was called, Allon-bacuth.

text.NRSV: God appeared to Jacob again when he came from Paddan-aram, and he blessed him.
text.Rotherham: And God appeared unto Jacob again, when he came in from Padan-aram,—and blessed him.

text.NRSV: God said to him, “Your name is Jacob; no longer shall you be called Jacob, but Israel shall be your name.” So he was called Israel.
text.Rotherham: And God said to him, Thy name, is Jacob,—Thy name shall no longer be called Jacob But, Israel, shall be thy name. So he called his name, Israel.

text.NRSV: God said to him, “I am God Almighty: be fruitful and multiply; a nation and a company of nations shall come from you, and kings shall spring from you.
text.Rotherham: And God said to him—I, am GOD Almighty! Be fruitful and multiply, A nation, and a multitude of nations shall spring from thee,—And, kings, from thy loins, shall come forth;

text.NRSV: The land that I gave to Abraham and Isaac I will give to you; and I will give the land to your offspring after you.”
text.Rotherham: And, the land which I gave to Abraham and to Isaac, To thee, will I give it,—And to thy seed after thee, will I give the land.

text.NRSV: Then God went up from him at the place where he had spoken with him.
text.Rotherham: And God went up from him at the place where he had spoken with him.

text.NRSV: Jacob set up a pillar in the place where he had spoken with him, a pillar of stone; and he poured out a drink offering on it, and poured oil on it.
text.Rotherham: And Jacob set up a pillar in the place where he had spoken with him, a pillar of stone,—and he poured out thereon a drink-offering, and poured thereon oil.

text.NRSV: So Jacob called the place where God had spoken with him Bethel.
text.Rotherham: And Jacob called the name of the place where God had spoken with him, Beth-el.

text.NRSV: Then they journeyed from Bethel; and when they were still some distance from Ephrath, Rachel was in childbirth, and she had hard labour.
text.Rotherham: Then brake they up from Beth-el, and it came to pass when there was yet a stretch of country, to enter into Ephrath, that Rachel was in childbirth, and had hard-labour in her child-birth.

text.NRSV: When she was in her hard labour, the midwife said to her, “Do not be afraid; for now you will have another son.”
text.Rotherham: So it came to pass when she was in hard-labour in her child-birth, that the midwife said to her—Do not fear, for this also of thine, is, a son.

text.NRSV: As her soul was departing (for she died), she named him Ben-oni; but his father called him Benjamin.
text.Rotherham: And it came to pass when her soul was going forth—for she died, that she called his name Ben-oni,—but, his father, called him, Ben-jamin.

text.NRSV: So Rachel died, and she was buried on the way to Ephrath (that is, Bethlehem),
text.Rotherham: So Rachel died,—and was buried on the way to Ephrath, the same, is Bethlehem.

text.NRSV: Jacob set up a pillar over her tomb; it is the pillar of Rachel’s tomb, which is there to this day.
text.Rotherham: And Jacob set up a pillar, over her grave,—the same, is—The Pillar of the Grave of Rachel until this day.

text.NRSV: Israel journeyed on, and pitched his tent beyond the tower of Eder.
text.Rotherham: Then Israel brake up,—and spread his tent at a distance from Migdal Eder.

text.NRSV: While Israel lived in that land, Reuben went and lay with Bilhah his father’s concubine; and Israel heard of it. Now the sons of Jacob were twelve.
text.Rotherham: And it came to pass while Israel inhabited that land, that Reuben went and lay with Bilhah his father’s concubine; and Israel heard of it. And so the Sons of Israel came to be Twelve.

text.NRSV: The sons of Leah: Reuben (Jacob’s firstborn), Simeon, Levi, Judah, Issachar, and Zebulun.
text.Rotherham: The sons of Leah, the firstborn of Jacob, Reuben,—And Simeon and Levi and Judah, and Issachar and Zebulon:

text.NRSV: The sons of Rachel: Joseph and Benjamin.
text.Rotherham: The sons of Rachel, Joseph and Benjamin:

text.NRSV: The sons of Bilhah, Rachel’s maid: Dan and Naphtali.
text.Rotherham: And the sons of Bilhah, handmaid of Rachel, Dan, and Naphtali:

text.NRSV: The sons of Zilpah, Leah’s maid: Gad and Asher. These were the sons of Jacob who were born to him in Paddan-aram.
text.Rotherham: And the sons of Zilpah, handmaid of Leah, Gad and Asher: These, are the sons of Jacob, who were born to him, in Padan-aram.

text.NRSV: Jacob came to his father Isaac at Mamre, or Kiriath-arba (that is, Hebron), where Abraham and Isaac had resided as aliens.
text.Rotherham: Then came Jacob unto Isaac his father, to Mamre, the city of Arba,—the same, is Hebron, where had sojourned Abraham and Isaac.

text.NRSV: Now the days of Isaac were one hundred eighty years.
text.Rotherham: And the days of Isaac came to be—a hundred and eighty years.

text.NRSV: And Isaac breathed his last; he died and was gathered to his people, old and full of days; and his sons Esau and Jacob buried him.
text.Rotherham: And Isaac breathed his last, and died and was added unto his people, old, and satisfied with days,—and Esau and Jacob his sons buried him.

text.NRSV: These are the descendants of Esau (that is, Edom).
text.Rotherham: Now these, are the generations of Esau the same, is Edom.

text.NRSV: Esau took his wives from the Canaanites: Adah daughter of Elon the Hittite, Oholibamah daughter of Anah son of Zibeon the Hivite,
text.Rotherham: Esau, took his wives of the daughters of Canaan,—Even Adah, daughter of Elon, the Hittite, And Oholibamah daughter of Anah, son of Zibeon, the Hivite;

text.NRSV: and Basemath, Ishmael’s daughter, sister of Nebaioth.
text.Rotherham: And Basemath daughter of Ishmael sister of Nebaioth.

text.NRSV: Adah bore Eliphaz to Esau; Basemath bore Reuel;
text.Rotherham: So Adah bare to Esau, Eliphaz, and, Basemath, bare Reuel;

text.NRSV: and Oholibamah bore Jeush, Jalam, and Korah. These are the sons of Esau who were born to him in the land of Canaan.
text.Rotherham: and Oholibamah, bare Jeush and Jalam and Korah. These, are the sons of Esau, who were born to him in the land of Canaan.

text.NRSV: Then Esau took his wives, his sons, his daughters, all the members of his household, his livestock, all his animals, and all the property he had acquired in the land of Canaan; and he moved far away from his brother Jacob.
text.Rotherham: Then took Esau his wives, and his sons, and his daughters and all the souls of his house, and his herds and all his beasts, and all his possessions whatsoever he had gathered in the land of Canaan,—and went into a land, away from the face of Jacob his brother,

text.NRSV: For their possessions were too great for them to live together; the land where they were staying could not support them because of their livestock.
text.Rotherham: For their substance had become too great for them to dwell together,—neither could the land of their sojournings sustain them, because of their herds,

text.NRSV: So Esau settled in the hill country of Seir; Esau is Edom.
text.Rotherham: So Esau dwelt in Mount Seir, Esau, is Edom.

text.NRSV: These are the descendants of Esau, the ancestor of the Edomites, in the hill country of Seir.
text.Rotherham: And, these, are the generations of Esau father of Edom,—in Mount Seir.

text.NRSV: These are the names of Esau’s sons: Eliphaz son of Adah the wife of Esau, Reuel son of Basemath the wife of Esau.
text.Rotherham: These, are the name of the sons of Esau,—Eliphaz son of Adah wife of Esau, Reuel son of Basemath wife of Esau.

text.NRSV: The sons of Eliphaz were Teman, Omar, Zepho, Gatam, and Kenaz.
text.Rotherham: Then came the sons of Eliphaz to be,—Teman, Omar, Zepho, and Gatam, and Kenaz.

text.NRSV: (Timna was a concubine of Eliphaz son of Esau; she bore Amalek to Eliphaz.) These are the sons of Adah Esau’s wife.
text.Rotherham: Now, Timna, became the concubine of Eliphaz son of Esau, and she bare to Eliphaz Amalek. These, are the sons of Adah, wife of Esau.

text.NRSV: These are the sons of Reuel: Nahath, Zerah, Shammah, and Mizzah. These are the sons of Basemath Esau’s wife.
text.Rotherham: And, these, are the sons of Reuel, Nahath and Zerah Shammah and Mizzah,—These were the sons of Basemath wife of Esau.

text.NRSV: These are the sons of Oholibamah daughter of Anah son of Zibeon Esau’s wife; she bore to Esau: Jeush, Jalam, and Korah.
text.Rotherham: And, these, were the sons of Oholibamah daughter of Anah, son of Zibeon, wife of—Esau,—she bare to Esau Jeush, and Jalam, and Korah.

text.NRSV: These are the clans of the sons of Esau. The sons of Eliphaz the firstborn of Esau: the clans of Teman, Omar, Zepho, Kenaz,
text.Rotherham: These, are the chiefs of the sons of Esau:—The sons of Eliphaz, firstborn of Esau,—Chief Teman, chief Omar, chief Zepho, chief Kenaz,

text.NRSV: Korah, Gatam, and Amalek; these are the clans of Eliphaz in the land of Edom; they are the sons of Adah.
text.Rotherham: chief Korah, chief Gatam, chief Amalek. These are the chiefs of Eliphaz in the land of Edom, these, the sons of Adah.

text.NRSV: These are the sons of Reuel son of Esau: the clans of Nahath, Zerah, Shammah, and Mizzah; these are the clans of Reuel in the land of Edom; they are the sons of Basemath Esau’s wife.
text.Rotherham: And these, are the sons of Reuel, son of Esau—Chief Nahath, chief Zerah, chief Shammah, chief Mizzah,—These are the chiefs of Reuel, in the land of Edom, these, the sons of Basemath wife of Esau.

text.NRSV: These are the sons of Oholibamah Esau’s wife: the clans of Jeush and Jalam and Korah; these are the clans of Oholibamah daughter of Anah, Esau’s wife.
text.Rotherham: And these, are the sons of Oholibamah wife of Esau, Chief Jeush chief Jalam, chief Korah,—These are the chiefs of Oholibamah daughter of Anah wife of Esau.

text.NRSV: These are the sons of Esau (that is, Edom), and these are their clans.
text.Rotherham: These, are the sons of Esau, and, these, their chiefs—the same, is Edom.

text.NRSV: These are the sons of Seir the Horite, the inhabitants of the land: Lotan, Shobal, Zibeon, Anah,
text.Rotherham: These, are the sons of Seir, the Horite, who were dwelling in the land,—Lotan and Shobal and Zibeon and Anah;

text.NRSV: Dishon, Ezer, and Dishan; these are the clans of the Horites, the sons of Seir in the land of Edom.
text.Rotherham: and Dishon and Ezer and Dishan,—These, are the chiefs of the Horites the sons of Seir, in the land of Edom.

text.NRSV: The sons of Lotan were Hori and Hemam; and Lotan’s sister was Timna.
text.Rotherham: Then came the sons of Lotan to be, Hori and Hemam,—And, the sister of Lotan was Timna.

text.NRSV: These are the sons of Shobal: Alvan, Manahath, Ebal, Shepho, and Onam.
text.Rotherham: And, these, are the sons of Shobal, Alvan and Manahath, and Ebal,—Shepho, and Onam.

text.NRSV: These are the sons of Zibeon: Aiah and Anah; he is the Anah who found the springs in the wilderness while he was pasturing the donkeys of his father Zibeon.
text.Rotherham: And, these, are the sons of Zibeon: Both Aiah and Anah,—The same, is the Anah that found the warm springs in the desert, as he fed the asses of Zibeon his father.

text.NRSV: These are the children of Anah: Dishon and Oholibamah daughter of Anah.
text.Rotherham: And these are the sons of Anah Dishon,—and Oholibamah, is daughter of Anah.

text.NRSV: These are the sons of Dishon: Hemdan, Eshban, Ithran, and Cheran.
text.Rotherham: And, these, are the sons of Dishon,—Hemdan and Eshban, and Ithran and Cheran.

text.NRSV: These are the sons of Ezer: Bilhan, Zaavan, and Akan.
text.Rotherham: These, are the sons of Ezer,—Bilhan and Zaavan, and Akan.

text.NRSV: These are the sons of Dishan: Uz and Aran.
text.Rotherham: These, are the sons of Dishan,—Uz and Aran.

text.NRSV: These are the clans of the Horites: the clans of Lotan, Shobal, Zibeon, Anah,
text.Rotherham: These, are the chiefs of the Horites,—Chief Lotan, chief Shobal, chief Zibeon, chief Anah;

text.NRSV: Dishon, Ezer, and Dishan; these are the clans of the Horites, clan by clan in the land of Seir.
text.Rotherham: chief Dishon, chief Ezer, chief Dishan. These, are the chiefs of the Horites, after their chiefs in the land of Seir.

text.NRSV: These are the kings who reigned in the land of Edom, before any king reigned over the Israelites.
text.Rotherham: And, these, are the kings, who reigned in the land of Edom,—before there reigned a king of the sons of Israel:

text.NRSV: Bela son of Beor reigned in Edom; the name of his city was Dinhabah.
text.Rotherham: There reigned in Edom, Bela, son of Beor,—and the name of his city, was Dinhabah.

text.NRSV: Bela died, and Jobab son of Zerah of Bozrah succeeded him as king.
text.Rotherham: Then died Bela,—and there reigned in his stead, Jobab son of Zerah, from Bozrah.

text.NRSV: Jobab died, and Husham of the land of the Temanites succeeded him as king.
text.Rotherham: Then died Jobab,—and there reigned in his stead, Husham, from the land of the Temanites.

text.NRSV: Husham died, and Hadad son of Bedad succeeded him as king; he defeated Midian in the country of Moab; the name of his city was Avith.
text.Rotherham: Then died Husham,—and there reigned in his stead, Hadad, son of Bedad, who smote Midian in the field of Moab, and, the name of his city, was Avith.

text.NRSV: Hadad died, and Samlah of Masrekah succeeded him as king.
text.Rotherham: Then died Hadad,—and there reigned in his stead, Samlah from Masrekah.

text.NRSV: Samlah died, and Shaul of Rehoboth on the Euphrates succeeded him as king.
text.Rotherham: Then died Samlah,—and there reigned in his stead, Shaul, from Rohoboth of the River.

text.NRSV: Shaul died, and Baal-hanan son of Achbor succeeded him as king.
text.Rotherham: Then died Shaul,—and there reigned in his stead, Baal-hanan, son of Achbor.

text.NRSV: Baal-hanan son of Achbor died, and Hadar succeeded him as king; the name of his city was Pau; his wife’s name was Mehetabel daughter of Matred daughter of Mezahab.
text.Rotherham: Then died Baal-hanan son of Achbor, and there reigned in his stead Hadar, and the name of his city, was Pau,—and, the name of his wife, Mehetabel, daughter of Matred, daughter of Me-zahab.

text.NRSV: These are the names of the clans of Esau, according to their families and their regions, by their names: the clans of Timna, Alvah, Jetheth,
text.Rotherham: These then are the names of the chiefs of Esau after their families, after their places by their names,—Chief Timnah, chief Alvah chief Jetheth;

text.NRSV: Oholibamah, Elah, Pinon,
text.Rotherham: chief Oholibamah, chief Elah, chief Pinon;

text.NRSV: Kenaz, Teman, Mibzar,
text.Rotherham: chief Kenaz, chief Teman, chief Mibzar;

text.NRSV: Magdiel, and Iram; these are the clans of Edom (that is, Esau, the ancestor of Edom), according to their settlements in the land they possessed.
text.Rotherham: chief Magdiel, chief Iram. These, are the chiefs of Edom—after their dwellings in the land of their possession, the same, is Esau, father of Edom.

text.NRSV: Jacob settled in the land where his father had lived as an alien, the land of Canaan.
text.Rotherham: So Jacob dwelt in the land of the sojournings of his father,—in the land of Canaan.

text.NRSV: This is the story of the family of Jacob. Joseph, being seventeen years old, was shepherding the flock with his brothers; he was a helper to the sons of Bilhah and Zilpah, his father’s wives; and Joseph brought a bad report of them to their father.
text.Rotherham: These, are the generations of Jacob—Joseph, when seventeen years old, was shepherding with his brethren among the flocks, and, he, being a youth, was with the sons of Bilhah and with the sons of Zilpah wives of his father,—so then Joseph brought in the talk about them—something bad, unto their father.

text.NRSV: Now Israel loved Joseph more than any other of his children, because he was the son of his old age; and he had made him a long robe with sleeves.
text.Rotherham: Now, Israel, loved Joseph more than any of his sons, because he was to him, the son of his old age, and he had made him, a long tunic.

text.NRSV: But when his brothers saw that their father loved him more than all his brothers, they hated him, and could not speak peaceably to him.
text.Rotherham: So his brethren saw that their father loved him, more than any of his brethren, and they hated him, and could not bid him prosper,

text.NRSV: Once Joseph had a dream, and when he told it to his brothers, they hated him even more.
text.Rotherham: And Joseph dreamed a dream, and told it to his brethren, and they went on yet more to hate him.

text.NRSV: He said to them, “Listen to this dream that I dreamed.
text.Rotherham: And he said unto them, Hear ye I pray you, this dream which I have dreamed:

text.NRSV: There we were, binding sheaves in the field, and lo and behold, my sheaf rose and stood upright; then your sheaves gathered around it, and bowed down to my sheaf.”
text.Rotherham: Lo! then, we, were binding sheaves in the midst of the field, when lo my sheaf rose up, yea and took its stand,—and lo! round about came your sheaves, and bowed themselves down to my sheaf.

text.NRSV: His brothers said to him, “Are you indeed to reign over us? Are you indeed to have dominion over us?” So they hated him even more because of his dreams and his words.
text.Rotherham: And his brethren said to him, Shalt thou, reign, over us, shalt thou, have dominion over us? So they went on yet more to hate him, because of his dreams and because of his words.

text.NRSV: He had another dream, and told it to his brothers, saying, “Look, I have had another dream: the sun, the moon, and eleven stars were bowing down to me.”
text.Rotherham: Then dreamed he, yet another, dream, and related it to his brethren, and said:—Lo! I have dreamed a dream, yet again, Lo! then, the sun and the moon, and eleven stars, were bowing themselves down to me.

text.NRSV: But when he told it to his father and to his brothers, his father rebuked him, and said to him, “What kind of dream is this that you have had? Shall we indeed come, I and your mother and your brothers, and bow to the ground before you?”
text.Rotherham: So he related it unto his father, and unto his brethren,—and his father rebuked him, and said unto him, What is this dream which thou hast dreamed? Shall we, indeed come in, I, and thy mother and thy brethren, to bow ourselves down to thee to the earth?

text.NRSV: So his brothers were jealous of him; but his father kept the matter in mind.
text.Rotherham: Then were his brethren jealous of him, but his father, marked the word.

text.NRSV: Now his brothers went to pasture their father’s flock near Shechem.
text.Rotherham: Now his brethren went their way,—to feed their father’s flock in Shechem.

text.NRSV: And Israel said to Joseph, “Are not your brothers pasturing the flock at Shechem? Come, I will send you to them.” He answered, “Here I am”;
text.Rotherham: So Israel said unto Joseph Are not, thy brethren, feeding the flock in Shechem? Come on! and let me send thee unto them. And he said to him Behold me!

text.NRSV: So he said to him, “Go now, see if it is well with your brothers and with the flock; and bring word back to me.” So he sent him from the valley of Hebron. He came to Shechem,
text.Rotherham: So he said to him—Go, I pray thee, look after the welfare of thy brethren, and the welfare of the flock, and bring me back word. And he sent him from the vale of Hebron, and he came in towards Shechem.

text.NRSV: and a man found him wandering in the fields; the man asked him, “What are you seeking?”
text.Rotherham: And a man found him, and lo! he was wandering about in the field, so the man asked him saying—What seekest thou?

text.NRSV: “I am seeking my brothers,” he said; “tell me, please, where they are pasturing the flock.”
text.Rotherham: And he said, My brethren, am, I, seeking,—do tell me, I pray thee, where they are feeding their flock.

text.NRSV: The man said, “They have gone away, for I heard them say, ‘Let us go to Dothan.’” So Joseph went after his brothers, and found them at Dothan.
text.Rotherham: And the man said, They have broken up from hence, for I heard them saying Let us go our way towards Dothan. So Joseph went after his brethren, and found them, in Dothan.

text.NRSV: They saw him from a distance, and before he came near to them, they conspired to kill him.
text.Rotherham: And they saw him afar off,—and, ere yet he drew near unto them, they conspired against him, to put him to death.

text.NRSV: They said to one another, “Here comes this dreamer.
text.Rotherham: And they said each man unto his brother, Lo! that master of dreams yonder, coming in!

text.NRSV: Come now, let us kill him and throw him into one of the pits; then we shall say that a wild animal has devoured him, and we shall see what will become of his dreams.”
text.Rotherham: Now, therefore, come! let us slay him and cast him into one of the pits, and we will say A cruel beast, hath devoured him,—And let us see what will become of his dreams.

text.NRSV: But when Reuben heard it, he delivered him from them, saying, “Let us not take his life.”
text.Rotherham: And Reuben heard it, and rescued him out of their hand, and said Let us not smite him, so as to take his life!

text.NRSV: Reuben said to them, “Shed no blood; throw him into this pit here in the wilderness, but lay no hand on him”—aiming to rescue him from them and to restore him to his father.
text.Rotherham: And Reuben said unto them Do not shed blood! Cast him into this pit, which is in the wilderness, but put not forth, a hand, against him! that he might rescue him out of their hand, to restore him unto his father.

text.NRSV: So when Joseph came to his brothers, they stripped him of his robe, the long robe with sleeves that he wore;
text.Rotherham: So it came to pass, when Joseph had come in unto his brethren, that they stript Joseph of his tunic, the long tunic which was upon him,

text.NRSV: and they took him and threw him into a pit. The pit was empty; there was no water in it.
text.Rotherham: and took him, and cast him into the pit, but the pit, was empty, there was in it no water.

text.NRSV: Then they sat down to eat; and looking up they saw a caravan of Ishmaelites coming from Gilead, with their camels carrying gum, balm, and resin, on their way to carry it down to Egypt.
text.Rotherham: And when they had sat down to eat bread, they lifted up their eyes and looked, and lo! a caravan of Ishmaelites, coming in from Gilead,—and, their camels, were bearing tragacanth gum, and balsam and cistus-gum, they were going their way, to take them down to Egypt.

text.NRSV: Judah said to his brothers, ‘What profit is it if we kill our brother and conceal his blood?
text.Rotherham: So Judah said unto his brethren,—What profit that we slay our brother, and conceal his blood?

text.NRSV: Come, let us sell him to the Ishmaelites, and not lay our hands on him, for he is our brother, our own flesh.’ And his brothers agreed.
text.Rotherham: Come and let us sell him to the Ishmaelites; but let not, our own hand, be upon him, for our own brother, our own flesh, is he And his brethren hearkened.

text.NRSV: When some Midianite traders passed by, they drew Joseph up, lifting him out of the pit, and sold him to the Ishmaelites for twenty pieces of silver. And they took Joseph to Egypt.
text.Rotherham: And there passed by certain Midianites travelling merchants, so they drew forth and uplifted Joseph out of the pit, and sold Joseph to the Ishmaelites, for twenty pieces of silver,—and they brought Joseph into Egypt.

text.NRSV: When Reuben returned to the pit and saw that Joseph was not in the pit, he tore his clothes.
text.Rotherham: And Reuben returned unto the pit, and lo Joseph was not in the pit, so he rent his clothes;

text.NRSV: He returned to his brothers, and said, ‘The boy is gone; and I, where can I turn?’
text.Rotherham: and returned unto his brethren, and said,—The, lad, is not! And, I, oh where can I, go?

text.NRSV: Then they took Joseph’s robe, slaughtered a goat, and dipped the robe in the blood.
text.Rotherham: And they took Joseph’s tunic,—and slaughtered a buck of the goats, and dipped the tunic in the blood;

text.NRSV: They had the long robe with sleeves taken to their father, and they said, ‘This we have found; see now whether it is your son’s robe or not.’
text.Rotherham: and sent the long tunic and brought it in unto their father, and said This, have we found! Examine, we pray thee, whether it is the tunic of thy son, or not!

text.NRSV: His father recognized it, and said, ‘It is my son’s robe! A wild animal has devoured him; Joseph is without doubt torn to pieces.’
text.Rotherham: So he examined it, and said—The tunic of my son! A cruel beast hath devoured him,—torn in pieces—torn in pieces, is Joseph!

text.NRSV: Then Jacob tore his garments, and put sackcloth on his loins, and mourned for his son many days.
text.Rotherham: And Jacob rent his garments, and put sackcloth upon his loins,—and mourned over his son many days.

text.NRSV: All his sons and all his daughters sought to comfort him; but he refused to be comforted and said, ‘No, I shall go down to Sheol to my son, mourning.’ Thus his father bewailed him.
text.Rotherham: And all his sons and all his daughters rose up to console him but he refused to be consoled, and said—Surely I will go down unto my son mourning to hades! And his father wept for him.

text.NRSV: Meanwhile the Midianites had sold him in Egypt to Potiphar, one of Pharaoh’s officials, the captain of the guard.
text.Rotherham: Now, the Midianites, sold him into Egypt,—to Potiphar courtier of Pharaoh, chief of the royal executioners.

text.NRSV: It happened at that time that Judah went down from his brothers and turned aside to a certain Adullamite, whose name was Hirah.
text.Rotherham: And it came to pass at that time, that Judah went down away from his brethren,—and turned aside unto a certain Adullamite, whose, name, was Hirah.

text.NRSV: There Judah saw the daughter of a certain Canaanite whose name was Shua; he married her and went in to her.
text.Rotherham: And Judah saw there a daughter of a certain Canaanite whose, name, was Shua,—and he took her, and went in unto her;

text.NRSV: She conceived and bore a son; and he named him Er.
text.Rotherham: and she conceived and bare a son,—and he called his name Er.

text.NRSV: Again she conceived and bore a son, whom she named Onan.
text.Rotherham: And she conceived again and bare a son,—and she called his name Onan.

text.NRSV: Again she bore a son, and she named him Shelah. He was at Chezib when she bore him.
text.Rotherham: And she, yet again, bare a son, and called his name Shelah,—and she, was at Chezib, when she bare him.

text.NRSV: Judah took a wife for Er his firstborn; and her name was Tamar.
text.Rotherham: And Judah took a wife, for Er his firstborn,—and, her name, was Tamar.

text.NRSV: But Er, Judah’s firstborn, was wicked in the sight of the Lord; and the Lord put him to death.
text.Rotherham: And it came to pass that Er, Judah’s firstborn was displeasing in the eyes of Yahweh,—so Yahweh put him to death,

text.NRSV: Then Judah said to Onan, ‘Go in to your brother’s wife and perform the duty of a brother-in-law to her; raise up offspring for your brother.’
text.Rotherham: Then said Judah to Onan, Go in unto thy brother’s wife, and fulfil the duty of a brother-in-law unto her,—and raise up seed, to thy brother.

text.NRSV: But since Onan knew that the offspring would not be his, he spilled his semen on the ground whenever he went in to his brother’s wife, so that he would not give offspring to his brother.
text.Rotherham: And Onan knew that the seed would not be his,—and so although he did go in unto his brother’s wife, yet made he waste upon the ground, so as not to give seed to his brother.

text.NRSV: What he did was displeasing in the sight of the Lord, and he put him to death also.
text.Rotherham: And it was displeasing, in the eyes of Yahweh what he had done,—so he put, him also, to death,

text.NRSV: Then Judah said to his daughter-in-law Tamar, ‘Remain a widow in your father’s house until my son Shelah grows up’; for he feared that he too would die, like his brothers. So Tamar went to live in her father’s house.
text.Rotherham: Then said Judah to Tamar his daughter-in-law—Remain a widow in the house of thy father until Shelah my son grow up. For he said, Lest, he also, die, like his brethren. So Tamar went her way, and remained in the house of her father.

text.NRSV: In course of time the wife of Judah, Shua’s daughter, died; when Judah was consoled, he went up to Timnah to his sheepshearers, he and his friend Hirah the Adullamite.
text.Rotherham: And when the days were multiplied, then died Shua’s daughter, Judah’s wife,—and Judah consoled himself and went up unto his sheep shearers, he and Hirah his friend the Adullamite, towards Timnah.

text.NRSV: When Tamar was told, ‘Your father-in-law is going up to Timnah to shear his sheep’,
text.Rotherham: And it was told Tamar saying,—Lo! thy father-in-law is going up towards Timnah, to the shearing of his sheep.

text.NRSV: she put off her widow’s garments, put on a veil to disguise herself, and sat down at the entrance to Enaim, which is on the road to Timnah. For she saw that Shelah was grown up, and she had not been given to him in marriage.
text.Rotherham: So she put off from her the garments of her widowhood and covered herself with a veil and wrapped herself up, and sat down in the entrance of, Enaim, which is by the way towards Timnah,—for she saw that Shelah had grown up, and she, had not been given him to wife.

text.NRSV: When Judah saw her, he thought her to be a prostitute, for she had covered her face.
text.Rotherham: And Judah, seeing her, reckoned her to be an unchaste woman,—for she had covered her face.

text.NRSV: He turned to her at the road side and said, ‘Come, let me come in to you’, for he did not know that she was his daughter-in-law. She said, ‘What will you give me, that you may come in to me?’
text.Rotherham: So he turned aside unto her. by the way, and said Grant it! I pray thee, let me come in unto thee! For he knew not that she was his daughter-in-law. And she said, What wilt thou give me, if thou shouldst come in unto me?

text.NRSV: He answered, ‘I will send you a kid from the flock.’ And she said, ‘Only if you give me a pledge, until you send it.’
text.Rotherham: And he said, I, will send a kid of the goats from the flock. And she said, Wilt thou give a pledge until thou send it?

text.NRSV: He said, ‘What pledge shall I give you?’ She replied, ‘Your signet and your cord, and the staff that is in your hand.’ So he gave them to her, and went in to her, and she conceived by him.
text.Rotherham: And he said—What is the pledge that I shall give thee? And She said—Thy signet-ring, and thy guard, and thy staff that is in thy hand. So he gave them to her and came in unto her and she conceived by him.

text.NRSV: Then she got up and went away, and taking off her veil she put on her widow’s garments.
text.Rotherham: Then she arose and went her way, and put off her veil from her,—and put on the garments of her widowhood.

text.NRSV: When Judah sent the kid by his friend the Adullamite to recover the pledge from the woman, he could not find her.
text.Rotherham: And Judah sent the kid of the goats by the hand of his friend the Adullamite, to receive the pledge at the hand of the woman,—but he found her not.

text.NRSV: He asked the townspeople, ‘Where is the temple prostitute who was at Enaim by the road?’ But they said, ‘No temple prostitute has been here.’
text.Rotherham: So he asked the men of her place saying. Where is the devotee, she that was in Enaim, by the way? And they said,—There hath been here no devotee.

text.NRSV: So he returned to Judah, and said, ‘I cannot find her; and also the townspeople say there was no temple prostitute here.’
text.Rotherham: Then returned he unto Judah, and said—I found her not,—moreover also, the men of the place said, There hath been here no devotee.

text.NRSV: Judah replied, ‘Let her keep the things as her own, otherwise we will be laughed at; you see, I sent this kid, and you could not find her.’
