text.Rotherham: And Judah said: Thou must take it to her, lest we become a contempt,—lo! I sent this kid, and, thou, hast not found her.

text.NRSV: About three months later Judah was told, ‘Your daughter-in-law Tamar has played the whore; moreover she is pregnant as a result of whoredom.’ And Judah said, ‘Bring her out, and let her be burned.’
text.Rotherham: And it came to pass that about three months after, it was told Judah, saying—Tamar thy daughter-in-law hath been guilty of unchastity, moreover also lo! she hath conceived by unchastity. Then said Judah, Bring her forth and let her be burnt.

text.NRSV: As she was being brought out, she sent word to her father-in-law, ‘It was the owner of these who made me pregnant.’ And she said, ‘Take note, please, whose these are, the signet and the cord and the staff.’
text.Rotherham: When, she, was about to be brought forth, then, she herself, sent unto her father-in-law saying, By the man to whom these belong, have I, conceived! And she said—Examine, I pray thee, to whom belong the signet-ring, and the guard, and the staff—these!

text.NRSV: Then Judah acknowledged them and said, ‘She is more righteous than I, since I did not give her to my son Shelah.’ And he did not lie with her again.
text.Rotherham: So Judah examined them and said—More righteous than I! forasmuch, as I had not given her to Shelah my son. And he knew her again no more.

text.NRSV: When the time of her delivery came, there were twins in her womb.
text.Rotherham: And, it came to pass at the time of her travail, that lo! twins, were in her womb.

text.NRSV: While she was in labour, one put out a hand; and the midwife took and bound on his hand a crimson thread, saying, ‘This one came out first.’
text.Rotherham: And it came to pass when she was in travail, that one put forth a hand,—so the midwife took and bound upon his hand a crimson thread, saying—This came first!

text.NRSV: But just then he drew back his hand, and out came his brother; and she said, ‘What a breach you have broken open!’ Therefore he was named Perez.
text.Rotherham: And so it was that, no sooner had he withdrawn his hand, than lo! his brother had come. And she said, Wherefore hast thou made for thyself a breach? So his name was called Perez.

text.NRSV: Afterwards his brother came out with the crimson thread on his hand; and he was named Zerah.
text.Rotherham: And afterwards, came his brother, on whose hand was the crimson thread,—so his name was called, Zerah.

text.NRSV: Joseph was taken down to Egypt, and Potiphar, an officer of Pharaoh, the captain of the guard, an Egyptian, bought him from the Ishmaelites who had taken him down there.
text.Rotherham: Now, Joseph, was taken down to Egypt,—and Potiphar, courtier of Pharaoh, chief of the royal executioners, an Egyptian bought him, at the hand of the Ishmaelites, who had brought him down thither.

text.NRSV: The Lord was with Joseph, and he became a successful man; he was in the house of his Egyptian master.
text.Rotherham: And it came to pass that Yahweh, was with Joseph, so that he became a prosperous man,—and was in the house of his lord the Egyptian.

text.NRSV: His master saw that the Lord was with him, and that the Lord caused all that he did to prosper in his hands.
text.Rotherham: And his lord saw, that, Yahweh, was with him—and that whatsoever, he was doing, Yahweh, was causing to prosper in his hand.

text.NRSV: So Joseph found favour in his sight and attended him; he made him overseer of his house and put him in charge of all that he had.
text.Rotherham: So Joseph found favour in his eyes and waited upon him,—and he made him overseer over his house, and all that he had, gave he into his hand.

text.NRSV: From the time that he made him overseer in his house and over all that he had, the Lord blessed the Egyptian’s house for Joseph’s sake; the blessing of the Lord was on all that he had, in house and field.
text.Rotherham: And it came to pass from the time that he made him overseer in his house and over all that he had, that Yahweh blessed the house of the Egyptian, for Joseph’s sake,—yea it came to pass, that the blessing of Yahweh, was with all that he had, in the house and in the field;

text.NRSV: So he left all that he had in Joseph’s charge, and with him there he had no concern for anything but the food that he ate. Now Joseph was handsome and good-looking.
text.Rotherham: so that he left all that he had in the hand of Joseph, and took note with him of nothing, save only the bread which, he himself, was eating. And so it was that Joseph was comely in form and comely in countenance.

text.NRSV: And after a time his master’s wife cast her eyes on Joseph and said, ‘Lie with me.’
text.Rotherham: And it came to pass after these things, that his lord’s wife lifted up her eyes unto Joseph,—and she said—Come! lie with me.

text.NRSV: But he refused and said to his master’s wife, ‘Look, with me here, my master has no concern about anything in the house, and he has put everything that he has in my hand.
text.Rotherham: And he refused and said unto his lord’s wife, Lo! my lord, taketh no note with me as to what is in the house,—but, all that pertaineth to him, hath he delivered into my hand:

text.NRSV: He is not greater in this house than I am, nor has he kept back anything from me except yourself, because you are his wife; how then can I do this great wickedness, and sin against God?’
text.Rotherham: There is no one greater in this house than I, neither hath he withheld from me anything, save only thyself in that thou, art his wife,—how, then could I do this great wickedness, and sin against God?

text.NRSV: And although she spoke to Joseph day after day, he would not consent to lie beside her or to be with her.
text.Rotherham: And it came to pass, that although she Spake unto Joseph day after day, yet hearkened he not unto her to lie beside her to be with her.

text.NRSV: One day, however, when he went into the house to do his work, and while no one from the household was inside,
text.Rotherham: And it came to pass, when, on a certain day, he went into the house to attend to his business,—and there was no man of the household, there in the house,

text.NRSV: she caught hold of his garment, saying, ‘Lie with me’; but he left his garment in her hand and fled and ran outside.
text.Rotherham: that she caught him by his garment, saying—Come! lie with me. Then left he his garment in her hand, and fled and gat forth outside,

text.NRSV: When she saw that he had left his garment in her hand and had fled outside,
text.Rotherham: And it came to pass when she saw that he had left his garment in her hand,—and fled forth outside,

text.NRSV: she called out to the members of her household and said to them, ‘See, my husband has brought us a Hebrew to insult us; he came in to me to lie with me, and I cried out with a loud voice;
text.Rotherham: that she cried out to the men of her house and spake to them, saying, Look ye! he hath brought in to us a Hebrew man to insult us,—He came in unto me, to lie with me, so I cried out with a loud voice.

text.NRSV: and after he heard me raise my voice and cry out, he left his garment beside me, and fled outside.’
text.Rotherham: And it came to pass, when he heard that I lifted high my voice, and cried out, then left he his garment beside me, and fled and gat forth outside.

text.NRSV: Then she kept his garment by her until his master came home,
text.Rotherham: So she laid by his garment beside her,—until his lord should come into his house,

text.NRSV: and she told him the same story, saying, ‘The Hebrew servant whom you have brought among us came to me to insult me;
text.Rotherham: Then spake she unto him, according to these words, saying,—He hath been in unto me—the Hebrew servant whom thou didst bring in to us—to insult me.

text.NRSV: but as soon as I raised my voice and cried out, he left his garment beside me, and fled outside.’
text.Rotherham: And it came to pass when I lifted high my voice and cried out, then left he his garment beside me, and fled outside.

text.NRSV: When his master heard the words that his wife told him, saying, ‘This is the way your servant treated me’, he became enraged.
text.Rotherham: And it came to pass, when his lord heard the words of his wife which she spake unto him saying, According to these words, hath thy servant done to me, Then was kindled his anger.

text.NRSV: And Joseph’s master took him and put him into the prison, the place where the king’s prisoners were confined; he remained there in prison.
text.Rotherham: So Joseph’s lord took him and delivered him up to the prison,: the place where, the prisoners of the king, were imprisoned,—so he was there, in the prison.

text.NRSV: But the Lord was with Joseph and showed him steadfast love and gave him favour in the sight of the chief jailer.
text.Rotherham: And it came to pass that, Yahweh, was with Joseph, and extended unto him lovingkindness,—and gave him his favour in the eyes of the chief of the prison.

text.NRSV: The chief jailer committed to Joseph’s care all the prisoners who were in the prison, and whatever was done there, he was the one who did it.
text.Rotherham: So the chief of the prison delivered up, into Joseph’s hand all the prisoners who were in the prison,—and of all that was doing there, he, was the doer.

text.NRSV: The chief jailer paid no heed to anything that was in Joseph’s care, because the Lord was with him; and whatever he did, the Lord made it prosper.
text.Rotherham: After nothing, was the chief of the prison looking, of whatever was in his hand,—in that Yahweh, was with him, and whatsoever, he was doing, Yahweh, was causing to prosper.

text.NRSV: After these things the cupbearer of the king of Egypt and his baker offended their lord the king of Egypt.
text.Rotherham: And it came to pass after these things, that the butler of the king of Egypt, and the baker, had sinned against their lord the king of Egypt.

text.NRSV: Pharaoh was angry with his two officers, the chief cupbearer and the chief baker,
text.Rotherham: Then fell the wroth of Pharaoh upon his two courtiers, upon the chief of the butlers, and upon the chief of the bakers;

text.NRSV: and he put them in custody in the house of the captain of the guard, in the prison where Joseph was confined,
text.Rotherham: so he delivered them up into the ward of the house of the chief of the royal executioners, into the prison,—the place where Joseph was imprisoned.

text.NRSV: and the captain of the guard charged Joseph with them, and he attended them; they continued in custody for some time.
text.Rotherham: And the chief of the royal executioners charged Joseph with them and he waited upon them, and they were some days in ward.

text.NRSV: And both of them dreamed a dream, each his own dream, with its own meaning, the cupbearer and the baker of the king of Egypt, who were confined in the prison.
text.Rotherham: And they dreamed a dream they two, each man, his dream in one night, each man, according to the interpretation of his dream,—the butler and the baker who belonged to the king of Egypt, who were imprisoned in the prison.

text.NRSV: When Joseph came to them in the morning, he saw that they were troubled.
text.Rotherham: And Joseph came in unto them in the morning,—and looked at them, and lo! they were sad.

text.NRSV: So he asked Pharaoh’s officers who were with him in custody in his master’s house, ‘Why are your faces downcast today?’
text.Rotherham: So he asked Pharaoh’s courtiers who were with him in the ward of his lord’s house, saying, Why are your faces troubled to-day!

text.NRSV: They answered him, ‘We have had a dream, and there is no one to interpret it.’ And Joseph said to them, ‘Do not interpretations belong to God? Please tell them to me.’
text.Rotherham: And they said unto him, A dream, have we dreamed, but there is none, to interpret, it. Then said Joseph unto them—Is it not unto God, that interpretations belong? Relate it. I pray you, to me.

text.NRSV: So the chief cupbearer told his dream to Joseph, and said to him, ‘In my dream there was a vine before me,
text.Rotherham: And the chief of the butlers related his dream to Joseph,—and said to him, In my dream, then lo! a vine, before me;

text.NRSV: and on the vine there were three branches. As soon as it budded, it blossomed, and its clusters ripened into grapes.
text.Rotherham: and, in the vine, three shoots,—and the same at sprouting time, had shot up her blossom, and her clusters had brought to perfection ripe grapes.

text.NRSV: Pharaoh’s cup was in my hand, and I took the grapes and pressed them into Pharaoh’s cup, and placed the cup in Pharaoh’s hand.’
text.Rotherham: Now, the cup of Pharaoh, being in my hand, I took the ripe grapes and pressed them out into the cup of Pharaoh, and set the cup upon Pharaoh’s palm.

text.NRSV: Joseph said to him, ‘This is its interpretation: the three branches are three days;
text.Rotherham: And Joseph said to him, This, is the interpretation thereof,—The three shoots are three days:

text.NRSV: within three days Pharaoh will lift up your head and restore you to your office; and you shall place Pharaoh’s cup in his hand, as you used to do when you were his cupbearer.
text.Rotherham: In three days more, will Pharaoh lift up thy head, and restore thee to thine office, and thou shalt set the cup of Pharaoh in his hand, according to the former custom when thou wast his butler.

text.NRSV: Only remember me, when it is well with you, and show me kindness, please, by mentioning me to Pharaoh and getting me out of this prison;
text.Rotherham: But if thou remember how I was with thee when it shall go well with thee, then wilt thou I pray thee do me a lovingkindness,—and make mention of me unto Pharaoh, and bring me forth out of this house;

text.NRSV: for I was stolen out of the land of the Hebrews; and here also I have done nothing that they should have put me into the dungeon.’
text.Rotherham: For I was, stolen, out of the land of the Hebrews,—and, even here, had I done nothing, that they should have put me in the dungeon,

text.NRSV: When the chief baker saw that the interpretation was favourable, he said to Joseph, ‘I also had a dream: there were three cake baskets on my head;
text.Rotherham: Then the chief of the bakers, seeing that he had well interpreted, said unto Joseph, I, too, was in my dream, when lo! three wicker baskets of fine bread were on my head;

text.NRSV: in the uppermost basket there were all sorts of baked food for Pharaoh, but the birds were eating it out of the basket on my head.’
text.Rotherham: and, in the uppermost basket, was some of every kind of food for Pharaoh that a baker could make,—but, the birds, kept eating them out of the basket from off my head.

text.NRSV: And Joseph answered, ‘This is its interpretation: the three baskets are three days;
text.Rotherham: And Joseph responded and said, This, is the interpretation thereof, The three baskets, are, three days:

text.NRSV: within three days Pharaoh will lift up your head from you, and hang you on a pole; and the birds will eat the flesh from you.’
text.Rotherham: In three days more, will Pharaoh lift up thy head from off thee, and hang thee upon a tree, and the birds shall eat thy flesh from off thee.

text.NRSV: On the third day, which was Pharaoh’s birthday, he gave a feast for all his officials, and lifted up the heads of the chief cupbearer and the chief baker among his officials;
text.Rotherham: And it came to pass on the third day the birthday of Pharaoh, that he made a banquet for all his servants, and uplifted the head of the chief of the butlers and the head of the chief of the bakers in the midst of his servants;

text.NRSV: he restored the chief cupbearer to his cupbearing, and he placed the cup in Pharaoh’s hand;
text.Rotherham: and restored the chief of the butlers to his butlership, so that he set the cup upon the palm of Pharaoh;

text.NRSV: but the chief baker he hanged, as Joseph had interpreted to them.
text.Rotherham: but, the chief of the bakers, he hanged,—as, Joseph, had interpreted unto them.

text.NRSV: Yet the chief cupbearer did not remember Joseph, but forgot him.
text.Rotherham: Yet the chief of the butlers made no mention of Joseph but did forget him.

text.NRSV: After two whole years, Pharaoh dreamed that he was standing by the Nile,
text.Rotherham: And it came to pass at the end of two years of days, that, Pharaoh, was dreaming, when lo! he was standing by the river (Nile);

text.NRSV: and there came up out of the Nile seven sleek and fat cows, and they grazed in the reed grass.
text.Rotherham: and lo! from the river, were coming up seven heifers, comely in appearance and fat in flesh,—and they fed among the rushes.

text.NRSV: Then seven other cows, ugly and thin, came up out of the Nile after them, and stood by the other cows on the bank of the Nile.
text.Rotherham: And lo! seven heifers more coming up after them out of the river, uncomely in appearance and lean in flesh,—and they came and stood beside the heifers, by the lip of the river.

text.NRSV: The ugly and thin cows ate up the seven sleek and fat cows. And Pharaoh awoke.
text.Rotherham: Then did the heifers that were uncomely in appearance, and lean in flesh, eat up, the seven heifers that were comely in appearance and fat. So Pharaoh awoke.

text.NRSV: Then he fell asleep and dreamed a second time; seven ears of grain, plump and good, were growing on one stalk.
text.Rotherham: And he fell asleep, and dreamed a second time, when lo! seven ears, coming up on one stalk fat and good;

text.NRSV: Then seven ears, thin and blighted by the east wind, sprouted after them.
text.Rotherham: and lo! seven ears, lean and shrivelled by an east wind, coming up after them.

text.NRSV: The thin ears swallowed up the seven plump and full ears. Pharaoh awoke, and it was a dream.
text.Rotherham: Then did the lean ears swallow up the seven fat and full ears. So Pharaoh awoke and lo! it was a dream.

text.NRSV: In the morning his spirit was troubled; so he sent and called for all the magicians of Egypt and all its wise men. Pharaoh told them his dreams, but there was no one who could interpret them to Pharaoh.
text.Rotherham: And it came to pass in the morning, that his spirit became restless, so he sent and called for all the sacred scribes of Egypt and all her wise men,—and Pharaoh related to them his dreams, but there was no one that could interpret them. to Pharaoh.

text.NRSV: Then the chief cupbearer said to Pharaoh, “I remember my faults today.
text.Rotherham: Then spake the chief of the butlers with Pharaoh saying, My faults, would, I, mention this day.

text.NRSV: Once Pharaoh was angry with his servants, and put me and the chief baker in custody in the house of the captain of the guard.
text.Rotherham: Pharaoh, was wroth with his servants,—and put them in the ward of the house of the chief of the royal executioners, me, and the chief of the bakers.

text.NRSV: We dreamed on the same night, he and I, each having a dream with its own meaning.
text.Rotherham: Then must we needs dream, a dream in one night, I and he,—each man, according to the interpretation of his dream, did we dream.

text.NRSV: A young Hebrew was there with us, a servant of the captain of the guard. When we told him, he interpreted our dreams to us, giving an interpretation to each according to his dream.
text.Rotherham: And, there with us, was a young man, a Hebrew servant to the chief of the royal executioners, and we related to him, and he interpreted to us our dreams,—to each man—according to his dream, did he interpret,

text.NRSV: As he interpreted to us, so it happened; I was restored to my office, and the baker was hanged.”
text.Rotherham: And it came to pass as he interpreted to us, so, it happened,—me, he restored to mine office but him, he hanged.

text.NRSV: Then Pharaoh sent for Joseph, and he was hurriedly brought out of the dungeon. When he had shaved himself and changed his clothes, he came in before Pharaoh.
text.Rotherham: Then sent Pharaoh and summoned Joseph, and they hastened him out of the dungeon,—so he shaved himself and changed his garments, and came in unto Pharaoh.

text.NRSV: And Pharaoh said to Joseph, “I have had a dream, and there is no one who can interpret it. I have heard it said of you that when you hear a dream you can interpret it.”
text.Rotherham: And Pharaoh said unto Joseph, A dream, have I dreamed, but none can interpret it,—but, I, have heard say concerning thee, that on hearing a dream, thou canst interpret it.

text.NRSV: Joseph answered Pharaoh, “It is not I; God will give Pharaoh a favourable answer.”
text.Rotherham: And Joseph answered Pharaoh saying, Not to me, doth it pertain! May, God grant in answer, the prosperity of Pharaoh!

text.NRSV: Then Pharaoh said to Joseph, “In my dream I was standing on the banks of the Nile;
text.Rotherham: Then spake Pharaoh unto Joseph,—In my dream, there was I, standing on the lip of the river;

text.NRSV: and seven cows, fat and sleek, came up out of the Nile and fed in the reed grass.
text.Rotherham: When lo! out of the river, were coming up seven heifers, fat in flesh and comely in form,—and they fed among the rushes.

text.NRSV: Then seven other cows came up after them, poor, very ugly, and thin. Never had I seen such ugly ones in all the land of Egypt.
text.Rotherham: And lo! seven other heifers, coming up after them, poor and very uncomely in form and lean in flesh,—I had never seen such in all the land of Egypt, for uncomeliness.

text.NRSV: The thin and ugly cows ate up the first seven fat cows,
text.Rotherham: Then did the lean and uncomely heifers eat up the first seven fat heifers;

text.NRSV: but when they had eaten them no one would have known that they had done so, for they were still as ugly as before. Then I awoke.
text.Rotherham: and they passed into their stomach yet could it not be known that they had passed into them, their appearance, being uncomely, as at the beginning. So I awoke.

text.NRSV: I fell asleep a second time and I saw in my dream seven ears of grain, full and good, growing on one stalk,
text.Rotherham: Then looked I in my dream,—And lo! seven ears, coming up on one stalk, full and good;

text.NRSV: and seven ears, withered, thin, and blighted by the east wind, sprouting after them;
text.Rotherham: And lo! seven ears, withered lean shrivelled by an east wind growing up after them.

text.NRSV: and the thin ears swallowed up the seven good ears. But when I told it to the magicians, there was no one who could explain it to me.”
text.Rotherham: Then did the lean ears swallow up, the seven good ears. So I told [these things] unto the sacred scribes, but there was none that could explain [them] to me.

text.NRSV: Then Joseph said to Pharaoh, “Pharaoh’s dreams are one and the same; God has revealed to Pharaoh what he is about to do.
text.Rotherham: Then said Joseph unto Pharaoh, The dream of Pharaoh, is, one. What God is about to do hath he announced to Pharaoh.

text.NRSV: The seven good cows are seven years, and the seven good ears are seven years; the dreams are one.
text.Rotherham: The seven good heifers, are, seven years, and, the seven good ears are, seven years,—the dream, is, one.

text.NRSV: The seven lean and ugly cows that came up after them are seven years, as are the seven empty ears blighted by the east wind. They are seven years of famine.
text.Rotherham: And the seven lean and uncomely heifers that were coming up after them, are seven years, and the seven lean ears, shrivelled by an east wind, will turn out to be—seven years of famine.

text.NRSV: It is as I told Pharaoh; God has shown to Pharaoh what he is about to do.
text.Rotherham: The very word that I spake unto Pharaoh, what, God, is about to do, hath he showed unto Pharaoh.

text.NRSV: There will come seven years of great plenty throughout all the land of Egypt.
text.Rotherham: Lo! seven years, coming in,—of great plenty, in all the land of Egypt.

text.NRSV: After them there will arise seven years of famine, and all the plenty will be forgotten in the land of Egypt; the famine will consume the land.
text.Rotherham: Then shall arise seven years of famine, after them, so shall be forgotten all the plenty in the land of Egypt,—and the famine shall consume the land;

text.NRSV: The plenty will no longer be known in the land because of the famine that will follow, for it will be very grievous.
text.Rotherham: neither shall the plenty in the land be discernible, because of that famine coming after,—for it shall be, very severe.

text.NRSV: And the doubling of Pharaoh’s dream means that the thing is fixed by God, and God will shortly bring it about.
text.Rotherham: And for that there was a repeating of the dream unto Pharaoh, twice, it is because the thing, is established, from God, and God is hastening to do it.

text.NRSV: Now therefore let Pharaoh select a man who is discerning and wise, and set him over the land of Egypt.
text.Rotherham: Now, therefore, let Pharaoh look out a man discreet and wise,—and set him over the land of Egypt.

text.NRSV: Let Pharaoh proceed to appoint overseers over the land, and take one-fifth of the produce of the land of Egypt during the seven plenteous years.
text.Rotherham: Let Pharaoh do this, that he may appoint overseers over the land,—so shall he take up a fifth of the land of Egypt, during the seven years of plenty.

text.NRSV: Let them gather all the food of these good years that are coming, and lay up grain under the authority of Pharaoh for food in the cities, and let them keep it.
text.Rotherham: And let them gather up all the food of these seven good years that are coming in,—and let them heap up corn under the hand of Pharaoh as food in cities so shall they keep it.

text.NRSV: That food shall be a reserve for the land against the seven years of famine that are to befall the land of Egypt, so that the land may not perish through the famine.”
text.Rotherham: So shall the food become a store for the land, for the seven years of famine which shall come about in the land of Egypt,—and the land shall not be cut off in the famine.

text.NRSV: The proposal pleased Pharaoh and all his servants.
text.Rotherham: And the thing was good in the eyes of Pharaoh,—and in the eyes of all his servants;

text.NRSV: Pharaoh said to his servants, “Can we find anyone else like this—one in whom is the spirit of God?”
text.Rotherham: and Pharaoh said unto his servants,—Can we find such a one, a man in whom is the Spirit of God?

text.NRSV: So Pharaoh said to Joseph, “Since God has shown you all this, there is no one so discerning and wise as you.
text.Rotherham: So Pharaoh said unto Joseph, After God hath made known unto thee all this, there is no one discreet and wise like thee.

text.NRSV: You shall be over my house, and all my people shall order themselves as you command; only with regard to the throne will I be greater than you.”
text.Rotherham: Thou, shalt be over my house, and on thy mouth, shall all my people kiss,—only as to the throne, will I be greater than thou.

text.NRSV: And Pharaoh said to Joseph, “See, I have set you over all the land of Egypt.”
text.Rotherham: And Pharaoh said unto Joseph,—See, I have set thee over all the land of Egypt.

text.NRSV: Removing his signet ring from his hand, Pharaoh put it on Joseph’s hand; he arrayed him in garments of fine linen, and put a gold chain around his neck.
text.Rotherham: So Pharaoh took his ring from off his hand and put it upon Joseph’s hand,—and clothed him in garments of fine linen, and put the chain of gold upon his neck;

text.NRSV: He had him ride in his second chariot; and they cried out in front of him, “Bow the knee!” Thus he set him over all the land of Egypt.
text.Rotherham: and made him ride in the second chariot which belonged to him, and they cried out before him. Bow the knee! thus setting him over all the land of Egypt.

text.NRSV: Moreover Pharaoh said to Joseph, “I am Pharaoh, and without your consent no one shall lift up hand or foot in all the land of Egypt.”
text.Rotherham: And Pharaoh said unto Joseph. I, am Pharaoh, Without thee, therefore shall no man raise his hand or his foot in all the land of Egypt.

text.NRSV: Pharaoh gave Joseph the name Zaphenath-paneah; and he gave him Asenath daughter of Potiphera, priest of On, as his wife. Thus Joseph gained authority over the land of Egypt.
text.Rotherham: And Pharaoh called Joseph’s name Zaphenath-paneah, and gave him Asenath daughter of Poti-phera priest of On, to wife,—and Joseph went forth over the land of Egypt.

text.NRSV: Joseph was thirty years old when he entered the service of Pharaoh king of Egypt. And Joseph went out from the presence of Pharaoh, and went through all the land of Egypt.
text.Rotherham: Now, Joseph, was thirty years old, when he took his station, before Pharaoh king of Egypt. So Joseph went forth from before Pharaoh, and passed along through all the land of Egypt.

text.NRSV: During the seven plenteous years the earth produced abundantly.
text.Rotherham: And the land produced, in the seven years of plenty, by handfuls,

text.NRSV: He gathered up all the food of the seven years when there was plenty in the land of Egypt, and stored up food in the cities; he stored up in every city the food from the fields around it.
text.Rotherham: And he gathered up all the food of the seven years in which there was plenty in the land of Egypt, and laid up food in cities—the food of the fields of the city. which were round about it, laid he up within it.

text.NRSV: So Joseph stored up grain in such abundance—like the sand of the sea—that he stopped measuring it; it was beyond measure.
text.Rotherham: Thus did Joseph heap up corn like the sand of the sea making it exceeding abundant,—until one hath left off reckoning, because it cannot be reckoned.

text.NRSV: Before the years of famine came, Joseph had two sons, whom Asenath daughter of Potiphera, priest of On, bore to him.
text.Rotherham: Now to Joseph, were born two sons, ere yet came in the year of famine,—whom Asenath daughter of Poti-phera priest of On, bare to him.

text.NRSV: Joseph named the firstborn Manasseh, “For,” he said, “God has made me forget all my hardship and all my father’s house.”
text.Rotherham: And Joseph called the name of the firstborn Manasseh,—For God hath made me forget all my trouble, and all the house of my father.

text.NRSV: The second he named Ephraim, “For God has made me fruitful in the land of my misfortunes.”
text.Rotherham: And, the name of the second, called he Ephraim, For God hath made me fruitful in the land of my humiliation.

text.NRSV: The seven years of plenty that prevailed in the land of Egypt came to an end;
text.Rotherham: Then came to an end the seven years of the plenty,—which was in the land of Egypt;

text.NRSV: and the seven years of famine began to come, just as Joseph had said. There was famine in every country, but throughout the land of Egypt there was bread.
text.Rotherham: and the seven years of famine began to come in, according as Joseph had said,—and it came to pass that there was a famine in all the lands, but in all the land of Egypt, there was bread.

text.NRSV: When the whole land of Egypt was famished, the people cried to Pharaoh for bread. Pharaoh said to all the Egyptians, “Go to Joseph; what he says to you, do.”
text.Rotherham: Yet was famine felt in all the land of Egypt, and the people made outcry; unto Pharaoh for bread,—and Pharaoh said to all Egypt,—Go ye unto Joseph, that which he saith to you, shall ye do.

text.NRSV: And since the famine had spread over all the land, Joseph opened all the storehouses, and sold to the Egyptians, for the famine was severe in the land of Egypt.
text.Rotherham: Now, the famine, was over all the face of the land,—so Joseph opened all [places] wherein it was and sold corn to the Egyptians, and the famine laid fast hold of the land of Egypt.

text.NRSV: Moreover, all the world came to Joseph in Egypt to buy grain, because the famine became severe throughout the world.
text.Rotherham: All the earth also, came in to Egypt to buy corn, unto Joseph,—because the famine had laid fast hold on all the earth.

text.NRSV: When Jacob learned that there was grain in Egypt, he said to his sons, “Why do you keep looking at one another?
text.Rotherham: Then saw Jacob that there was corn in Egypt,—and Jacob said to his sons, Wherefore look ye one at another?

text.NRSV: I have heard,” he said, “that there is grain in Egypt; go down and buy grain for us there, that we may live and not die.”
text.Rotherham: And he said, Lo I have heard that there is corn in Egypt,—Go down thither and buy corn for us from thence, that we may live and not die.

text.NRSV: So ten of Joseph’s brothers went down to buy grain in Egypt.
text.Rotherham: So Joseph’s ten brethren went down,—to buy corn from the Egyptians;

text.NRSV: But Jacob did not send Joseph’s brother Benjamin with his brothers, for he feared that harm might come to him.
text.Rotherham: but, Benjamin, Joseph’s own brother, Jacob sent not with his brethren,—for he said, Lest there befall him any mischief.

text.NRSV: Thus the sons of Israel were among the other people who came to buy grain, for the famine had reached the land of Canaan.
text.Rotherham: Thus came in the sons of Israel, to buy corn in the midst of them that came—for the famine was in the land of Canaan.

text.NRSV: Now Joseph was governor over the land; it was he who sold to all the people of the land. And Joseph’s brothers came and bowed themselves before him with their faces to the ground.
text.Rotherham: Now, Joseph—he, it was, that was in power over the land, he, it was that was selling corn to all the people of the land,—so Joseph’s brethren came in, and bowed themselves down to him, with their faces to the earth.

text.NRSV: When Joseph saw his brothers, he recognized them, but he treated them like strangers and spoke harshly to them. He said to them, “Where do you come from?” They said, “From the land of Canaan, to buy food.”
text.Rotherham: And Joseph beheld his brethren and recognised them,—and made himself a stranger unto them and spake with them harsh things and said unto them—Whence have ye come in? And they said, From the land of Canaan, to buy food.

text.NRSV: Although Joseph had recognized his brothers, they did not recognize him.
text.Rotherham: And Joseph recognised his brethren,—whereas they, recognised him not.

text.NRSV: Joseph also remembered the dreams that he had dreamed about them. He said to them, “You are spies; you have come to see the nakedness of the land!”
text.Rotherham: And Joseph remembered the dreams which he had dreamed regarding them,—so he said unto them—Spies, are, ye! to see the nakedness of the land, have ye come in!

text.NRSV: They said to him, “No, my lord; your servants have come to buy food.
text.Rotherham: And they said unto him, Nay, my lord,—but, thy servants, are come to buy food.

text.NRSV: We are all sons of one man; we are honest men; your servants have never been spies.”
text.Rotherham: All of us, are, sons of one man,—honest men, are we: thy servants are not, spies.

text.NRSV: But he said to them, “No, you have come to see the nakedness of the land!”
text.Rotherham: And he said unto him,—Nay! but, the nakedness of the land, are ye come in to see.

text.NRSV: They said, “We, your servants, are twelve brothers, the sons of a certain man in the land of Canaan; the youngest, however, is now with our father, and one is no more.”
text.Rotherham: And they said: We, thy twelve servants, are, brethren, sons of one man in the and of Canaan,—and lo! the youngest: is with our father this day, and, the one, is not!

text.NRSV: But Joseph said to them, “It is just as I have said to you; you are spies!
text.Rotherham: And Joseph said unto them,—The very thing, that I spake unto you, saying—Spies, are, ye.

text.NRSV: Here is how you shall be tested: as Pharaoh lives, you shall not leave this place unless your youngest brother comes here!
text.Rotherham: Hereby, shall ye be proved,—By the life of Pharaoh, ye shall not go forth from hence, save only by the coming in of your youngest brother hither.

text.NRSV: Let one of you go and bring your brother, while the rest of you remain in prison, in order that your words may be tested, whether there is truth in you; or else, as Pharaoh lives, surely you are spies.”
text.Rotherham: Send—from among you—one that he may fetch your brother, but be, ye, held as prisoners, that your words may be proved whether, truth, is with you,—and, if not, by the life of Pharaoh, surely, spies, ye are.

text.NRSV: And he put them all together in prison for three days.
text.Rotherham: So he drew them all together into ward, for three days.

text.NRSV: On the third day Joseph said to them, “Do this and you will live, for I fear God:
text.Rotherham: And Joseph said unto them on the third day, This, do and live,—God himself, do, I, revere.

text.NRSV: if you are honest men, let one of your brothers stay confined in your prison, and the rest of you go and carry grain for the famine of your households,
text.Rotherham: If ye, are, honest men, one brother of you shall be kept as a prisoner in your house of ward,—but, ye, go, take in corn for the famine of your houses;

text.NRSV: and bring your youngest brother to me. Thus your words will be verified, and you shall not die.” And they agreed to do so.
text.Rotherham: and your youngest brother, shall ye bring in unto me, that your words may be confirmed, and ye die not. And they did so.

text.NRSV: They said to one another, “Alas, we are paying the penalty for what we did to our brother; we saw his anguish when he pleaded with us, but we would not listen. That is why this anguish has come upon us.”
text.Rotherham: And they said each man unto his brother—Verily guilty, we are, respecting our brother, in that we saw the distress of his soul when he appealed unto us for favour and we hearkened not,—therefore, hath come in unto us this distress.

text.NRSV: Then Reuben answered them, “Did I not tell you not to wrong the boy? But you would not listen. So now there comes a reckoning for his blood.”
text.Rotherham: And Reuben responded to them, saying—Spake I not unto you saying, Do not sin against the child! And ye hearkened not? His very blood, therefore lo! it is required.

text.NRSV: They did not know that Joseph understood them, since he spoke with them through an interpreter.
text.Rotherham: But, they, knew not that Joseph was hearkening,—for, the interpreter, was between them.

text.NRSV: He turned away from them and wept; then he returned and spoke to them. And he picked out Simeon and had him bound before their eyes.
text.Rotherham: So he turned away from them, and wept,—then came back unto them and spake unto them, and took away from them Simeon, and bound him prisoner before their eyes.

text.NRSV: Joseph then gave orders to fill their bags with grain, to return every man’s money to his sack, and to give them provisions for their journey. This was done for them.
text.Rotherham: Then commanded Joseph that their bags should be filled with corn, and their silver be returned each man’s into his sack, and provision be given for the journey,—and it was done to them thus.

text.NRSV: They loaded their donkeys with their grain, and departed.
text.Rotherham: So they lifted up their corn on their asses,—and went their way, from thence.

text.NRSV: When one of them opened his sack to give his donkey fodder at the lodging place, he saw his money at the top of the sack.
text.Rotherham: And the first opened his sack to give fodder to his ass at the inn,—and beheld his silver, yea there, it was in the mouth of his sack!

text.NRSV: He said to his brothers, “My money has been put back; here it is in my sack!” At this they lost heart and turned trembling to one another, saying, “What is this that God has done to us?”
text.Rotherham: So he said unto his brethren My silver hath been returned, yea verily, there it is in my sack! Then went forth their heart and they turned trembling—each man unto his brother saying. What is this that God hath done to us?

text.NRSV: When they came to their father Jacob in the land of Canaan, they told him all that had happened to them, saying,
text.Rotherham: So they came in unto Jacob their father to the land of Canaan,—and told him all that had befallen them saying:

text.NRSV: The man, the lord of the land, spoke harshly to us, and charged us with spying on the land.
text.Rotherham: The man that is lord of the land spake unto us harsh things,—and then delivered us up. as spies of the land.

text.NRSV: But we said to him, ‘We are honest men, we are not spies.
text.Rotherham: And we said unto him Honest men, are, we,—we are not spies!

text.NRSV: We are twelve brothers, sons of our father; one is no more, and the youngest is now with our father in the land of Canaan.’
text.Rotherham: Twelve brethren, are, we, sons of our father,—the one, is not, and the youngest is this day with our father in the land of Canaan.

text.NRSV: Then the man, the lord of the land, said to us, ‘By this I shall know that you are honest men: leave one of your brothers with me, take grain for the famine of your households, and go your way.
text.Rotherham: Then said the man the lord of the land unto us, Hereby, shall I get to know that honest men, ye are,—One brother of you, leave ye remaining with me, and corn for the famine of your houses, take ye and go your way;

text.NRSV: Bring your youngest brother to me, and your words will be verified, and you shall not die.’ And they agreed to do so.
text.Rotherham: And bring ye in your youngest brother unto me, So must I get to know that ye are, not spies, but are honest men: Your brother, will I give up to you, and with the land, shall ye traffic.

text.NRSV: When they emptied their sacks, there was each man’s bundle of money in his sack; and when they and their father saw their bundles of money, they were dismayed.
text.Rotherham: And it came to pass when, they, were emptying their, sacks then lo! each man’s bundle of silver, was in his sack,—and when they beheld their bundles of silver—they and their father, they were afraid,

text.NRSV: Their father Jacob said to them, “It is me that you bereave of children: Joseph is no more, and Simeon is no more, and now you would take Benjamin. All this has happened to me!”
text.Rotherham: And Jacob their father said unto them, Me, have ye bereaved,—Joseph, is not, and, Simeon, is not, and Benjamin, ye would take! Against me, have happened all these things.

text.NRSV: Then Reuben said to his father, “You may kill my two sons if I do not bring him back to you. Put him in my hands, and I will bring him back to you.”
text.Rotherham: Then spake Reuben unto his father saying, My two sons, shalt thou put to death, if I bring him not unto thee,—Come give him up upon my hand, and I, will restore him unto thee.

text.NRSV: But he said, “My son shall not go down with you, for his brother is dead, and he alone is left. If harm should come to him on the journey that you are to make, you would bring down my gray hairs with sorrow to Sheol.”
text.Rotherham: And he said, My son shall not go down with you,—For, his brother, is dead and, he alone, is left, and as surely as there befall him any mischief by the way wherein ye go, so surely shall ye bring down my grey hairs with sorrow unto hades.

text.NRSV: Now the famine was severe in the land.
text.Rotherham: But the famine, was severe in the land.

text.NRSV: And when they had eaten up the grain that they had brought from Egypt, their father said to them, “Go again, buy us a little more food.”
text.Rotherham: So it came to pass when they had made an end of eating the corn which they had brought in out of Egypt, that their father said unto them,—Return buy for us a little food.

text.NRSV: But Judah said to him, “The man solemnly warned us, saying, ‘You shall not see my face unless your brother is with you.’
text.Rotherham: Then spake Judah unto him saying,—The man, did protest, to us saying—Ye shall not see my face, except, your brother, is with you.

text.NRSV: If you will send our brother with us, we will go down and buy you food;
text.Rotherham: If thou art sending our brother with us, we will certainly go down, and buy for thee food;

text.NRSV: but if you will not send him, we will not go down, for the man said to us, ‘You shall not see my face, unless your brother is with you.’”
text.Rotherham: but, if thou art not sending him, we will not go down, for the man, said unto us Ye shall not see my face, except, your brother, is with you.

text.NRSV: Israel said, “Why did you treat me so badly as to tell the man that you had another brother?”
text.Rotherham: And Israel said, Wherefore did ye ill to me,—to tell the man, there yet remained to you a brother?

text.NRSV: They replied, “The man questioned us carefully about ourselves and our kindred, saying, ‘Is your father still alive? Have you another brother?’ What we told him was in answer to these questions. Could we in any way know that he would say, ‘Bring your brother down’?”
text.Rotherham: And they said, The man, did ask, concerning ourselves and concerning our kindred saying—Is your father yet alive? Have ye a brother? So we told him according to the tenor of these words. Could we, at all know, that he would say, Bring down your brother?

text.NRSV: Then Judah said to his father Israel, “Send the boy with me, and let us be on our way, so that we may live and not die—you and we and also our little ones.
text.Rotherham: Then said Judah unto Israel his father—Come! send thou down the young man with me that we may arise and go our way,—and live and not die, both we and thou and our little ones.

text.NRSV: I myself will be surety for him; you can hold me accountable for him. If I do not bring him back to you and set him before you, then let me bear the blame forever.
text.Rotherham: I, will be surety for him, at my hand, shalt thou require him,—as surely as I bring him not in unto thee, and set him down before thee, so surely will I be counted a sinner against thee all the days,

text.NRSV: If we had not delayed, we would now have returned twice.”
text.Rotherham: For, if we had not lingered, surely now, had we come back here a second time.

text.NRSV: Then their father Israel said to them, “If it must be so, then do this: take some of the choice fruits of the land in your bags, and carry them down as a present to the man—a little balm and a little honey, gum, resin, pistachio nuts, and almonds.
text.Rotherham: Then Israel their father said unto them—If so, then do this,—Take of the song of the land in your vessels, and carry down to the man a present,—A little balsam and a little honey, tragacanth gum and cistus gum, pistachio nuts, and almonds.

text.NRSV: Take double the money with you. Carry back with you the money that was returned in the top of your sacks; perhaps it was an oversight.
text.Rotherham: And double silver, take in your hand,—also the silver that was put back in the mouth of your sacks, carry ye back in your hand, peradventure it was an error,

text.NRSV: Take your brother also, and be on your way again to the man;
text.Rotherham: Your brother also, take ye,—and arise go again unto the man.

text.NRSV: may God Almighty grant you mercy before the man, so that he may send back your other brother and Benjamin. As for me, if I am bereaved of my children, I am bereaved.”
text.Rotherham: And, God Almighty, give you compassion before the man, so shall he send with you, your other brother, and Benjamin. But, as for me, when I am bereaved, I am bereaved!

text.NRSV: So the men took the present, and they took double the money with them, as well as Benjamin. Then they went on their way down to Egypt, and stood before Joseph.
text.Rotherham: So the men took this present, double silver also, took they in their hand and Benjamin,—and arose and went down into Egypt, and stood before Joseph.

text.NRSV: When Joseph saw Benjamin with them, he said to the steward of his house, “Bring the men into the house, and slaughter an animal and make ready, for the men are to dine with me at noon.”
text.Rotherham: And Joseph saw—with them—Benjamin, so he said to him that was over his house Bring the men into the house,—and slay meat and make ready, for with me, shall the men eat at noon.

text.NRSV: The man did as Joseph said, and brought the men to Joseph’s house.
text.Rotherham: So the men did as Joseph had said, and the man brought the men into Joseph’s house,

text.NRSV: Now the men were afraid because they were brought to Joseph’s house, and they said, “It is because of the money, replaced in our sacks the first time, that we have been brought in, so that he may have an occasion to fall upon us, to make slaves of us and take our donkeys.”
text.Rotherham: Then were the men afraid because they had been brought into the house of Joseph, and they said—For the matter of the silver that came back in our sacks at the beginning, have, we, been brought in,—that he may turn round upon us and fall upon us, and take us for servants and our asses.

text.NRSV: So they went up to the steward of Joseph’s house and spoke with him at the entrance to the house.
text.Rotherham: So they drew near unto the man that was over Joseph’s house,—and spake unto him at the entrance of the house;

text.NRSV: They said, “Oh, my lord, we came down the first time to buy food;
text.Rotherham: and said, May it please my lord! We, did indeed come down, at the beginning to buy food.

text.NRSV: and when we came to the lodging place we opened our sacks, and there was each one’s money in the top of his sack, our money in full weight. So we have brought it back with us.
text.Rotherham: And it came to pass when we had entered into the inn, that we opened our sacks, and lo! the silver of each man, in the mouth of his sack,—our silver in its full weight,—so we have bought it back in our hand!

text.NRSV: Moreover we have brought down with us additional money to buy food. We do not know who put our money in our sacks.”
text.Rotherham: And, more silver, have we brought down in our hand, to buy food,—we know not who put our silver in our sacks.

text.NRSV: He replied, “Rest assured, do not be afraid; your God and the God of your father must have put treasure in your sacks for you; I received your money.” Then he brought Simeon out to them.
text.Rotherham: And he said—Peace to you do not fear! Your God, and the God of your father, hath given you secret treasure, in your sacks, as for your silver, it came in unto me. And he brought forth unto them Simeon.

text.NRSV: When the steward had brought the men into Joseph’s house, and given them water, and they had washed their feet, and when he had given their donkeys fodder,
text.Rotherham: So the man brought the men into Joseph’s house,—and gave them water and they bathed their feet, and he gave them provender for their asses.

text.NRSV: they made the present ready for Joseph’s coming at noon, for they had heard that they would dine there.
text.Rotherham: And they made ready the present, against the coming in of Joseph at noon,—for they heard that it was there they should eat bread.

text.NRSV: When Joseph came home, they brought him the present that they had carried into the house, and bowed to the ground before him.
text.Rotherham: So when Joseph came into the house, they brought in to him the present which was in their hand into the house,—and bowed themselves down to him to the earth.

text.NRSV: He inquired about their welfare, and said, “Is your father well, the old man of whom you spoke? Is he still alive?”
text.Rotherham: Then asked he after their welfare, and said—Is it well with your aged father, of whom ye spake? Is he yet alive?

text.NRSV: They said, “Your servant our father is well; he is still alive.” And they bowed their heads and did obeisance.
text.Rotherham: And they said—It is well with thy servant our father, he is yet alive. And they bent their heads and bowed them-selves down.

text.NRSV: Then he looked up and saw his brother Benjamin, his mother’s son, and said, “Is this your youngest brother, of whom you spoke to me? God be gracious to you, my son!”
text.Rotherham: Then lifted he up his eyes, and saw Benjamin his brother son of his mother, and said—Is, this, your youngest brother, of whom ye spake unto me? And he said, God, grant thee favour my son!

text.NRSV: With that, Joseph hurried out, because he was overcome with affection for his brother, and he was about to weep. So he went into a private room and wept there.
text.Rotherham: And Joseph made haste for his compassions towards his brother were pent up, and he sought to weep,—so he went into the inner chamber and wept there,

text.NRSV: Then he washed his face and came out; and controlling himself he said, “Serve the meal.”
text.Rotherham: Then bathed he his face and came forth,—and restrained himself, and said Set on bread!

text.NRSV: They served him by himself, and them by themselves, and the Egyptians who ate with him by themselves, because the Egyptians could not eat with the Hebrews, for that is an abomination to the Egyptians.
text.Rotherham: So they set on for him—by himself, and for them—by themselves,—and for the Egyptians that were eating with him—by themselves, for the Egyptians might not eat, bread, with the Hebrews for an abomination, had that been to Egyptians.

text.NRSV: When they were seated before him, the firstborn according to his birthright and the youngest according to his youth, the men looked at one another in amazement.
text.Rotherham: So they took their seats before him, the firstborn, according to his birthright and the youngest, according to his youth,—and the men looked with amazement, each one at his companion.

text.NRSV: Portions were taken to them from Joseph’s table, but Benjamin’s portion was five times as much as any of theirs. So they drank and were merry with him.
text.Rotherham: And he sent portions from before him unto them, and the portion of Benjamin exceeded the portions of them all, five-fold. And they drank and made merry with him.

text.NRSV: Then he commanded the steward of his house, “Fill the men’s sacks with food, as much as they can carry, and put each man’s money in the top of his sack.
text.Rotherham: Then commanded he him who was over his house saying—Fill the sacks of the men, with food, as much as they can carry, and put each man’s silver in the mouth of his sack;

text.NRSV: Put my cup, the silver cup, in the top of the sack of the youngest, with his money for the grain.” And he did as Joseph told him.
text.Rotherham: and my cup—the cup of silver, shalt thou put in the mouth of the sack of the youngest, with his corn-silver. So he did, according to the word of Joseph, which he had spoken.

text.NRSV: As soon as the morning was light, the men were sent away with their donkeys.
text.Rotherham: By the time, the morning, was light, the men, had been sent away, they, and their asses.

text.NRSV: When they had gone only a short distance from the city, Joseph said to his steward, “Go, follow after the men; and when you overtake them, say to them, ‘Why have you returned evil for good? Why have you stolen my silver cup?
text.Rotherham: They themselves, had gone out of the city—they had not gone far, when Joseph said to him who was over his house, Rise chase after the men,—so shalt thou overtake them and shalt say unto them, Wherefore have ye requited evil for good?

text.NRSV: Is it not from this that my lord drinks? Does he not indeed use it for divination? You have done wrong in doing this.’”
text.Rotherham: Is not this that in which my lord drinketh, and whereby, he himself, doth divine? Ye have done evil in what ye have done.

text.NRSV: When he overtook them, he repeated these words to them.
text.Rotherham: So he overtook them,—and spake unto them these words.

text.NRSV: They said to him, “Why does my lord speak such words as these? Far be it from your servants that they should do such a thing!
text.Rotherham: And they said unto him, Wherefore should my lord speak such words as these? Far be thy servants, from doing such a thing as this!

text.NRSV: Look, the money that we found at the top of our sacks, we brought back to you from the land of Canaan; why then would we steal silver or gold from your lord’s house?
text.Rotherham: Lo! the silver that we found in the mouth of our sacks, we brought back unto thee out of the land of Canaan,—how then could we steal out of the house of thy lord, silver or gold?

text.NRSV: Should it be found with any one of your servants, let him die; moreover the rest of us will become my lord’s slaves.”
text.Rotherham: With whomsoever of thy servants it can be found, he shall die,—and, we also, will become my lord’s servants.

text.NRSV: He said, “Even so; in accordance with your words, let it be: he with whom it is found shall become my slave, but the rest of you shall go free.”
text.Rotherham: And he said, Yea, now, according to your words, so, let it be,—he with whom it is found, shall be my servant, Ye, however, shall be clear.

text.NRSV: Then each one quickly lowered his sack to the ground, and each opened his sack.
text.Rotherham: So they hastened and took down each man his sack to the ground,—and opened each man his sack.

text.NRSV: He searched, beginning with the eldest and ending with the youngest; and the cup was found in Benjamin’s sack.
text.Rotherham: Then made he thorough search, with the eldest, he began, and with the youngest, he ended,—and the cup was found in the sack of Benjamin!

text.NRSV: At this they tore their clothes. Then each loaded his donkey, and they returned to the city.
text.Rotherham: And they rent their mantles,—and laded each man his ass, and returned to the city.

text.NRSV: Judah and his brothers came to Joseph’s house while he was still there; and they fell to the ground before him.
text.Rotherham: And Judah and his brethren came into the house of Joseph, he, yet being there,—and fell down before him to the earth.

text.NRSV: Joseph said to them, “What deed is this that you have done? Do you not know that one such as I can practice divination?”
text.Rotherham: And Joseph said to them, What is this deed which ye have done? Know ye not that such a man as I, can even divine?

text.NRSV: And Judah said, “What can we say to my lord? What can we speak? How can we clear ourselves? God has found out the guilt of your servants; here we are then, my lord’s slaves, both we and also the one in whose possession the cup has been found.”
text.Rotherham: Then said Judah—What shall we say to my lord, how shall we speak and how shall we justify ourselves,—when, God himself, hath found out the iniquity of thy servants? Behold us! my lord’s servants, both we, and he in whose hand the cup hath been found.

text.NRSV: But he said, “Far be it from me that I should do so! Only the one in whose possession the cup was found shall be my slave; but as for you, go up in peace to your father.”
text.Rotherham: And he said, Far be it from me to do this thing! The man in whose hand the cup hath been found, he, shall be my servant, but as for you, go ye up in peace unto your father.

text.NRSV: Then Judah stepped up to him and said, “O my lord, let your servant please speak a word in my lord’s ears, and do not be angry with your servant; for you are like Pharaoh himself.
text.Rotherham: Then Judah drew near unto him, and said—Pardon my lord! Pray let thy servant speak a word in the ears of my lord, and do not let thine anger be kindled against thy servant,—for, thus, art, thou as Pharaoh!

text.NRSV: My lord asked his servants, saying, ‘Have you a father or a brother?’
text.Rotherham: It was, my lord, who asked his servants, saying,—Have ye a father, or a brother?

text.NRSV: And we said to my lord, ‘We have a father, an old man, and a young brother, the child of his old age. His brother is dead; he alone is left of his mother’s children, and his father loves him.’
text.Rotherham: So we said unto my lord, We have an aged father, and the child of his old age a youth,—his brother, being dead, he alone, is left of his mother and, his father, loveth him.

text.NRSV: Then you said to your servants, ‘Bring him down to me, so that I may set my eyes on him.’
text.Rotherham: So then thou saidst unto thy servants, Bring him down unto me,—that I may set mine eyes upon him.

text.NRSV: We said to my lord, ‘The boy cannot leave his father, for if he should leave his father, his father would die.’
text.Rotherham: And we said unto my lord, The lad cannot leave his father,—as surely as he leaveth his father, so surely will he, die.

text.NRSV: Then you said to your servants, ‘Unless your youngest brother comes down with you, you shall see my face no more.’
text.Rotherham: And thou saidst unto thy servants, Except your youngest brother do come down with you, ye shall not again behold my face.

text.NRSV: When we went back to your servant my father we told him the words of my lord.
text.Rotherham: So it came to pass when we had gone up unto thy servant, my father, that we told him the words of my lord.

text.NRSV: And when our father said, ‘Go again, buy us a little food,’
text.Rotherham: Then said our father,—Go again buy us a little food,

text.NRSV: we said, ‘We cannot go down. Only if our youngest brother goes with us, will we go down; for we cannot see the man’s face unless our youngest brother is with us.’
text.Rotherham: And we said, We cannot go down,—If our youngest brother be with us, then will we go down, for we cannot see the face of the man if, our youngest brother, is not with us.

text.NRSV: Then your servant my father said to us, ‘You know that my wife bore me two sons;
text.Rotherham: Then said thy servant my father, unto us,—Ye yourselves, know, that, two, did my wife bear to me,

text.NRSV: one left me, and I said, Surely he has been torn to pieces; and I have never seen him since.
text.Rotherham: and the one went forth from me, and I said—Certainly he hath been, torn in pieces,—And I have never seen him since.

text.NRSV: If you take this one also from me, and harm comes to him, you will bring down my gray hairs in sorrow to Sheol.’
text.Rotherham: As surely as ye take, this one also, away from before my face and there befall him any mischief, so surely shall ye bring down my grey hairs, with misfortune to hades.

text.NRSV: Now therefore, when I come to your servant my father and the boy is not with us, then, as his life is bound up in the boy’s life,
text.Rotherham: Now, therefore, as surely as I go in unto thy servant my father, and, the lad, is not with us,—seeing that his soul is bound up with the lad’s soul,

text.NRSV: when he sees that the boy is not with us, he will die; and your servants will bring down the gray hairs of your servant our father with sorrow to Sheol.
text.Rotherham: so surely shall it come to pass that when he seeth that the lad is not with us, then will he die. So shall thy servants bring down the grey hairs of thy servant our father, with sorrow to hades.

text.NRSV: For your servant became surety for the boy to my father, saying, ‘If I do not bring him back to you, then I will bear the blame in the sight of my father all my life.’
text.Rotherham: For thy servant, became surety for the lad, while away from my father saying,—If I bring him not unto thee, then will I count myself a sinner against my father all the days.

text.NRSV: Now therefore, please let your servant remain as a slave to my lord in place of the boy; and let the boy go back with his brothers.
text.Rotherham: Now, therefore, I pray thee let thy servant remain instead of the lad, as servant to my lord,—but as for the lad, let him go up with his brethren.

text.NRSV: For how can I go back to my father if the boy is not with me? I fear to see the suffering that would come upon my father.”
text.Rotherham: For how can I go up unto my father, should the lad, not be with me? lest I look on the calamity, that shall find out my father,

text.NRSV: Then Joseph could no longer control himself before all those who stood by him, and he cried out, “Send everyone away from me.” So no one stayed with him when Joseph made himself known to his brothers.
text.Rotherham: And Joseph could not restrain himself before all who were stationed near him, so he cried out—Have forth every man from me! And there stood no man with him, when Joseph made himself known unto his brethren,

text.NRSV: And he wept so loudly that the Egyptians heard it, and the household of Pharaoh heard it.
text.Rotherham: Then gave he forth his voice in weeping,—and the Egyptians heard, and the house of Pharaoh heard.

text.NRSV: Joseph said to his brothers, “I am Joseph. Is my father still alive?” But his brothers could not answer him, so dismayed were they at his presence.
text.Rotherham: Then said Joseph unto his brethren: I, am Joseph, is my father yet alive? But his brethren could not answer him, for they were terrified because of him.

text.NRSV: Then Joseph said to his brothers, “Come closer to me.” And they came closer. He said, “I am your brother, Joseph, whom you sold into Egypt.
text.Rotherham: Then said Joseph unto his brethren—Draw near I pray you, unto me. And they drew near. And he said—I, am Joseph your brother, whom ye sold into Egypt.

text.NRSV: And now do not be distressed, or angry with yourselves, because you sold me here; for God sent me before you to preserve life.
text.Rotherham: But, now, do not grieve, neither let it be vexing in your eyes, that ye sold me hither,—for, to save life, did God send me before you.

text.NRSV: For the famine has been in the land these two years; and there are five more years in which there will be neither plowing nor harvest.
text.Rotherham: For these two years, hath the famine been in the midst of the land,—and, yet five years, are there, in which there shall be neither ploughing, nor harvest.

text.NRSV: God sent me before you to preserve for you a remnant on earth, and to keep alive for you many survivors.
text.Rotherham: So then God sent me before you, to plant for you a remainder in the earth,—and to save you alive, by a great deliverance,

text.NRSV: So it was not you who sent me here, but God; he has made me a father to Pharaoh, and lord of all his house and ruler over all the land of Egypt.
text.Rotherham: Now, therefore it was not ye, who sent me hither, but, God, himself,—who also appointed me to be a father to Pharaoh, and a lord to all his house, and a ruler over all the land of Egypt.

text.NRSV: Hurry and go up to my father and say to him, ‘Thus says your son Joseph, God has made me lord of all Egypt; come down to me, do not delay.
text.Rotherham: Haste ye and go up unto my father, then shall ye say unto him—Thus saith thy son Joseph, God hath appointed me lord to all Egypt,—come down unto me do not delay;

text.NRSV: You shall settle in the land of Goshen, and you shall be near me, you and your children and your children’s children, as well as your flocks, your herds, and all that you have.
text.Rotherham: so shalt thou dwell in the land of Goshen, and shalt be near unto me, thou, and thy sons, and thy sons’ sons,—and thy flocks and thy herds and all that is thine;

text.NRSV: I will provide for you there—since there are five more years of famine to come—so that you and your household, and all that you have, will not come to poverty.’
text.Rotherham: so will I sustain thee, there, for there are yet five years of famine,—lest thou come to poverty—thou and thy house and all who are thine.

text.NRSV: And now your eyes and the eyes of my brother Benjamin see that it is my own mouth that speaks to you.
text.Rotherham: Now lo! your own eyes do see, and the eyes of my brother Benjamin,—that it is my own mouth, that doth speak unto you.

text.NRSV: You must tell my father how greatly I am honored in Egypt, and all that you have seen. Hurry and bring my father down here.”
text.Rotherham: As soon as ye have told my father all my honour in Egypt, and all that ye have seen, so soon shall ye hasten and bring down my father hither.

text.NRSV: Then he fell upon his brother Benjamin’s neck and wept, while Benjamin wept upon his neck.
text.Rotherham: Then fell he upon the neck of Benjamin his brother, and wept,—and Benjamin wept on his neck.

text.NRSV: And he kissed all his brothers and wept upon them; and after that his brothers talked with him.
text.Rotherham: Then kissed he all his brethren, and wept upon them,—and, thereafter, his brethren spake with him.

text.NRSV: When the report was heard in Pharaoh’s house, “Joseph’s brothers have come,” Pharaoh and his servants were pleased.
text.Rotherham: Now, the report, was heard by the house of Pharaoh, saying, The brethren of Joseph have come in, And it was good in the eyes of Pharaoh, and in the eyes of all his servants.

text.NRSV: Pharaoh said to Joseph, “Say to your brothers, ‘Do this: load your animals and go back to the land of Canaan.
text.Rotherham: So then Pharaoh said unto Joseph—Say unto thy brethren This, do,—lade your asses, and go enter into the land of Canaan;

text.NRSV: Take your father and your households and come to me, so that I may give you the best of the land of Egypt, and you may enjoy the fat of the land.
text.Rotherham: and fetch your father and your households and come in unto me,—that I may give you the best of the land of Egypt, and eat ye the fat of the land.

text.NRSV: You are further charged to say, ‘Do this: take wagons from the land of Egypt for your little ones and for your wives, and bring your father, and come.
text.Rotherham: Thou, therefore command them, This, do ye,—Take you out of the land of Egypt, waggons for your little ones and for your wives, so shall ye bring your father and come in.

text.NRSV: Give no thought to your possessions, for the best of all the land of Egypt is yours.’”
text.Rotherham: Your eye, moreover, let it not look with pity upon your goods,—for, the best of all the land of Egypt, is, yours.

text.NRSV: The sons of Israel did so. Joseph gave them wagons according to the instruction of Pharaoh, and he gave them provisions for the journey.
text.Rotherham: And the sons of Israel did so, and Joseph gave them waggons, at the bidding of Pharaoh,—and gave them provisions for the way:

text.NRSV: To each of them he gave a set of garments; but to Benjamin he gave three hundred pieces of silver and five sets of garments.
text.Rotherham: to all of them, gave he to each man changes of raiment,—but, to Benjamin, he gave three hundred pieces of silver, and five changes of raiment.

text.NRSV: To his father he sent the following: ten donkeys loaded with the good things of Egypt, and ten female donkeys loaded with grain, bread, and provision for his father on the journey.
text.Rotherham: Moreover, to his father, sent he as followeth—ten he-asses, laden from the best of Egypt,—and ten she-asses laden with corn and bread and nourishing food for his father for the way.

text.NRSV: Then he sent his brothers on their way, and as they were leaving he said to them, “Do not quarrel along the way.”
text.Rotherham: Thus sent he forth his brethren and they departed,—and he said unto them, Do not fall out by the way.

text.NRSV: So they went up out of Egypt and came to their father Jacob in the land of Canaan.
text.Rotherham: And they went up out of Egypt,—and came into the land of Canaan, unto Jacob their father.

text.NRSV: And they told him, “Joseph is still alive! He is even ruler over all the land of Egypt.” He was stunned; he could not believe them.
text.Rotherham: And they told him saying—Joseph: is, yet alive, yea, indeed, he, is ruler in all the land of Egypt. And his heart became faint, for he believed them not.

text.NRSV: But when they told him all the words of Joseph that he had said to them, and when he saw the wagons that Joseph had sent to carry him, the spirit of their father Jacob revived.
text.Rotherham: So they spake unto him all the words of Joseph which he had spoken unto them h then saw he the waggons, which Joseph had sent to carry him,—and the spirit of Jacob their father revived.

text.NRSV: Israel said, “Enough! My son Joseph is still alive. I must go and see him before I die.”
text.Rotherham: And Israel said, Enough! Joseph my son is, yet alive, I must go and see him before I die!

text.NRSV: When Israel set out on his journey with all that he had and came to Beer-sheba, he offered sacrifices to the God of his father Isaac.
text.Rotherham: So Israel brake up, with all that he had, and came in to Beer-sheba,—and offered sacrifices to the God of his father Isaac.

text.NRSV: God spoke to Israel in visions of the night, and said, “Jacob, Jacob.” And he said, “Here I am.”
text.Rotherham: And God said to Israel in the visions of the night, and he said—Jacob! Jacob! And he said—Behold me!

text.NRSV: Then he said, “I am God, the God of your father; do not be afraid to go down to Egypt, for I will make of you a great nation there.
text.Rotherham: And he said: I, am GOD himself, the God of thy father,—Be not afraid of going down to Egypt, seeing that for a great nation, will I plant thee there.

text.NRSV: I myself will go down with you to Egypt, and I will also bring you up again; and Joseph’s own hand shall close your eyes.”
text.Rotherham: I will go down with thee to Egypt, and I will bring thee up, yea wholly up,—and, Joseph, shall lay his own hand upon thine eyes.

text.NRSV: Then Jacob set out from Beer-sheba; and the sons of Israel carried their father Jacob, their little ones, and their wives, in the wagons that Pharaoh had sent to carry him.
text.Rotherham: So Jacob rose up from Beer-sheba,—and the sons of Israel brought Jacob their father, and their little ones, and their wives, in the waggons which Pharaoh had sent to bring him;

text.NRSV: They also took their livestock and the goods that they had acquired in the land of Canaan, and they came into Egypt, Jacob and all his offspring with him,
text.Rotherham: and they took their cattle and the gains which they had gained in the land of Canaan, and came into Egypt,—Jacob and all his seed with him:

text.NRSV: his sons, and his sons’ sons with him, his daughters, and his sons’ daughters; all his offspring he brought with him into Egypt.
text.Rotherham: his sons, and his sons’ sons with him, his daughters, and his sons’ daughters, and all his seed, brought he with him into Egypt.

text.NRSV: Now these are the names of the offspring of Israel, who came into Egypt, Jacob and his sons. Reuben, Jacob’s firstborn,
text.Rotherham: Now, these, are the names of the sons of Israel who came into Egypt—Jacob and his sons,—the firstborn of Jacob, Reuben:

text.NRSV: and the children of Reuben: Hanoch, Pallu, Hezron, and Carmi.
text.Rotherham: And, the sons of Reuben,—Hanoch and Pallu, and Hezron and Carmi;

text.NRSV: The children of Simeon: Jemuel, Jamin, Ohad, Jachin, Zohar, and Shaul, the son of a Canaanite woman.
text.Rotherham: And, the sons of Simeon,—Jemuel and Jamin and Ohad and Jachin and Zohar,—and Shaul, son of the Canaanitess;

text.NRSV: The children of Levi: Gershon, Kohath, and Merari.
text.Rotherham: And the sons of Levi,—Gershon, Kohath and Merari;

text.NRSV: The children of Judah: Er, Onan, Shelah, Perez, and Zerah (but Er and Onan died in the land of Canaan); and the children of Perez were Hezron and Hamul.
text.Rotherham: And the sons of Judah,—Er and Onan and Shelah, and Perez and Zerah,—but Er and Onan died in the land of Canaan, and the sons of Perez were Hezron and Hamul;

text.NRSV: The children of Issachar: Tola, Puvah, Job, and Shimron.
text.Rotherham: And, the sons of Issachar,—Tola and Puvah, and Iob and Shimron;

text.NRSV: The children of Zebulun: Sered, Elon, and Jahleel.
text.Rotherham: And the sons of Zebulon,—Sered and Elon, and Jahleel.

text.NRSV: These are the sons of Leah, whom she bore to Jacob in Paddan-aram, together with his daughter Dinah; all his sons and his daughters numbered thirty-three.
text.Rotherham: These, are the sons of Leah whom she bare to Jacob in Padan-aram, with Dinah also his daughter,—All the souls of his sons and of his daughters, were thirty-three.

text.NRSV: The children of Gad: Ziphion, Haggi, Shuni, Ezbon, Eri, Arodi, and Areli.
text.Rotherham: And, the sons of Gad,—Zaphon and Haggi, Shuni and Ezbon,—Eri and Arodi and Areli;

text.NRSV: The children of Asher: Imnah, Ishvah, Ishvi, Beriah, and their sister Serah. The children of Beriah: Heber and Malchiel.
text.Rotherham: And, the sons of Asher,—Imnah and Ishvah and Ishvi and Beriah, and Serah their sister,—and the sons of Beriah, Heber and Malchiel.

text.NRSV: These are the children of Zilpah, whom Laban gave to his daughter Leah; and these she bore to Jacob—sixteen persons.
text.Rotherham: These, are the sons of Zilpah, whom Laban gave to Leah his daughter,—so she bare these to Jacob,—sixteen souls.

text.NRSV: The children of Jacob’s wife Rachel: Joseph and Benjamin.
text.Rotherham: The sons of Rachel, Jacob’s wife,—Joseph and Benjamin;

text.NRSV: And to Joseph in the land of Egypt were born Manasseh and Ephraim, whom Asenath daughter of Potiphera, priest of On, bore to him.
text.Rotherham: And there were born to Joseph in the land of Egypt, whom Asenath daughter of Poti-phera, priest of On, bare to him,: Manasseh and Ephraim;

text.NRSV: The children of Benjamin: Bela, Becher, Ashbel, Gera, Naaman, Ehi, Rosh, Muppim, Huppim, and Ard.
text.Rotherham: And the sons of Benjamin—Bela and Becher, and Ashbel, Gera and Naaman, Ehi and Rosh,—Muppim and Huppim, and Ard.

text.NRSV: These are the children of Rachel, who were born to Jacob—fourteen persons in all.
text.Rotherham: These, are the sons of Rachel, who were born to Jacob,—all the souls, were fourteen.

text.NRSV: The child of Dan: Hushim.
text.Rotherham: And, the sons of Dan,—Hushim;

text.NRSV: The children of Naphtali: Jahzeel, Guni, Jezer, and Shillem.
text.Rotherham: And the sons of Naphtali,—Jahzeel and Guni, and Jezer and Shillem.

text.NRSV: These are the children of Bilhah, whom Laban gave to his daughter Rachel, and these she bore to Jacob—seven persons in all.
text.Rotherham: These, are the sons of Bilhah, whom Laban gave to Rachel his daughter,—so she bare these to Jacob,—all the souls, were seven.

text.NRSV: All the persons belonging to Jacob who came into Egypt, who were his own offspring, not including the wives of his sons, were sixty-six persons in all.
text.Rotherham: All the souls that came in with Jacob to Egypt that had come forth of his loins, besides Jacob’s sons’ wives,—all the souls, were sixty-six;

text.NRSV: The children of Joseph, who were born to him in Egypt, were two; all the persons of the house of Jacob who came into Egypt were seventy.
text.Rotherham: and, the sons of Joseph who were born to him in Egypt, were two souls: All the souls of the house of Jacob that came into Egypt, were seventy.

text.NRSV: Israel sent Judah ahead to Joseph to lead the way before him into Goshen. When they came to the land of Goshen,
text.Rotherham: And he sent, Judah, before him unto Joseph, to direct his face to Goshen,—so they came in towards the land of Goshen.

text.NRSV: Joseph made ready his chariot and went up to meet his father Israel in Goshen. He presented himself to him, fell on his neck, and wept on his neck a good while.
text.Rotherham: Then yoked Joseph his chariot, and went up to meet Israel his father towards Goshen,—and he appeared unto him, and fell upon his neck, and wept upon his neck a good while.

text.NRSV: Israel said to Joseph, “I can die now, having seen for myself that you are still alive.”
